L. (L.) amazonensis induced considerably higher levels of TNFα, IL-1Ra and IL-10 than L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 1C).
No differences were observed for IL-10 mRNA expression and protein production for both Leishmania species in IL-32 knockdown cells (Fig 3D).
No differences were found between the two Leishmania species on IL-10 mRNA expression and proteins levels (Fig 4D).
150.Rationale: Although IL-10 is higher with L. (L.) amazonensis and is an anti-inflammatory cytokine that can limit pathology, the experimental manipulations in the paper show IL-10 is not altered by changing IL-32γ levels (silencing or overexpression). The authors emphasize IL-32γ as the central regulator that controls multiple downstream effectors affecting both parasite control and inflammation. Therefore, IL-10 alone is not supported by the experimental evidence as the single most influential host factor in this model.

150.Option: The induction of IL-1β by L. (V.) braziliensis most strongly influences the balance between parasite control and immunopathology by enhancing macrophage activation and pathogen clearance
Evidence: Remarkably, only L. (V.) braziliensis induced a major increase in IL-1β production.
L. (V.) braziliensis-induced IL-1β and IL-1Ra as well as IL-10 induced by both Leishmania species were not affected by the up or down regulation of IL-32γ.
That IL-32γ had no effect on IL-1β production was unexpected since silencing of IL-32 in THP-1 cells reduced TNFα, IL-8 and IL-1β after infection with MTB [6].
150.Rationale: While L. (V.) braziliensis uniquely induces IL-1β in this system, the passage shows IL-1β levels are not controlled by IL-32γ and the authors did not present data linking IL-1β as the primary determinant of the balance between control and pathology. The weight of the experimental findings points to IL-32γ as the upstream regulator that modulates multiple pathways (including microbicidal effectors and proinflammatory cytokines) and changes infection index, making IL-1β induction alone an incomplete explanation for the dominant host factor.

150.Option: The species-specific induction of TNFα, which is higher in L. (L.) amazonensis infection, most strongly influences the balance between parasite control and immunopathology by driving both microbicidal activity and tissue damage
Evidence: In this study, TNFα production was higher in macrophages infected with L. (L.) amazonenis than L. (V.) braziliensis.
IL-32γ upregulates the production of proinflammatory cytokines such as TNFα, IL-1β and IL-8 [3].
Cells depleted of IL-32 exhibited an increase in macrophage infection index after Leishmania species infection, which was reversed when IL-32γ was overexpressed.
Heinhuis et al. [53] demonstrated that in cells overexpressing intracellular IL-32γ there is an enhanced TNFα mRNA stability, which explains higher TNFα levels than in control cells.
150.Rationale: TNFα is clearly an important proinflammatory and microbicidal cytokine and is produced at higher levels with L. (L.) amazonensis. However, the experimental results indicate TNFα is downstream of IL-32γ (IL-32γ modulates TNFα transcription and mRNA stability) and manipulating IL-32γ produces larger, pleiotropic effects on microbicidal molecules and infection index than changing TNFα alone. The study frames IL-32γ as the central regulator; TNFα is one important effector but not identified as the single strongest factor determining the balance between control and immunopathology.

151.Option: Including an internal amplification control (IAC) in every sample to detect PCR inhibition and ensure assay validity
Evidence: Extraction was performed using 300 μl of GEB and 5 μl (40 ρg/μl) of an internal amplification control (IAC), were added to each sample at the beginning of the purification process to ensure the quality of the DNA purification process and the absence of PCR inhibitors.
A recombinant plasmid (pZErO-2) containing the aquaporin gene of Arabidopsis thaliana as a heterologous extrinsic internal amplification control (IAC) was used [12,20,23].

The qPCR for a sample was considered as valid when the Cq corresponding to the amplification of its IAC was lower than the 75th percentile plus 1.5 the interquartile range of the median of each extraction batch (CqIAC<75th percentile + 1.5*IQR).
151.Rationale: Including an IAC in every sample directly detects per-sample PCR inhibition or extraction failures and provides an objective validity check. The passage explicitly states an IAC was added to each sample "to ensure the quality of the DNA purification process and the absence of PCR inhibitors" and that qPCR results were considered valid only when the IAC amplification met a predefined threshold, which directly prevents false-negative results due to pre-analytical inhibition or failed extraction.

151.Option: Using a standardized DNA extraction protocol for both clot and whole blood samples to ensure consistent processing conditions
Evidence: Blood samples mixed with Guanidine/EDTA (GEB) were processed using the High Pure PCR Template Preparation kit (Roche Diagnostics GmbH, Mannheim, Germany) as previously described [12,20].
Clot samples were initially homogenized using the FastPrep machine followed by extraction using the High Pure PCR Template Preparation kit.

The technique is based on the initial disruption of clot using the fast prep machine followed by the internationally standardized DNA extraction protocol for the molecular diagnosis of Chagas disease for GEB samples.
151.Rationale: Applying a standardized extraction protocol to both sample types reduces pre-analytical variability introduced by differing laboratory procedures, which can otherwise produce inconsistent DNA yields and contribute to false negatives. The authors specifically used the same Roche High Pure kit for GEB and adapted the same standardized protocol for clot samples, supporting this approach as a valid method to minimize processing-related variability.

151.Option: Employing a high-efficiency homogenization method (FastPrep) to ensure complete disruption of the clot matrix
Evidence: Clot samples were initially homogenized using the FastPrep machine followed by extraction using the High Pure PCR Template Preparation kit.

Briefly, 300 μl of clot, 300 μl of Guanidine/EDTA (GE), 40 μl of Proteinase K (20 mg/ml) and 5 μl of IAC (40 ρg/μl) were place in a Lysing Matrix E tube (MP Biomedicals, Santa Ana, CA). The mixture was FastPrep processed at 5,5 m/s for 30 sec and then centrifuged at 14000 x g for 2 min at room temperature.

The technique is based on the initial disruption of clot using the fast prep machine followed by the internationally standardized DNA extraction protocol for the molecular diagnosis of Chagas disease for GEB samples.
DNA extracted from clot proved to have a higher sensitivity and presented a lower Cq value compared to DNA extracted from whole blood-GE (GEB) samples.
151.Rationale: Efficient homogenization of the clot enhances release of trapped parasite DNA and improves extraction efficiency; inadequate disruption could leave DNA inaccessible and cause false negatives. The study implemented FastPrep processing and linked clot-extracted DNA to higher sensitivity and lower Cq values, indicating this methodological choice directly reduces pre-analytical losses that would lead to false negatives.

151.Option: Avoiding clot washing prior to DNA extraction to prevent loss of circulating parasite DNA
Evidence: washing the clot with water or with erythrocyte lysing buffers as previously reported [14,33] most likely lyses the T. cruzi parasites releasing the genetic material, which will also be washed out along with hemoglobin.
Thus, washing the clot might lead to decrease the sensitivity of any technique targeting the T. cruzi DNA, such as the qPCR.
Our protocol avoided any washing steps of the clot previous to the DNA purification to ensure that most of the DNA from the parasites could be recovered from the sample.
151.Rationale: Washing the clot can remove parasite DNA and reduce assay sensitivity, causing false-negative results. The authors explicitly note that washing may decrease sensitivity and therefore avoided washing to maximize DNA recovery, a direct pre-analytical choice to reduce false negatives.

151.Option: Using a single, consistent sample collection time to minimize biological variability in parasite load
Evidence: The blood obtained without additives was allowed to sit for at least 30 min to let the clot to form (no more than 60 min) and then centrifuged at 1100 x g for 20 min [18].
Peripheral blood from individuals suspected of Chagas diseases was collected in tubes with EDTA and tubes without additives.

A total of 265 pairs of samples (one GEB and a clot sample) were obtained from archived specimens in our biorepository. The samples were obtained from two sample sets: Archived samples from women presenting for delivery at the Hospital Municipal Camiri in Camiri, Bolivia (n = 100); and samples collected at the Hospital Municipal San Juan de Dios, Santa Cruz, Bolivia (n = 165).
151.Rationale: While standardizing aspects of collection (e.g., clot formation timing) is described, the study does not present use of a single, consistent sampling time across participants to control biological variability in parasite load, nor does it promote this as a primary methodological safeguard against false negatives. The passage emphasizes other pre-analytical controls (IAC, standardized extraction, FastPrep, avoiding washing) that more directly address sample processing losses or inhibition. Therefore this option is not supported as the most direct method in the study to reduce false-negative results.

152.Option: Chilling duration and compaction height must be limited to preserve male mosquito survival and longevity while enabling efficient packing and transportation
Evidence: It is therefore essential to determine the tolerable and optimal range of chilling temperature, duration, and compaction for male mosquitoes.
Our results indicate that a temperature range of 5–10°C is optimal for the immobilization of male adult Ae. albopictus with minimal effect observed on the survival rate of post-chilled males.
a 3-hour chilling duration at 5-10°C did not influence the longevity and mating competitiveness of males, regardless whether they were exposed to irradiation or not at pupal stage.
male longevity significantly decreased if the chilling duration extended to 24 h.

When males were compacted at different heights during chilling, we found that the compaction height had a significant impact on the survival of IHC males ... reduced survival was found for IHC males when compacted at a height of 8 cm.
Our results indicate that 3 h of chilling at 5°C or 10°C with either horizontal stacking or vertical compaction height of less than 6 cm did not affect the survival of IHC males.
Immobilized male mosquitoes are required to be transported and packed under cold conditions.
space requirements for transportation is greatly reduced if males are immobilized and compacted.
152.Rationale: The passage repeatedly states that chilling temperature, duration and compaction (stacking height/density) affect survival, longevity and mating competitiveness, while immobilization and compaction are operationally important for packing and transport. Optimal operational conditions (5–10°C, short durations like 3 h, and vertical compaction <6 cm) preserve male quality; excessive duration or excessive compaction (e.g., 8 cm) reduces survival and competitiveness. Therefore balancing chilling duration and compaction height is the factor that must be carefully managed to maintain both operational feasibility (efficient packing/transport) and male quality.

152.Option: The temperature during transportation must be maintained between 1 and 5°C to prevent premature recovery and ensure high survival rates
Evidence: Reduced survival of males was observed when males were chilled at 1°C, regardless of the durations (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P < 0.0001).
After chilling for 24 h, the mean survival of IHC males was only 2.5% at 1°C as compared to about 89% at both 5 and 10°C (Table 1).
Our results indicate that a temperature range of 5–10°C is optimal for the immobilization of male adult Ae. albopictus with minimal effect observed on the survival rate of post-chilled males.
the temperature during packing and transportation fluctuated between 8 and 12°C.
we recommend the chilling of Ae. albopictus male mosquitoes at 10°C for less than 3 h due to its lack of negative effects on their survival, longevity and mating competitiveness.
152.Rationale: The passage shows that 1°C is harmful (very low survival) and that the optimal immobilization temperature is 5–10°C (with recommendation of 10°C for <3 h). Actual transportation temperatures observed were 8–12°C. Thus maintaining 1–5°C is inconsistent with the evidence: 1°C greatly reduces survival and 5–10°C is preferred. The option's specified range (1–5°C) is therefore incorrect.

152.Option: Vertical stacking at a height of 8 cm should be used to maximize packing density and reduce transportation costs
Evidence: When males were compacted at different heights during chilling, we found that the compaction height had a significant impact on the survival of IHC males ... reduced survival was found for IHC males when compacted at a height of 8 cm (Dunnett's multiple comparisons test, 5°C-3 h: P < 0.05; 5°C-24 h: P < 0.05; 10°C-3 h: P < 0.05).
Our results indicate that 3 h of chilling at 5°C or 10°C with either horizontal stacking or vertical compaction height of less than 6 cm did not affect the survival of IHC males.
a reduced survival rate was observed when males were vertically compacted a height of 8 cm.
152.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that vertical compaction at 8 cm reduced survival, while heights under 6 cm did not affect survival. Therefore recommending 8 cm to maximize density is contradicted by the data; it would harm male quality despite potential packing benefits.

152.Option: The use of sugar solution feeding before release should be avoided to prevent energy depletion and maintain mating competitiveness
Evidence: Two different feeding regimes were applied daily to the post-chilled IHC males: either water only or sugar solution (10%).

When post-chilled males were fed on water only, longevity of IHC males was reduced by a median of one-day when chilled for 24 h compared to the control ... The median adult longevity was 4 d for IHC0h, IHC5°C-3h and IHC10°C-3h males, and was reduced to 3 d for IHC5°C-24h and IHC10°C-24h males, respectively.

However, when post-chilled males were fed on 10% sugar solution, IHC5°C-24h males were found to live one-day less than other treatments of males ... The median longevity was 11 d for IHC5°C-24h males, and 12 d for the other treatment groups.

As glucose plays an important role in maintaining life, flight and mating of insects [53], we speculate that the decline in mating competitiveness may also be linked to the decrease in glucose reserves in post-chilled male mosquitoes as observed in this study.
we propose glucose measurement as an additional, simple and effective method to detect the qualities of sterile males after chilling.
152.Rationale: The passage indicates sugar feeding (10% solution) supports greater longevity compared with water-only feeding, and glucose reserves are important for flight and mating. There is no evidence that sugar feeding before release should be avoided; rather, maintaining energy reserves (glucose) is associated with better mating competitiveness. Thus the option is incorrect.

152.Option: The duration of chilling should be maximized to ensure complete immobilization and reduce the risk of male escape during release
Evidence: a 3-hour chilling duration at 5-10°C did not influence the longevity and mating competitiveness of males, regardless whether they were exposed to irradiation or not at pupal stage.
male longevity significantly decreased if the chilling duration extended to 24 h.
the competitive performance of the males in this study was reduced by approximately 50% when the chilling duration reached 24 h (Fig 3B & 3D).
we recommend the chilling of Ae. albopictus male mosquitoes at 10°C for less than 3 h due to its lack of negative effects on their survival, longevity and mating competitiveness.
152.Rationale: Contrary to maximizing chilling duration, the passage documents harms from prolonged chilling (24 h) including reduced longevity and ~50% reduction in mating competitiveness; authors recommend short durations (<3 h). Thus maximizing chilling duration is inconsistent with maintaining male quality and is incorrect.

153.Option: The infection status of the brown dog tick vector must be confirmed, as the absence of infected ticks would explain the low prevalence in dogs and prevent spillover to humans
Evidence: E. canis is transmitted by the brown dog tick Rhipicephalus sanguineus [3], [4].
This low prevalence rate is interesting, as one would expect Malaysia to be a highly endemic region for E. canis due to the suitable climate and the abundance of the tick vector.
Thus further studies are needed to detect E. canis DNA from ticks, as there would be no transmission to dogs if the ticks are not infected.
153.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies Rhipicephalus sanguineus as the vector and states that detecting E. canis in ticks is necessary because "there would be no transmission to dogs if the ticks are not infected." Vector infection status therefore critically influences interpretation of a low dog prevalence (it could reflect absence of infected ticks rather than low dog susceptibility) and is the primary determinant of spillover potential to humans and other hosts.

153.Option: The geographic distribution of the samples must be analyzed, as regional differences in tick abundance and climate could influence the prevalence and affect spillover risk to humans
Evidence: A total of 500 blood samples were collected from dogs in Peninsular Malaysia, comprising 177 samples from stray dogs at shelters around Selangor state (144) and Langkawi Island (33), and 323 samples from dogs that were presented to private veterinary clinics from Selangor (86) Johor (30), Melaka (27), Sabah (3), and the veterinary teaching hospital at Universiti Putra Malaysia (UPM), Selangor (177) for routine health care or specific treatment.
This low prevalence rate is interesting, as one would expect Malaysia to be a highly endemic region for E. canis due to the suitable climate and the abundance of the tick vector.
153.Rationale: The study sampled multiple geographic areas and the authors note expectations of high endemicity given climate and tick abundance. Regional variation in tick density, tick infection prevalence, and environmental conditions can change dog exposure risk and therefore influence observed dog prevalence and the local risk of spillover to humans. Thus geography is an important factor to consider when interpreting prevalence and assessing human risk.

153.Option: The use of PCR as a diagnostic method must be critically evaluated, as its sensitivity may be insufficient to detect low-level or chronic E. canis infections, which could explain the low prevalence rate
Evidence: Conversely, polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is a sensitive method of detection of acute monocytic ehrlichiosis in dogs; in fact it is designed to aim for the organism itself which makes PCR an invaluable technique capable of detecting traces of pathogen even before the onset of clinical signs [14].
Therefore, ideally, PCR using both blood and splenic aspirates should be considered to overcome this limitation [25].
Furthermore PCR sensitivity varies between laboratories and this fact may have contributed to the low number of positive dogs identified.
153.Rationale: The passage both endorses PCR's sensitivity for acute infections and warns about limitations: PCR on blood can miss subclinical or chronic infections and sensitivity varies by laboratory and specimen. These diagnostic limitations can lead to underestimation of true prevalence in dogs, which in turn affects assessment of spillover risk to humans; if infections are underdetected, human risk may be underestimated.

153.Option: The presence of clinical signs such as thrombocytopenia and anemia in dogs must be evaluated, as these symptoms are highly specific indicators of active E. canis infection and influence the interpretation of prevalence rates
Evidence: Analysis of laboratory findings of clinic dogs revealed few associations between hematological findings and E. canis infection status (Table 2).
Among the clinic cases 27 (8.3%) dogs had both thrombocytopenia and anemia, but only one of them was positive for E. canis.
Thus a reliable diagnosis of E. canis can only be made based on a combination of clinical signs, laboratory test results, serological tests, and molecular methods of detection.
over diagnosis or misdiagnosis may result if a diagnosis is made solely on clinical or hematological findings as they are not specific for CME.
153.Rationale: The option asserts that thrombocytopenia and anemia are "highly specific indicators," but the passage directly contradicts that: hematological signs are not specific for CME, many dogs with those findings were not PCR-positive, and diagnosis should not rely solely on clinical/hematological findings. Therefore this option is incorrect.

153.Option: The age and sex distribution of the dog population must be considered, as younger and male dogs are more susceptible to E. canis infection and could explain the low observed prevalence
Evidence: Information (age, breed, sex, and all laboratory results) of all the clinic cases was recorded, and the sex of the stray dogs was noted (Table 1).

Five hundred blood samples (323 clinic cases and 177 stray dogs) were evaluated using PCR in this study out of which ten were identified as E. canis positive, giving an overall prevalence rate of two (2.0%) percent.
153.Rationale: While age and sex were recorded, the passage provides no evidence that younger or male dogs are more susceptible in this dataset, nor that those factors explain the low prevalence. The authors do not report age/sex effects on prevalence. Therefore the specific claim that younger and male dogs explain the low observed prevalence is unsupported by the passage and is incorrect.

154.Option: The presence of more health extension workers in Amhara and Somali regions increases early detection and reporting, leading to the observed regional variation
Evidence: An opportunity lies in leveraging the existing health extension program, which is a key component of the primary health system workforce.
Eighty-five percent of the participant stated that there is no oral health program in the country’s health system.
154.Rationale: The passage suggests leveraging the health extension program as an opportunity but does not state that Amhara and Somali have more health extension workers or that such a difference explains the regional clustering of cases. In fact, the assessment highlights major gaps in oral health programming ("Eighty-five percent of the participant stated that there is no oral health program in the country’s health system."), indicating limited routine detection capacity overall. Because the text provides no evidence that greater numbers of health extension workers in Amhara or Somali drive higher reporting, this option is not supported.

154.Option: The higher population density in Amhara and Somali regions increases the likelihood of noma transmission and explains the regional clustering
Evidence: While the exact cause of noma remains unclear, it is believed to result from a combination of factors, including malnutrition, immunocompromis (e.g., bacterial or viral infections, immunosuppression from HIV, tuberculosis, leukemia, or non-Hodgkin lymphoma), concomitant diseases like measles or malaria, poor oral hygiene, certain social and environmental factors, damage to the gingival mucosa barrier, and bacterial infections [2–5].
The assessment demonstrated that noma cases are reported from all regions in Ethiopia, with regional variation in case distribution.
154.Rationale: The passage frames noma as resulting from malnutrition, immunosuppression and other individual/systemic vulnerabilities rather than from person-to-person transmission amplified by population density. There is no evidence in the text linking population density to noma transmission or to the observed regional clustering. Therefore, attributing the regional variation to higher population density is unsupported by the passage.

154.Option: The greater availability of specialized surgical care in Amhara and Somali regions leads to higher case reporting and thus explains the regional variation
Evidence: Most of the report (67 patients) were extracted from the NGO data base who are working on noma care.
The selection of hospitals was done based on convenience prioritizing institutions that provide plastic surgery, oral care, and dermatology services in each region.

The assessment have identified only three hospitals-two in Addis Ababa and one in Bahir Dar- that are managing noma patients and the hospitals in Addis Ababa provide service only during a medical mission.
154.Rationale: The passage documents that most cases were identified through NGOs and that only three hospitals (two in Addis Ababa and one in Bahir Dar, Amhara) manage noma patients, with Addis Ababa services often tied to missions. While availability of specialized services and NGO outreach clearly influence case identification and reporting, the passage does not claim greater specialized surgical capacity in Somali region, yet Somali is reported among high-case regions. The text explicitly links the regional focality to malnutrition prevalence (see evidence in option 3). Thus, greater availability of specialized surgical care may contribute to reporting bias but is not presented as the most direct explanation for the regional clustering.

154.Option: The higher prevalence of childhood malnutrition in Amhara and Somali regions most directly explains the regional clustering of noma cases
Evidence: The strong focality of noma cases in Amhara and Somali region also goes also with the highest prevalence of childhood malnutrition on those regions [21].

While the exact cause of noma remains unclear, it is believed to result from a combination of factors, including malnutrition, immunocompromis (e.g., bacterial or viral infections, immunosuppression from HIV, tuberculosis, leukemia, or non-Hodgkin lymphoma), concomitant diseases like measles or malaria, poor oral hygiene, certain social and environmental factors, damage to the gingival mucosa barrier, and bacterial infections [2–5].

The top five risks leading to deaths and disabilities in the country include malnutrition, WaSH (Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene), air pollution, dietary risks, and high fasting blood glucose levels [18].
154.Rationale: The passage explicitly links the regional focality of reported noma cases in Amhara and Somali to those regions' high prevalence of childhood malnutrition ("The strong focality... also goes also with the highest prevalence of childhood malnutrition on those regions [21]."). Malnutrition is repeatedly described as a primary risk factor and one of the top health risks in Ethiopia. Therefore, higher childhood malnutrition in these regions is the most direct and textually supported explanation for the observed regional clustering of noma cases.

154.Option: The higher incidence of measles and malaria in Amhara and Somali regions contributes to the regional clustering of noma cases
Evidence: While the exact cause of noma remains unclear, it is believed to result from a combination of factors, including malnutrition, immunocompromis (e.g., bacterial or viral infections, immunosuppression from HIV, tuberculosis, leukemia, or non-Hodgkin lymphoma), concomitant diseases like measles or malaria, poor oral hygiene, certain social and environmental factors, damage to the gingival mucosa barrier, and bacterial infections [2–5].

A study from Ethiopia on the risk factor has also concluded poverty-related diseases such as malaria, helminths infection, measle, diarrheal diseases, and unfavourable living conditions were identified to be the risk factor for noma.
154.Rationale: The passage identifies measles and malaria as known concomitant risk factors for noma, so they could plausibly contribute to regional susceptibility. However, the text specifically attributes the observed focality in Amhara and Somali to the high prevalence of childhood malnutrition (see option 3 evidence). While measles and malaria may play a role, they are not presented as the most direct explanation for the regional clustering in the passage; thus this option is less supported and judged incorrect.

155.Option: The antivenom's neutralization of coagulant activity is primarily driven by the presence of snake venom serine proteinases (SVSPs) in the venom, which are highly reactive with SAB
Evidence: SVSPs are generally thrombin-like enzymes that are involved in the coagulation disturbances observed in most patients [33].
Bands between 20 and 30 kDa, with masses corresponding to SVSPs and P-I SVMPs, were also recognized by SAB (Figure 6B).
P-III SVMPs are the predominant antigens in the venom of snakes from the Bothrops complex.
SAB preferentially recognized bands of approximately 50 kDa by western blotting (Figure 6B), confirming the higher immunogenicity of SVMPs class P-III.
P-III SVMPs are cross-reactive antigens that are equally recognized in venoms, regardless of their inclusion in the immunization pool.
155.Rationale: Although SVSPs are involved in coagulopathy and are recognized to some extent by SAB (quote 1 and 2), the passage emphasizes that P-III SVMPs—not SVSPs—are the predominant, most immunogenic antigens and the main targets of SAB (quotes 3–5). The strongest driver of cross-species neutralization described is recognition of conserved P-III SVMP epitopes rather than high reactivity to SVSPs, so the option attributing primary responsibility to SVSPs is not supported.

155.Option: The antivenom's effectiveness is determined by the total venom protein concentration, with higher concentrations leading to greater neutralization capacity across species
Evidence: The SAB antibody titers were the same, regardless of the antigen used, and they corresponded to a dilution of 640,000.

antivenom reacted similarly with toxins from the same protein family, as SVMPs, SVSPs or PLA2s, regardless of the snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production.
In all experiments, 3 LD50 doses of B. jararaca (105 µg) or B. atrox (225 µg) venom were incubated with SAB at ratios of 1, 2 and 4 times the potency reference value (1 mL/5 mg venom).
155.Rationale: The passage does not support a simple relationship between total venom protein concentration and cross-species neutralization. Instead it shows comparable antivenom titers across different venoms (quote 1) and emphasizes antigenic composition and family-level conserved epitopes as determinants of reactivity (quote 2). Neutralization assays were interpreted in terms of antigen composition and potency ratios, not total protein concentration (quote 3). Thus the statement is unsupported.

155.Option: Conserved epitopes on P-III SVMPs enable cross-reactivity and effective neutralization across diverse Bothrops venoms
Evidence: P-III SVMPs are the predominant antigens in the venom of snakes from the Bothrops complex.
The same fractions were the most SAB-reactive antigens in all venoms, regardless of whether these venoms were included in the immunization pool used to prepare the SAB antivenom.
P-III SVMPs are cross-reactive antigens that are equally recognized in venoms, regardless of their inclusion in the immunization pool.

In Figure 7, we demonstrate the strong reactivity of the monoclonal antibody with the fractions eluted after 160 minutes (in all chromatograms), confirming that these fractions correspond to P-III SVMPs.

Thus, it is very intriguing that P-I SVMPs are less recognized by the antivenoms than are P-III SVMPs ... the most immunogenic epitopes of SVMPs may be located within the disintegrin-like or cysteine-rich domains

SAB is efficient in neutralizing the most important effects of B. atrox venom despite the phylogenetic distance of the snake and the fact that the venom is not included in the immunization pool used to produce SAB.
155.Rationale: The passage repeatedly identifies class P-III SVMPs as the dominant, highly immunogenic antigens that are strongly and consistently recognized by SAB across different Bothrops venoms (quotes 1–4). It also suggests the immunodominant epitopes lie in domains unique to P-III SVMPs (quote 5), and links this cross-reactivity to actual neutralization of toxic activities even when the venom species was not in the immunization mix (quote 6). Together these statements directly support conserved P-III SVMP epitopes as the key factor enabling cross-species neutralization.

155.Option: The phylogenetic proximity of snake species to those used in antivenom production determines the strength of antivenom reactivity and neutralization efficacy
Evidence: The venom analysis showed that phylogenetic classification per se is not directly linked to venom composition.
Thus, apparently, venom composition was not related to the phylogenetic position of the snakes.

antivenom reacted similarly with toxins from the same protein family, as SVMPs, SVSPs or PLA2s, regardless of the snake phylogeny or the presence of the venom in the immunization pool used for antivenom production.

SAB is efficient in neutralizing the most important effects of B. atrox venom despite the phylogenetic distance of the snake and the fact that the venom is not included in the immunization pool used to produce SAB.
155.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that venom composition does not map neatly to phylogeny (quotes 1–2) and that SAB reactivity and neutralization occur regardless of a species' phylogenetic relationship to those used in immunization (quotes 3–4). Therefore phylogenetic proximity is not the primary determinant of antivenom efficacy, making this option incorrect.

155.Option: The abundance of phospholipase A2 (PLA2) in the venom correlates with the antivenom's ability to neutralize local myotoxic effects across species
Evidence: PLA2s are involved in local effects and the myotoxicity observed in bites with some species [34].
However, PLA2s are generally myotoxic or pro-inflammatory [34], and these symptoms are not well neutralized by antivenoms.
The limited reactivity of SAB with these fractions should be addressed.
In contrast, this does not appear to be the case for the neutralization of the local effects of envenomings induced by PLA2s or P-I SVMPs.
155.Rationale: While PLA2s cause local myotoxicity (quote 1), the passage indicates antivenoms (including SAB) show limited reactivity to PLA2 fractions and that local myotoxic symptoms are not well neutralized (quotes 2–4). Thus greater PLA2 abundance does not correlate with better neutralization by the antivenom; if anything, PLA2-rich venoms are less effectively neutralized locally, so this option is not supported.

156.Option: The chicken model demonstrates that ST313 is not host-adapted, as it causes severe disease in a non-human species with no evidence of immune tolerance.
Evidence: Here we clearly demonstrate that ST313 S. Typhimurium are not restricted to humans and that an invasive phenotype may occur in other host animals.
the pathology seen is mild compared to that of avian adapted serovars [27].
Although ST313 isolates show a reduced ability to thrive in the intestinal tract, the level of colonization is sufficient for chicken to act as a potential source of zoonotic infection.
156.Rationale: The passage does show ST313 can infect chickens ("not restricted to humans"), but it explicitly states the pathology in chickens is mild compared with avian-adapted serovars, contradicting the claim of "severe disease." The statement also over-interprets host-adaptation: demonstration of infection in a model does not prove lack of host adaptation. Thus the option is not supported by the text.

156.Option: The experimental findings confirm that chickens are a major reservoir for ST313 in sub-Saharan Africa, given the high prevalence of invasive disease in avian populations.
Evidence: Previous studies have failed to determine an animal or environmental reservoir for iNTS in Africa [6], [10].
these studies have been limited in size and scope, so the absence of a known wild or domestic animal host is perhaps a consequence of these limitations.
ST313 can colonize the intestine of the chicken, albeit not as successfully as other common S. Typhimurium pathovars, but sufficiently well to have the potential to be a reservoir of infection.
156.Rationale: The authors explicitly note that prior studies have not identified an animal reservoir and that field investigations have been limited, so the experimental evidence only shows potential for chickens to act as a reservoir rather than confirming they are a major reservoir in Africa. There is no evidence presented of high prevalence of invasive disease in avian populations in the field.

156.Option: Chickens are a primary source of ST313 transmission to humans, given their high colonization rates and frequent shedding of the pathogen in feces.
Evidence: Although cecal colonization of the gastrointestinal tract by ST313 is significantly lower than the ST19 isolates examined, ST313 clearly retains the ability to colonize the ceca.

This type of survey is complicated because the Salmonella carriage rate in healthy wild birds in the UK is around 0.2%, meaning detection of Salmonella in wild hosts may require large numbers of animals to be sampled if carriage is at a similar low rate [11].
only a limited number of live chickens and poultry meat have been sampled for iNTS in Africa [6], [10].
156.Rationale: The passage reports that ST313 colonizes chickens at lower levels than ST19 (so not "high colonization rates") and notes limited field sampling and low carriage rates in analogous bird populations, so there is insufficient evidence to assert chickens are a primary source via frequent shedding.

156.Option: Chickens are likely the original zoonotic source of ST313, as the pathogen's genomic degradation and invasive phenotype are consistent with long-term co-evolution in avian hosts.
Evidence: genomic degradation including pseudogene formation and chromosomal deletion that is a feature of host-adapted serovars such as S. Typhi and S. Gallinarum, that cause systemic typhoidal disease in their hosts [4], [5].

genetic analysis indicates it has probably evolved in the last 30 years and is split into two distinct, but closely related phylogenies, that have evolved sequentially, likely driven by the use of antimicrobials and the emergence of HIV [3].

It is likely that ST313 has an original zoonotic source, but possible that once it entered a susceptible population then person-to-person transmission became more important. Alternatively, it is possible that birds are the natural host of ST313 and that direct transmission from infected feces or via contaminated food may remain an important source of infection.
156.Rationale: While genomic degradation is noted, the authors do not conclude long-term co-evolution with birds; they state ST313 likely evolved recently ("probably evolved in the last 30 years") and consider multiple possibilities (including a zoonotic origin or subsequent human-dominated transmission). The evidence does not specifically support the strong claim that chickens are the original zoonotic source because the passage presents uncertainty and alternative explanations.

156.Option: Chickens may serve as a cryptic reservoir for ST313 despite limited detection in field studies, due to their ability to support colonization and systemic infection.
Evidence: Here we clearly demonstrate that ST313 S. Typhimurium are not restricted to humans and that an invasive phenotype may occur in other host animals.

The data presented here indicate that the S. Typhimurium genotype ST313 is capable of causing invasive infection in the chicken, accompanied by intestinal inflammation and colonization of the chicken gastrointestinal tract.
Although cecal colonization of the gastrointestinal tract by ST313 is significantly lower than the ST19 isolates examined, ST313 clearly retains the ability to colonize the ceca.

Previous studies have failed to determine an animal or environmental reservoir for iNTS in Africa [6], [10]. However, these studies have been limited in size and scope, so the absence of a known wild or domestic animal host is perhaps a consequence of these limitations.
ST313 can colonize the intestine of the chicken, albeit not as successfully as other common S. Typhimurium pathovars, but sufficiently well to have the potential to be a reservoir of infection.
156.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence that ST313 can both colonize and cause invasive infection in chickens, and explicitly states that limited surveillance could explain the failure to detect animal reservoirs in field studies. Together these points support the inference that chickens could act as a cryptic (underdetected) reservoir for ST313, even though definitive field confirmation is lacking.

157.Option: Recurrent disease presentation with bilateral involvement
Evidence: In this study, four (50%) were de novo cases, three (37.5%) were OT reactivations, and one (12.5%) was inactive by the time the patient presented to our hospital.
The presence of a classical retinochoroidal lesion does not exclude the possibility of having PORT in the same and/or the contralateral eye, as seen in patients 5 and 8.
bilateral involvement is less common.
The most common and most feared complication is retinochoroidal scarring in the macular area, which can reduce a patient’s BCVA to the point of legal blindness, as in 42.8% of our cases.
157.Rationale: Although PORT can be a de novo episode or a reactivation and bilateral involvement can occur, the passage does not identify recurrence or bilateral disease as the feature that most strongly predicts permanent visual loss. The authors explicitly single out macular retinochoroidal scarring as the complication most associated with progression to legal blindness. Therefore recurrent/bilateral presentation is not the strongest predictor of permanent, uncorrectable visual loss in this cohort.

157.Option: Macular location of the lesion with associated retinochoroidal scarring
Evidence: PORT lesions may be small, yet require optimal management because of their macular location[4] and their significant impact on visual prognosis and patient quality of life.
Eight out of eight patients presented PORT in macular region.
The most common and most feared complication is retinochoroidal scarring in the macular area, which can reduce a patient’s BCVA to the point of legal blindness, as in 42.8% of our cases.
retinal scarring is highly probable, and final BCVA may not optimal, especially in eyes with foveal involvement.
157.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies macular retinochoroidal scarring as the complication most likely to reduce BCVA to legal blindness. Multiple statements link macular location and scarring to poor visual prognosis, and the cohort data (all PORT lesions in the macula; high rates of legal blindness) support that macular lesions with resulting retinochoroidal scarring are the strongest indicator of permanent visual loss uncorrectable by optical or surgical means.

157.Option: Presence of vitreous inflammation with elevated intraocular pressure
Evidence: PORT is characterized by multifocal grey-white lesions at the outer retinal layers and retinal pigment epithelium associated with minimal vitreous inflammation.

Severe inflammation in classical OT derives in ocular complications and poor visual outcomes, including raised intraocular pressure, cataract, retinal tear, rhegmatogenous and/or tractional retinal detachment, vascular occlusion, epiretinal membrane, macular edema, choroidal neovascularization, vitreous haemorrhage, optic atrophy.
157.Rationale: Raised intraocular pressure and marked vitritis are listed as complications of severe (classical) OT and can contribute to poor outcomes, but PORT is specifically described as having minimal vitreous inflammation. The passage emphasizes macular scarring/foveal involvement as the primary driver of permanent central vision loss. Thus vitreous inflammation with elevated IOP is not presented as the most strongly predictive feature of uncorrectable permanent central visual loss in PORT.

157.Option: Foveal involvement with persistent intraretinal cysts on OCT
Evidence: retinal scarring is highly probable, and final BCVA may not optimal, especially in eyes with foveal involvement.
Complications encountered include choroidal neovascularization, serous retinal detachment, and macular cysts[7] that may convert to what is known as huge outer retinal cystoid space (HORC).
Four patients presented PORT in the fovea (patients 1, 2, 3 and 4)...

Case 1 ... SD-OCT showed a 494 µm macular hole, epiretinal membrane (ERM), and outer layer retinal hyperreflectivity, corresponding to a PORT lesion.... Fourteen months later, BCVA was 20/200 in the OS. ... Clinically, a retinochoroidal atrophic scar was demonstrated, and no signs of disease activity were observed.

Case 4 ... SD-OCT exhibited intraretinal cysts, outer retinal layer disorganization, focal disruption of the ellipsoid, interdigitation zone (IZ), and a hyperreflective subfoveal image corresponding to a choroidal neovascular membrane. ... Post-injection BCVA in the OD was 20/800
157.Rationale: The text highlights that foveal involvement is particularly associated with poorer final BCVA and that macular complications (including intraretinal/macular cysts and HORC) can contribute to irreversible structural damage. Case examples show foveal lesions with intraretinal cysts or macular holes leading to very poor BCVA and scarring. Therefore foveal involvement with persistent intraretinal cystic change on OCT is a valid imaging finding that strongly suggests higher risk of permanent central visual loss.

157.Option: Positive Toxoplasma IgG with negative IgM and high avidity
Evidence: All patients presented increased levels of Toxoplasma Ig G with a median of 274.1 IU/dL (IQR 75.25 – 1293). Seven out of eight patients (87.5%) had negative IgM but significantly elevated IgG, hence supporting OT diagnosis.
Avidity test in used to differentiate recently contracted infections from those that manifested in the past.
retinal scarring is highly probable, and final BCVA may not optimal, especially in eyes with foveal involvement.
157.Rationale: Positive IgG with negative IgM and high avidity are serologic markers that support prior exposure or chronic infection and help with diagnosis/timing, but the passage does not present these serologic findings as predictors of permanent visual loss. The authors emphasize lesion location (macula/fovea) and structural complications (retinochoroidal scarring, cysts, CNV) on imaging as the primary determinants of irreversible visual outcome, not serology.

158.Option: The presence of spermidine increases autophagy and metacyclogenesis, indicating that polyamine metabolism alone is sufficient to drive adaptation to the insect vector environment
Evidence: Our results showed that the presence of either Spd or Spm significantly increased the detection of Atg8.1 (Fig 5A). Quantitative data showed a significant increase of parasites with autophagic vesicles after PA treatment, as compared to control parasites (≈ 80% vs. ≈ 20%).
Polyamines (PA) ... exerting a potent induction of T. cruzi autophagy under control conditions.
Polyamines ... activated differentiation processes like metacyclogenesis. In this work, we demonstrate that this action is mediated by the induction of parasite autophagy.

Metacyclogenesis of T. cruzi takes place in the insect's rectum due to many factors such as nutrient scarcity produced by the fast replication of epimastigotes, specific components of intestinal wall and lumen of the vector, etc.
nutrient deprivation, the classical inducer of autophagy, is the common most frequent stimulus required to trigger epimastigote differentiation.
158.Rationale: The passage clearly shows that spermidine (Spd) can induce autophagy and that polyamines can activate metacyclogenesis (quotes 1–3). However, the option asserts that polyamine metabolism alone is sufficient to drive adaptation to the insect gut. The passage also emphasizes that metacyclogenesis in the vector is driven by multiple factors (e.g., nutrient scarcity, components of the intestinal wall) and that nutrient deprivation is a common inducer of differentiation (quotes 4–5). Thus while Spd promotes autophagy and differentiation, the text does not support the stronger claim that polyamine metabolism alone is sufficient to drive adaptation; other environmental triggers are implicated. Therefore the option overstates what the data demonstrate.

158.Option: The colocalization of DQ-BSA and Atg8.1 vesicles in starved parasites indicates that autophagy is involved in nutrient recycling, supporting the hypothesis that autophagy enables adaptation through metabolic reprogramming
Evidence: We observed that starved parasites displayed a higher frequency of colocalization of DQ-BSA with TcAtg8.1, as compared to controls (Mander´s overlap coefficient 0.87 +/- 0.03 vs. 0.42 +/- 0.05), indicating fusion of autophagosomes with lysosomes to form autolysosomes (Fig 2E).
This compound emitted red fluorescence after BSA hydrolysis into small peptides in lysosomes, thus identifying degradative compartments.
It contributes to the turnover of cellular components by delivering portions of the cytoplasm and organelles to lysosomes, where they are digested [1].
158.Rationale: The quoted sentences show direct experimental evidence that autophagosomes (Atg8.1-positive structures) fuse with degradative lysosomal compartments (DQ-BSA signal) in starved parasites (first quote). The DQ-BSA description confirms that the signal reflects hydrolytic digestion into peptides (second quote), and the general definition of autophagy states that it delivers cytoplasmic material to lysosomes for digestion (third quote). Together these observations support that autophagy in T. cruzi mediates degradation/recycling of cellular material under starvation, a metabolic reprogramming mechanism that would enable adaptation to nutrient stress in the insect gut. Therefore this observation directly supports the hypothesis.

158.Option: Treatment with rapamycin increases autophagy and metacyclogenesis, suggesting that mTOR inhibition is the primary mechanism enabling adaptation to the insect vector environment
Evidence: the treatment of parasites with 50 ng/μl Rap under control conditions (Diamond media at 28°C) for 2 h, induced a significant increase in the percentage of parasites with more than two Atg8.1 positive vesicles, as compared to non-treated parasites.
Conversely, Rap was able to increase the generation of MT when added to control medium at the first period of differentiation (8.3 +/- 0.2% vs. 3.9 +/- 0,6%).

mTOR is an evolutionary conserved kinase that senses the nutrient and energy status of cells by forming two distinct complexes. One of them, mTORC1 enhances glycolysis and biosynthetic processes and inhibits autophagy [6].

Several factors are required to activate this process. Attachment of the parasite to the luminal surface of the insect’s rectum, hemolymph components, cAMP action and even the redox status of the parasite may promote metacyclogenesis [33–37].
158.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that rapamycin, an mTOR inhibitor, induces autophagy and increases metacyclogenesis (quotes 1–2), so rapamycin's effect supports involvement of the mTOR pathway. However, the option claims mTOR inhibition is the primary mechanism enabling adaptation. The passage notes multiple factors contribute to metacyclogenesis (quote 4) and describes other kinases (Vps34) and pathways that regulate autophagy (quote 3). The data show rapamycin can induce autophagy and differentiation but do not establish that mTOR inhibition is the primary or sole mechanism in the natural insect environment. Therefore the stronger causal claim in the option is not supported.

158.Option: Inhibition of autophagy during metacyclogenesis reduces the production of infective metacyclic trypomastigotes, indicating that autophagy is essential for parasite adaptation to nutrient scarcity in the insect vector
Evidence: treatment with 100 nM Wort of parasites exposed to differentiation conditions impaired the autophagic response (Fig 3A).
Furthermore the addition of Wort to TAU during the first (Wort-F) or the total (Wort-T) period significantly reduced the number of MT in the parasite mixture, as compared to TAU-T condition.
the presence of Wort at either the first (Wort-F) or the whole (Wort-T) period of differentiation in TAU significantly reduced the infection rate.
Taken together, these results demonstrated that autophagy has a key role during T. cruzi differentiation.
158.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental evidence that pharmacologic inhibition of autophagy with wortmannin both impairs autophagic markers (first quote) and reduces the yield of infective metacyclic trypomastigotes and subsequent infection of host cells (second and third quotes). The authors conclude that autophagy has a key role in differentiation (fourth quote). These observations most strongly support the hypothesis that autophagy is essential for parasite adaptation to the nutrient-scarce insect gut during metacyclogenesis, because blocking autophagy reduces formation of the infective stage.

158.Option: Autophagy is induced in T. cruzi epimastigotes under nutrient-rich conditions, suggesting that autophagy is a constitutive process independent of environmental stress
Evidence: We observed that autophagy is induced during T. cruzi metacyclogenesis

After the first period of metacyclogenesis parasites were stained with the Trypan blue vital dye ... In contrast to the low percentage of survival displayed by UV irradiated parasites, survival of starved parasites was high and similar to the control (S1B Fig).
The percentage of parasites with more than two Atg8.1 positive vesicles was significantly higher in TAU (80 +/- 1.65%) than in control conditions (32.45 +/- 4.35%) (Fig 1B).

epimastigotes maintained under nutrient-rich conditions displayed lower frequency of MDC or DQ-BSA labeling. In contrast, epimastigotes subjected to nutritional and thermic stress in TAU differentiation medium shown a significant increase in the number of lysosomes, as compared to controls (Fig 2C and 2D).
158.Rationale: The option asserts autophagy is induced in nutrient-rich conditions and is constitutive. The passage shows the opposite: autophagy is induced during metacyclogenesis under starvation/thermic stress (first quote), autophagosome counts are much higher in TAU (starvation) than control (third quote), and markers of acidic/hydrolytic compartments are lower in nutrient-rich conditions (fourth quote). While some basal autophagy exists in controls (32%), the experimental data emphasize induction under stress, not constitutive independence from environment. Thus the option is not supported by the passage.

159.Option: The DNA/Live prime-boost regimen induced a strong initial IFN-γ response that declined by 8 weeks post-challenge, indicating the need for repeated antigen exposure to maintain immunity
Evidence: The production of IFN-γ in response to rA2-rCPA-rCPB antigens reached the highest level (524±6.4 pg/ml) at 4 weeks after challenge in G2 (Live/Live). This was significantly (p<0.05) different from G1 (DNA/Live) (404±9.9 pg/ml).
IFN-γ levels were maintained high even 8 weeks post-challenge in both G1 (404.5±101 pg/ml) and G2 (331±27 pg/ml) (Fig. 3A).
IFN-γ levels remained high (although slightly less than before challenge) after 4 and 8 weeks post-challenge in G1 but to a lesser extent in G2 (Fig. 3A).
159.Rationale: The option asserts that the DNA/Live (G1) regimen showed an initial IFN-γ response that declined by 8 weeks, implying waning and need for repeated exposure. The passage explicitly states that IFN-γ levels were "maintained high even 8 weeks post-challenge in both G1... and G2", and even notes G1 maintained high levels. Therefore the claim of a decline by 8 weeks for DNA/Live is not supported by the text.

159.Option: The Live/Live prime-boost regimen, which provides repeated exposure to live antigen, resulted in sustained high levels of nitric oxide production at 8 weeks post-challenge
Evidence: The highest amount of nitric oxide, which is essential for killing parasites inside infected macrophages, was observed with the Live/Live modality vaccinated mice (G2) upon stimulation with rA2-rCPA-rCPB, F/T L. tarA2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP and F/T L. infantum antigens at 8 weeks after challenge (Fig. 3E).

In our experimental system, immunization of BALB/c mice with recombinant L. tarentolae A2-CPA-CPB-CTE revealed the induction and secretion of IFN-γ and IL-10, although IFN-γ was much higher than IL-10. Determining the main source of IL-10 production has some beneficial results for understanding mechanisms of resistance and susceptibility to VL. Here, in immunized G1 and G2 groups, we detected high IL-10 production, especially at 8 weeks after challenge, upon stimulation with L. infantum lysate as a recall antigen. ... when administered as a Live/Live modality, induces a strong humoral and cellular immune response in addition to NO generation in response to rA2-rCPA-rCPB, F/T L. tarentolae A2-CPA-CPB-CTE and F/T L. infantum specific stimulators at 8 weeks after infectious challenge with L. infantum.
159.Rationale: The Live/Live regimen (G2) represents repeated exposure to live antigen. The passage directly reports that the highest nitric oxide levels at 8 weeks were observed in the Live/Live group, supporting that repeated/prolonged antigen exposure via Live/Live produced sustained effector function (NO) at that timepoint, consistent with the stated hypothesis.

159.Option: The Live/Live regimen induced a higher IFN-γ/IL-10 ratio than the DNA/Live regimen, indicating that repeated antigen exposure enhances balanced Th1/Regulatory responses
Evidence: Indeed, the Leishmania-specific IFN-γ/IL-10 ratios upon stimulation with rA2-rCPA-rCPB and L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP recall antigens were higher in G1 and G2 compared to the control G3, G4 and G5 mice both at 4 and 8 weeks after challenge.

The production of IFN-γ in response to rA2-rCPA-rCPB antigens reached the highest level (524±6.4 pg/ml) at 4 weeks after challenge in G2 (Live/Live). This was significantly (p<0.05) different from G1 (DNA/Live) (404±9.9 pg/ml).
The production of IL-10 upon antigen stimulation at 4-week and especially at 8 weeks after challenge was lower in G1 and G2 vaccinated groups in comparison to the control groups (G3 to G5).
159.Rationale: While the passage reports that both vaccinated groups (G1 and G2) had higher IFN-γ/IL-10 ratios than controls, it does not state that the Live/Live regimen produced a higher ratio than DNA/Live. The text gives explicit IFN-γ values showing G2 had higher IFN-γ at 4 weeks, but it does not provide a direct comparison of IFN-γ/IL-10 ratios between G2 and G1. Thus the specific claim that Live/Live induced a higher ratio than DNA/Live is not directly supported.

159.Option: The Live/Live regimen induced higher IgG2a titers than the DNA/Live regimen, suggesting that repeated antigen exposure enhances Th1 memory formation
Evidence: Interestingly, in G2 vaccinated mice with Live L.tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP/Live L.tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP, a higher amount of F/T L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP specific IgG1 and IgG2a was detected in comparison to G1 and G4 vaccinated with DNA/Live modality (Fig. 4B).
As shown in Figures 4A and 4C, both rA2-rCPA-rCPB and F/T L. infantum specific IgG1 and IgG2a were higher in vaccinated groups G1 and G2 compared to the control groups.
159.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that G2 (Live/Live) showed a higher amount of F/T L. tar-specific IgG2a than G1 (DNA/Live). In mice IgG2a is associated with Th1-type responses, so the observed higher IgG2a in the repeated-exposure Live/Live group supports the idea that repeated/prolonged antigen exposure enhances Th1-associated humoral memory, consistent with the hypothesis.

159.Option: The Live/Live regimen resulted in complete parasite clearance in the spleen by 4 weeks post-challenge, demonstrating that sustained antigen exposure leads to early sterilizing immunity
Evidence: We could not find parasites in the spleen in vaccinated groups (G1 and G2) but they could be seen in nearly all non-vaccinated groups (G3, G4 and G5, Fig. 5B).
no parasites were seen in the liver parenchyma and spleen at 4th week post-challenge in the vaccinated groups G1 and G2, whereas they were easily detected in the non-vaccinated control groups.

immunization using a DNA A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE (G1) and L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE (G2) prime-boosting regiments drastically (p<0.01) reduced the infection levels in both the liver (Fig. 2A and 2B) and the spleen (Fig. 2D and 2E) at 4 weeks after L. infantum challenge in contrast to the control groups (G3 to G5, n = 4 at each point/organ).
159.Rationale: Although vaccinated mice lacked detectable spleen parasites at 4 weeks, this outcome applied to both vaccinated regimens (DNA/Live G1 and Live/Live G2), not uniquely to Live/Live. The option claims Live/Live specifically produced complete splenic clearance by 4 weeks and frames this as evidence for early sterilizing immunity due to sustained exposure; the passage does not support the exclusivity to Live/Live and does not fully claim sterilizing immunity attributable solely to repeated exposure. Therefore the option overstates and is not supported as written.

160.Option: The presence of multiple sand fly species, as increased vector diversity increases the chance of parasite transmission to humans
Evidence: Sand flies of the subgenus Larroussius, predominantly Phlebotomus perniciosus, are the main vectors of ZVL in Tunisia [9,11,12].
Other sand fly species of the subgenus Larroussius, mainly Phlebotomus perfiliewi, also play an important role in the transmission of ZVL throughout the Mediterranean basin including Tunisia [12–14].
160.Rationale: The passage identifies particular sand fly species (P. perniciosus, and to a lesser extent P. perfiliewi) as the primary vectors, but it does not state that greater vector species diversity per se is the key driver of emergence. The emphasis is on the presence and overlap of competent vector species with the reservoir host rather than on sheer species richness. Thus the statement that multiple sand fly species (vector diversity) increases the chance of transmission is not supported as the most important factor by the passage. The quoted lines show specific competent vectors are central, not that diversity alone drives emergence.

160.Option: The density of sand fly populations in proximity to domestic dog habitats, as higher vector abundance increases the probability of transmission initiation
Evidence: From an eco-epidemiological view, the parasite requires an overlap between the vector (P. perniciosus) and the reservoir host (dogs), which is a prerequisite for the emergence of a ZVL focus.
This study was performed in an endemic area for ZVL where P. perniciosus is the most abundant sand fly species followed by P. perfiliewi [30].
A total of 1,939 sand flies were collected during 27 trapping-nights. Phlebotomus perniciosus was the most abundant sand fly species (n = 1228, 63.3%) followed by P. perfiliewi (n = 662, 34.1%).
160.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that an overlap between vector and reservoir is a prerequisite for emergence of a ZVL focus; that implies that local abundance/density of the vector near dog habitats strongly influences transmission initiation. The cited quotes indicate that vector presence and abundance (e.g., P. perniciosus being most abundant and numbers collected in traps) are epidemiologically important, supporting the view that higher sand fly density near dogs increases the likelihood of transmission emerging.

160.Option: The proportion of dogs in the population that are symptomatic and clinically infected with L. infantum, as these individuals are the most infectious to sand flies
Evidence: In previous studies in Tunisia we showed that the prevalence of ZVL in dogs is an important parameter for determining transmission to humans [4,15].
Several studies have reported that symptomatic dogs, infected with L. infantum are highly infectious to their sand fly vectors compared to oligosymptomatic and asymptomatic dogs [51–58].
In Tunisia, the zymodeme MON-1 is responsible for the majority of human and canine cases [8,27].
160.Rationale: The passage directly links dog infection prevalence to transmission risk and states that symptomatic dogs are particularly infectious to sand flies. Therefore the proportion of dogs that are clinically infected and symptomatic is a strong determinant of the chance that sand flies will become infected and subsequently transmit to humans, supporting this option as a correct influential factor.

160.Option: The presence of infected dogs that are more attractive to sand flies due to altered odour, creating a feedback loop that amplifies transmission
Evidence: We hypothesized that physiological changes in dogs parasitized by L. infantum change the dog’s odour making them more attractive to P. perniciosus and therefore enhancing the parasite’ s transmission success.
Our results showed that dogs infected with L. infantum were highly attractive to both female and male P. perniciosus.
This odour manipulation ensures successful transmission of the parasite to the vector.

The infectiousness the infected and uninfected dogs to sand flies was confirmed by xenodiagnosis ... a minimum of 36% to 100% of the lab-colonised P. perniciosus that were fed on infected dogs developed L. infantum infections ... whereas none of the sand flies fed on uninfected dogs were found to be infected with parasites.
160.Rationale: The passage provides direct experimental and mechanistic evidence that infected dogs emit altered cues (likely odour) that make them markedly more attractive to sand flies, and that sand flies feeding on these infected dogs become infected at high rates. This creates a positive feedback loop (infected dogs attract more vectors, which become infected and sustain transmission). Given this strong, specific evidence, the presence of such attractive infected dogs is the most powerful factor indicated by the passage for amplifying and enabling emergence of zoonotic Leishmania transmission.

160.Option: The availability of alternative hosts such as rodents or livestock, as these can dilute vector feeding and reduce transmission to dogs
Evidence: The general zoophilic feeding behaviour of P. perniciosus and L. longipalpis based on host availability rather than on attractiveness to specific hosts should lead to a dilution of the parasite as it is spread to non-competent hosts, and this would subsequently exert a zooprophylactic effect on the transmission of L. infantum [49].
Our work illustrates a potential zoopotentiation effect exerted by infected reservoir hosts that are highly attractive to sand fly vectors of L. infantum.
160.Rationale: The passage acknowledges that alternative (non-competent) hosts can dilute parasite transmission (zooprophylaxis), thereby reducing transmission to dogs. However, the question asks which factor would most strongly influence the likelihood of emergence; while availability of alternative hosts can influence (by reducing) emergence, the passage stresses mechanisms that promote emergence (infected, attractive dogs; vector-dog overlap). Therefore although alternative hosts are an influencing factor, the passage indicates they would act to reduce emergence risk rather than being the primary driver of emergence—so as the most influential factor driving emergence, this option is not supported.

161.Option: The size of the administrative unit selected for surveying directly determines the number of individuals sampled and thus influences both cost and accuracy of intervention classification
Evidence: The district, or an area of equivalent size, is the recommended implementation unit (IU) for LF elimination [23]. An area this size, however, may be too large for co-administration of a standardized drug package, because schistosomiasis and onchocerciasis are unlikely to be endemic throughout.
The MoH-GoSS therefore decided to consider both the payam and the county as IUs of the Integrated NTD Control Programme.
For LF, up to three communities and 250 individuals per payam were sampled.

If the standard operating procedures of the survey were thus modified, quantities of ICT tests, safety lancets and other supplies required to safely take blood samples would be reduced by approximately 80%, incurring substantial savings on survey consumables, particularly ICTs that cost >USD 4.0 per test. Furthermore, existing vehicles and staff could be deployed in three teams, which would decrease the survey duration by about one day per payam and reduced the majority of running costs accordingly. The cost per IU classified could thus be reduced by about 25% without any apparent risk of missing LF foci.
161.Rationale: The passage explicitly discusses choice of implementation unit (payam vs county) and shows that sampling intensity ("up to three communities and 250 individuals per payam") depends on that unit. It also demonstrates that changing the IU (payam→county) changes the number of tests and supplies required and reduces cost per IU (~25%) while preserving classification for LF. Thus administrative-unit size directly affects both the accuracy of classifying IUs (appropriate spatial resolution to detect foci) and the survey's cost-efficiency.

161.Option: The diagnostic method used for urinary schistosomiasis affects both the accuracy of intervention classification and the cost-efficiency of survey design due to its specificity in the local context
Evidence: Testing for urinary schistosomiasis infection could, for example, be conducted only with Hemastix reagent sticks (Bayer Diagnostics, Basingstoke, UK), instead of the current procedure whereby all samples with blood in urine and a sub-sample of negatives were cross checked using urine filtration. By cutting out the cost of these and other supplies for urine filtration, the cost per implementation unit surveyed would have decreased by 2% (Table 3).

In Southern Sudan, however, this modification would result in considerable misclassification of IUs for treatment with praziquantel owing to the relatively low specificity of Hemastix reagent strips in this setting [34].
161.Rationale: The passage documents a specific trade-off: replacing urine filtration with cheaper Hemastix strips would slightly reduce costs (≈2% per IU) but, because Hemastix has relatively low specificity in Southern Sudan, would cause "considerable misclassification of IUs" for treatment. This directly supports the statement that the chosen diagnostic method influences both accuracy of classification and cost-efficiency in this context.

161.Option: The use of a single standardized diagnostic test for all NTDs across all administrative units ensures both cost-efficiency and high accuracy in intervention classification
Evidence: Because of these differing transmission ecologies, prevalence data on each disease are required to identify areas of overlap and to target these with integrated PCT delivery [13].

The underlying rationale for such integrated surveys is the same as that for co-administration of PCT; reaching communities in SSA is often challenging and associated with considerable costs, so avoiding repeated access to conduct similar activities is likely to minimize the investment required to achieve a desired outcome, be it classification of an administrative area (e.g. district) for intervention or curing people from NTD infection.
Only the distribution of onchocerciasis has been comprehensively mapped, while LF mapping is ongoing in several countries and has not commenced in Chad and Eritrea [14].
161.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that the targeted diseases have differing transmission ecologies and thus require disease-specific prevalence data and diagnostics. It offers no support for the notion that a single standardized diagnostic for all NTDs would yield high accuracy; indeed, disease-specific tests and sometimes different sampling strategies are necessary. While integration can improve cost-efficiency by sharing logistics, a single test for all pathogens is neither proposed nor supported as accurate in the text.

161.Option: The geographic distribution of disease transmission zones determines the optimal implementation unit and thus balances cost and accuracy in survey design
Evidence: Co-endemicity, the prerequisite for the anticipated efficiency gains of integrated control, applies not at the country level, but at sub-national levels where climatic and other determinants are suitable for the transmission of more than one of above diseases [6], [7].
Because of these differing transmission ecologies, prevalence data on each disease are required to identify areas of overlap and to target these with integrated PCT delivery [13].

It is unlikely that sampling LF at the county level would fail to identify endemic foci, because in most settings the disease tends to occur over relatively large geographical areas [12] and survey sites are purposefully selected using information on the occurrence of lymphoedema and hydrocoele [23].
161.Rationale: The passage makes clear that spatial heterogeneity of transmission (ecologies and co-endemicity at sub-national levels) dictates what implementation unit is appropriate for each disease. Selecting an IU that aligns with transmission zones (e.g., payam vs county) therefore affects the accuracy of classifying units for intervention and has direct cost implications (sampling effort, number of tests). The authors use geographic transmission patterns to justify IU selection and sampling strategy.

161.Option: The number of survey teams deployed across administrative units affects the precision of intervention classification and the total financial burden of the survey
Evidence: The composition of survey teams varied depending on the number of NTDs to be surveyed in each location. Where all four NTDs were surveyed, teams included two drivers, one supervisor, one interviewer/translator, and up to four technicians, travelling in two vehicles.
existing vehicles and staff could be deployed in three teams, which would decrease the survey duration by about one day per payam and reduced the majority of running costs accordingly.
The principal cost drivers were personnel (34.4%) followed by survey consumables (30.4%) (Table 2).
161.Rationale: The passage supports that number and composition of teams influence operational speed and costs (duration and running costs) and that personnel are a major cost driver. However, it does not claim that the number of teams materially affects the precision/accuracy of classifying administrative units for intervention; precision is driven more by sampling design, IU size and diagnostics. Thus while team number affects financial burden, it is not presented as the most critical factor influencing classification accuracy.

162.Option: The human-animal-environment interface shaped by occupation and land use patterns
Evidence: the dual role of climate change and environmental change has led to a leap in the epidemic area range of HFRS.

factors relating to the dynamics of the rodent hosts and human activities, such as urbanization indicators, should be considered when understanding the results from these ecological studies, given the fact that China is a topographically heterogeneous country.

The study in Huludao City, Liaoning province found that HFRS activity in the population aged 35–59 years was significantly affected by air temperature, but this phenomenon could not be found in the population of other age groups [25]; the authors of this Huludao study attributed the phenomenon to the increased virus exposure in agricultural labor work in the middle-aged population.
162.Rationale: Interpretation of temperature–HFRS associations across geographic and temporal scales is critically influenced by the human–animal–environment interface because land use, urbanization and occupation (e.g., agricultural labor) alter exposure to infected rodent reservoirs and thus modify the observed relationship between temperature and disease incidence. The passage explicitly links environmental change, urbanization and human activities to shifts in epidemic range and to differing population-level associations, indicating this interface is a primary driver of geographic/temporal heterogeneity in findings.

162.Option: The seasonality of rodent population dynamics in different regions
Evidence: Climate and environmental changes could affect the reservoir ecology and dynamics of rodent carriers and hence trigger the spread of hantavirus transmission [5,8,9].
Effect of temperature on the HFRS epidemic was seasonal

With regards to the statistical methods adopted by the researchers to explore the correlation between air temperature and HFRS incidence, ... distributed lag nonlinear model conditions, conditional logistic regression analysis and wavelet analysis have been indicated in the included studies, as shown in Table 1.
162.Rationale: The reservoir (rodent) ecology and dynamics are directly implicated as mediators between temperature and human HFRS risk. Seasonal fluctuations in rodent populations and behavior (which vary regionally) will change transmission risk and thus critically affect how temperature correlates with HFRS across times and places. The passage explicitly states climate/environmental changes affect reservoir ecology and that the effect of temperature on the epidemic is seasonal, supporting the importance of rodent seasonality.

162.Option: The type of statistical model used to analyze the temperature-HFRS relationship
Evidence: The choice of appropriate statistical methods according to the type of HFRS epidemic area, the availability, fineness and data distribution characteristics of historical data such as epidemic situation or meteorological data in various regions is being another challenge.

With regards to the statistical methods adopted by the researchers to explore the correlation between air temperature and HFRS incidence, Spearman correlation analysis, Pearson correlation analysis, generalized additive model, seasonal differential autoregressive moving average model, negative binomial multivariate regression analysis, distributed lag nonlinear model conditions, conditional logistic regression analysis and wavelet analysis have been indicated in the included studies, as shown in Table 1.
