If the immunogens used in production are antigenically dissimilar to the toxins in the envenoming species, antibodies will not bind or neutralize effectively (as shown by EcAV/DrAV/BhAV/ViAV failing to neutralize H. hypnale while HhAV did). Therefore the degree of antigenic similarity between the venom toxins and the immunogens used to produce the antivenom is the critical factor determining cross-protection.

186.Option: The duration of time between venom exposure and antivenom administration
Evidence: Groups (n = 6) of mice were independently injected intraperitoneally (i.p.) with 2 LD50 (7 mg/kg) dose of Hhv in 50 μl of PBS. Post 10 min of venom injection, the mice were administered with ED50 (140 mg/kg), and 2 ED50 (280 mg/kg) doses of HhAV, and 700 mg/kg body weight of EcAV, DrAV, BhAV, and ViAV independently via tail vein (i.v.).
EcAV, DrAV, BhAV, and ViAV failed to neutralize the H. hypnale venom, while HhAV neutralized all the properties of its venom efficiently.

EcAV, DrAV, BhAV, and ViAV did not neutralize the biochemical properties such as proteolytic, indirect hemolytic, L-amino acid oxidase, and 5’-nucleotidase activities of H. hypnale venom even at an anti-venom dose of 10000 μg/ml (Fig 8).
186.Rationale: While timing of antivenom administration is clinically important, the passage shows that even when antivenoms were administered shortly after venom (e.g., 10 minutes post-injection), antivenoms raised against other species still failed to neutralize H. hypnale. The dominant reason for failure in these experiments was antigenic mismatch (lack of cross-reactivity), not timing. Thus timing is not the most critical factor in the context of an immunologically distinct venom.

186.Option: The molecular weight distribution of the venom proteins in the target species
Evidence: All three venoms displayed a unique protein-banding pattern in SDS-PAGE (10%) under non-reduced condition.
HhAV rigorously reacted with its low molecular mass protein bands as suggested by intense bands compared to EcAV and DrAV which showed only marginal intensity (Fig 7Ba).
Both BhAV and ViAV showed marginal cross-reactivity with H. hypnale venom, where the cross-reactivity was seen only with the high molecular mass protein bands.
186.Rationale: The passage documents differences in protein-banding patterns and notes which molecular-weight bands reacted with particular antivenoms, but it frames the core issue as antigenic distinctness and lack of cross-reactive antibodies rather than molecular weight per se. Molecular-weight distribution may correlate with which proteins are antigenic, but it is not the primary determinant of cross-protection: the critical issue is whether antigenic (epitope) similarity exists. Hence molecular-weight distribution alone is not the most critical factor.

186.Option: The concentration of antivenom administered relative to the venom dose in the envenomed individual
Evidence: Thus, wherever neutralization was achieved, the anti-venoms, HhAV/EcAV/DrAV/BhAV/ViAV neutralized the biochemical and pathological properties of H. hypnale, E. carinatus, and D. russelii venoms at a venom to anti-venom ratio of 1: 30–60 (w/w).
EcAV, DrAV, BhAV, and ViAV did not neutralize the biochemical properties ... of H. hypnale venom even at an anti-venom dose of 10000 μg/ml (Fig 8).

With an ED50 dose of 140 mg/kg (95% CL: 52.7–308.95 mg/kg) weight, the HhAV efficiently protected the mice from death while EcAV, DrAV, BhAV, and ViAV did not protect the mice even at the dose of 700 mg/kg weight.
186.Rationale: Dose (antivenom concentration relative to venom) is certainly important for neutralization when antibodies can bind the venom. The passage shows typical venom:antivenom neutralization ratios and ED50 values. However, it also demonstrates that even very large doses of non-matching antivenoms failed to neutralize H. hypnale venom. Thus concentration cannot overcome lack of antigenic recognition—antigenic match/cross-reactivity is the overriding factor. Therefore concentration is important but not the most critical factor for protection against an immunologically distinct venom.

186.Option: The presence of cross-reactive epitopes on the venom proteins that are conserved across species
Evidence: The study highlighted a markedly reduced cross-neutralizing competence of four commercial Indian anti-venoms against the said neglected snake venoms [4].
HhAV rigorously reacted with its low molecular mass protein bands as suggested by intense bands compared to EcAV and DrAV which showed only marginal intensity (Fig 7Ba).
Both BhAV and ViAV showed marginal cross-reactivity with H. hypnale venom, where the cross-reactivity was seen only with the high molecular mass protein bands.
EcAV, DrAV, BhAV, and ViAV failed to neutralize the H. hypnale venom, while HhAV neutralized all the properties of its venom efficiently.
Thus, paradoxically, marginal or lack of formation of cross-reacting antibodies underscore the immunological distinctness of homologous snake venoms.

Thus, different snake venoms attack similar targets, maybe with varying degrees of affinity and specificity, thus functionally closely related. Generally, shape complementarity is the hallmark of interacting agents... Hence, similar toxins (enzymatic or non-enzymatic) from different snake venoms are expected to share some degree of similarity, at least in their interacting/binding sites, and therefore in their antigenicity as well.
186.Rationale: The ability of an antivenom to protect against a venom from a different species depends on whether antibodies in the therapeutic preparation recognize and bind conserved epitopes on the venom proteins. The passage emphasizes reduced cross-neutralization and cross-reactivity where such conserved, cross-reactive epitopes are absent or limited (e.g., only high-MW bands showing marginal cross-reactivity). Where cross-reactive epitopes exist, antivenoms can neutralize heterologous venoms; where they do not, even high doses fail. Therefore presence (or absence) of conserved cross-reactive epitopes is a critical determinant of cross-protection.

187.Option: A serological test with high sensitivity for subclinical infection will lead to a lower estimated seroprevalence, indicating a reduced risk of transmission and allowing for less intensive control measures in endemic regions
Evidence:
LPG-ELISA is able to detect subclinical L. infantum infection in dogs
Regarding the ELISA tests carried out with serum samples from dogs with subclinical infection by L. infantum, the LPG-ELISA identified 9/10 (90%) as positive, while just one sample (1/9) was detected as positive by the TLA-ELISA.

In this study, we were able to include samples from 10 subclinically infected dogs that presented no alterations at the clinical examination and ancillary clinical pathology tests made by veterinarian infectologists.

One of the consequences of such variable specificities and sensitivities of different assays for CanL immunodiagnosis is that the seroprevalence in endemic areas can present marked fluctuations depending on the chosen assay [10].
187.Rationale: This option is incorrect because high sensitivity increases the detection of subclinical infections and therefore tends to increase, not lower, estimated seroprevalence. The passage explicitly shows LPG-ELISA detected 90% of subclinical infections compared with far fewer positives using other assays, and it states seroprevalence can vary markedly depending on the assay used. Thus high sensitivity would raise measured seroprevalence and would not by itself indicate reduced transmission risk or automatically allow less intensive control.

187.Option: A serological test with high sensitivity for subclinical infection will result in a higher number of false-negative results, leading to underestimation of the true infection burden and undermining the effectiveness of control programs
Evidence: false-negative results, the LPG-ELISA identified nine samples from infected dogs as negative (S1 Table), with OD readings below the established cut-off point, while the TLA-ELISA presented 17 false-negative results.
The TR DPP-CVL immunochromatographic assay presented 10 false negative and 05 false positive results, while LPG-ELISA had 09 false negatives and 01 false positives.
187.Rationale: This statement is incorrect because high sensitivity reduces the number of false-negative results rather than increases them. The passage contrasts assays of differing sensitivity and shows the more sensitive assay (LPG-ELISA) produced fewer false negatives (9) than the less sensitive TLA-ELISA (17). Therefore a highly sensitive test would reduce underestimation of infection burden, not increase it.

187.Option: A serological test with high sensitivity for subclinical infection will reduce the number of false-positive results, leading to more accurate estimates of population immunity and enabling broader culling of seropositive dogs in control programs
Evidence: The TR DPP-CVL immunochromatographic assay presented 10 false negative and 05 false positive results, while LPG-ELISA had 09 false negatives and 01 false positives.
These results conferred a 93.1% specificity and 90.6% sensitivity for TR DPP, and LPG-ELISA consequently had higher validation parameters (98.5% specificity and 91.5% sensitivity).
False-positive results can lead to the unnecessary treatment or even culling of non-infected dogs.
187.Rationale: This option conflates sensitivity with specificity. High sensitivity reduces false negatives; reductions in false positives are driven by high specificity. The passage shows LPG-ELISA combined high sensitivity and high specificity and that fewer false positives (important to avoid unnecessary culling) are due to specificity. Thus high sensitivity alone does not guarantee fewer false positives or justify broader culling; in fact the authors warn that false positives can lead to unnecessary culling and that avoiding false positives is desirable.

187.Option: A serological test with high sensitivity for subclinical infection will improve the accuracy of predicting disease progression, allowing for early intervention and reducing the need for population-wide control strategies
Evidence: For this reason, in Brazil the official protocol recommended by the Ministry of Agriculture for the diagnosis of CanL is based on the application of two tests, a screening immunochromatographic assay with the recombinant rK28 protein as antigen (TR DPP-CVL), and a confirmatory ELISA with L. major total antigen (EIE). The need for two tests makes the control of the disease expensive and time-consuming.

The proper diagnosis of subclinically infected dogs is very important for the control of leishmaniasis in endemic areas, since these dogs can remain untreated or unfollowed. Susceptible subclinically infected dogs can develop active infection, representing a source of infection for other dogs and humans.
Such dogs, even with a recent infection, produce specific antibodies in high levels, before developing clinical signs and would also be characterized as having subclinical infection.

Leishmania-specific humoral responses in susceptible dogs are characterized by high levels of specific immunoglobulins [48]. However, such abundant immunoglobulins are non-protective and are instead associated with active infection, intense disease development and presentation of clinical signs in dogs [47, 48].

Regarding the ELISA tests carried out with serum samples from dogs with subclinical infection by L. infantum, the LPG-ELISA identified 9/10 (90%) as positive, while just one sample (1/9) was detected as positive by the TLA-ELISA.
187.Rationale: This option is correct because detecting subclinically infected animals with a sensitive test can identify individuals who are antibody-positive and at risk of progressing to clinical disease (the passage notes susceptible subclinically infected dogs produce high antibody levels before symptoms and that humoral markers are associated with progression). Early identification enables targeted clinical follow-up or treatment of these animals rather than blunt population-wide measures, potentially reducing the need for broad interventions. The passage emphasizes the importance of diagnosing subclinical infection for control and notes that more sensitive assays (e.g., LPG-ELISA) detect many more subclinical infections.

187.Option: A serological test with high sensitivity for subclinical infection will increase estimated seroprevalence, potentially misrepresenting population immunity and necessitating more targeted control measures
Evidence: Regarding the ELISA tests carried out with serum samples from dogs with subclinical infection by L. infantum, the LPG-ELISA identified 9/10 (90%) as positive, while just one sample (1/9) was detected as positive by the TLA-ELISA.

One of the consequences of such variable specificities and sensitivities of different assays for CanL immunodiagnosis is that the seroprevalence in endemic areas can present marked fluctuations depending on the chosen assay [10].
Such abundant immunoglobulins are non-protective and are instead associated with active infection, intense disease development and presentation of clinical signs in dogs [47, 48].

The proper diagnosis of subclinically infected dogs is very important for the control of leishmaniasis in endemic areas, since these dogs can remain untreated or unfollowed. Susceptible subclinically infected dogs can develop active infection, representing a source of infection for other dogs and humans.
187.Rationale: This option is correct. A highly sensitive test will detect more subclinical infections and therefore raise measured seroprevalence. Because antibody detection reflects exposure and not necessarily protective immunity (the passage explicitly notes abundant antibodies can be non-protective), higher seroprevalence could be misinterpreted as high population-level immunity. The passage also stresses that assay choice alters seroprevalence estimates and that identifying subclinical reservoirs is important for targeted control; thus a sensitive test supports more targeted interventions rather than simple assumptions of herd immunity.

188.Option: Fit separate models for each country to estimate individual-level effects while controlling for country-specific baseline infection rates
Evidence: We compare three different Binomial mixed models, making use of either individual-level variables, country-level indicators or both sources of information.
Data from countries that had only one individual were excluded since it would not be possible to represent the country in both the training set and the testing set.

The data consists of 3,830 individuals (migrants) from 64 countries. Of these, 87.4% were males. Fig 2 shows the number of individuals from each country. We observe that 12 countries were only represented by one migrant.
188.Rationale: Fitting separate models for each country is not the approach used in the study and would be impractical here. The authors use pooled mixed models rather than per-country models (first quote). Moreover, many countries have very small sample sizes (12 countries with a single migrant), and the authors explicitly excluded singletons from some procedures because countries with one individual cannot be represented in both training and test sets (second and third quotes). Separate country-specific models would therefore lack power, be unstable for small-sample countries, and were not the methodological choice of the paper.

188.Option: Apply a fixed-effects model with country dummies to control for unobserved country-level confounders while estimating individual-level effects
Evidence: The second model (M2) accounts for the country-level indicators and an additional term accounting for the variance between countries which could not be accounted for by the covariates which will be referred to as the country random effects.
The third model (M3) accounts for both individual-level variables and country-level indicators, along with an individual random effects term and a country random effects term.
A two-step estimation procedure was used to fit each of the mixed effects models.
188.Rationale: While fixed-effects with country dummies can control for unobserved country-level confounders, the study instead used mixed-effects models with country-level random effects (first and second quotes) to capture between-country variance and to allow prediction for new countries. The authors explicitly describe fitting mixed-effects models and estimating random-effect variances (third quote). Thus the fixed-effects-with-dummies strategy is not the methodological choice employed and would also limit generalizability to unsampled countries.

188.Option: Use a mixed-effects model with separate random effects for individuals and countries to disentangle the contributions of individual-level and country-level factors
Evidence: We compare three different Binomial mixed models, making use of either individual-level variables, country-level indicators or both sources of information.

The first model (M1) accounts for the individual-level variables and an additional term accounting for the variance between individuals which could not be accounted for by the covariates and will be referred to as the individual random effect.

The second model (M2) accounts for the country-level indicators and an additional term accounting for the variance between countries which could not be accounted for by the covariates which will be referred to as the country random effects.
The third model (M3) accounts for both individual-level variables and country-level indicators, along with an individual random effects term and a country random effects term.
188.Rationale: This option precisely matches the study's chosen methodology (M3). The authors fit Binomial mixed-effects models that include both individual- and country-level covariates and explicitly include random effects at both the individual and country levels to capture unmeasured heterogeneity (all quotes). That structure allows estimating fixed-effect contributions of predictors at each level while partitioning residual variance into individual- and country-level components, thereby disentangling the influence of country versus individual factors on infection risk.

188.Option: Include country-level indicators as fixed effects and individual-level variables as random effects to isolate the impact of country-specific factors on infection risk
Evidence: The first model (M1) accounts for the individual-level variables and an additional term accounting for the variance between individuals which could not be accounted for by the covariates and will be referred to as the individual random effect.
The third model (M3) accounts for both individual-level variables and country-level indicators, along with an individual random effects term and a country random effects term.
Where all the covariates are individual variables provided by the migrants. Specifically, is their age,  is their sex, and  is their time spent in Italy.
188.Rationale: The suggested option inverts the roles used in the paper. The study treats individual-level variables (age, sex, time in Italy) as fixed-effect covariates and adds an individual-level random effect to capture unexplained individual heterogeneity (first and third quotes). It does not treat individual-level variables themselves as random effects. Therefore this recommendation does not reflect the study's modeling strategy and would be conceptually inappropriate for isolating fixed individual predictors while using random effects to model unexplained variance.

188.Option: Use a hierarchical regression model with country-level indicators as predictors and individual-level variables as outcome measures to assess the influence of country context on individual infection status
Evidence: We compare three different Binomial mixed models, making use of either individual-level variables, country-level indicators or both sources of information.

The predictive power of M1, M2, and M3 were assessed in two main scenarios: predicting for a new individual from a country in the existing dataset and predicting for a new individual from a new unsampled country.
The predictive power is assessed using a cross-validation technique outlined below.
188.Rationale: This option misstates the modeling direction. The study uses hierarchical/mixed models where the outcome is infection status (binary) and the predictors are both individual-level and country-level variables; it does not treat individual-level variables as outcomes (first quote). The models are used to predict infection likelihood for individuals (second and third quotes). Thus the proposed reversal (individual-level variables as outcomes) is incorrect and not the methodological choice made to assess the influence of country context on individual infection risk.

189.Option: Use a permutation test to randomly reassign variant labels to years and assess whether the observed shift in dominance is more extreme than expected by chance
Evidence: We obtained 79 nucleotide sequences from patients hospitalized in 2001 (n = 15), 2002 (n = 8), 2003 (n = 4), 2004 (n = 7), 2005 (n = 3), 2006 (n = 2), 2010 (n = 10), 2011 (n = 6), 2012 (n = 11) and 2013 (n = 13).
On average we sequenced approximately 50% of total confirmed cases in each municipality. Therefore our results portray a realistic picture of the distribution of viral variants in the endemic area.

With regard to the temporal changes in virus population we observed changing dynamics of viral variant abundances from 2011 to 2013. From 2001 to 2010 the two major phylogenetic groups (A1 and A2) occurred in similar abundances. However in 2011, 80% confirmed patients were infected with A1 virus variant (and 20% with A3). On the contrary, in 2012 we detected the A2 virus variant alone (we sequenced 92% confirmed CCHF cases).
189.Rationale: A permutation (randomization) test directly addresses the null hypothesis that observed temporal clustering of variants could arise from random sampling. The passage documents per-year counts and explicitly notes abrupt shifts in variant abundances (e.g., 80% A1 in 2011 vs only A2 in 2012) and varying sequencing fractions; a permutation test that randomly reassigns variant labels to years while preserving per-year sample sizes tests whether the observed pattern is more extreme than expected by chance given the actual sampling. This is an appropriate, assumption-minimal way to distinguish non-random temporal shifts from random sampling variation in the context of the data structure described.

189.Option: Calculate the frequency of each viral variant per year and compare the trend over time using linear regression to detect non-random changes
Evidence: We obtained 79 nucleotide sequences from patients hospitalized in 2001 (n = 15), 2002 (n = 8), 2003 (n = 4), 2004 (n = 7), 2005 (n = 3), 2006 (n = 2), 2010 (n = 10), 2011 (n = 6), 2012 (n = 11) and 2013 (n = 13).
With regard to the temporal changes in virus population we observed changing dynamics of viral variant abundances from 2011 to 2013.
On average we sequenced approximately 50% of total confirmed cases in each municipality.
189.Rationale: Calculating annual frequencies is informative (and the passage provides per-year counts and frequencies), but using simple linear regression on raw frequencies is not the most appropriate statistical approach for proportion data with variable and sometimes small yearly sample sizes, nor does it account for non-independence, overdispersion, or the multinomial nature of variants. Better approaches would be logistic/multinomial regression or permutation/phylogenetic methods that account for sampling uncertainty. Thus linear regression of frequencies is suboptimal for testing whether observed shifts exceed random sampling variation or reflect epidemiological drivers.

189.Option: Use Bayesian coalescent analysis with time-scaled phylogenies to test whether observed variant frequency shifts exceed expectations under neutral evolution
Evidence: Bayesian phylogenetic analyses were performed in MrBayes 3.2 [15] and Tracer version 1.5 [16]. Four independent Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) runs of four chains each consisting of 10,000,000 generations were run to ensure effective sample sizes (ESS) of at least 1000.

We estimated the evolutionary rates using a MCMC method implemented in BEAST 1.8.0 [16] with a relaxed molecular clock (under the GTR+G+I model of nucleotide substitution) and a piecewise-constant Bayesian skyline plot as a coalescent prior.
We estimated a mean evolutionary rate of 2.76×10−4 substitutions/site/year and the mean tMRCA for the root of 729.4 years ago.
With regard to the temporal changes in virus population we observed changing dynamics of viral variant abundances from 2011 to 2013.
189.Rationale: Bayesian coalescent/time-scaled phylogenetic approaches (e.g., BEAST) explicitly model genealogies, demographic history, and the timing of lineage divergence, allowing formal tests of whether changes in lineage frequencies and population size over time are consistent with neutral demographic processes or reflect selection/transmission-driven shifts. The passage indicates the authors have the dated sequences, used Bayesian MCMC, estimated evolutionary rates and tMRCAs, and used skyline priors—tools well suited to infer whether frequency changes exceed neutral expectations and to relate shifts to transmission dynamics and host interactions. Therefore this method is highly appropriate to infer drivers of observed shifts beyond random sampling variation.

189.Option: Perform a chi-squared test for independence between year and viral variant group to assess whether the observed distribution differs from random chance
Evidence: We obtained 79 nucleotide sequences from patients hospitalized in 2001 (n = 15), 2002 (n = 8), 2003 (n = 4), 2004 (n = 7), 2005 (n = 3), 2006 (n = 2), 2010 (n = 10), 2011 (n = 6), 2012 (n = 11) and 2013 (n = 13).
On average we sequenced approximately 50% of total confirmed cases in each municipality.
In 2011, 80% confirmed patients were infected with A1 virus variant (and 20% with A3). On the contrary, in 2012 we detected the A2 virus variant alone (we sequenced 92% confirmed CCHF cases).
189.Rationale: A chi-squared test for independence can test whether the joint distribution of year and variant departs from expected randomness, and is a simple first-pass test. However, chi-squared assumptions (adequate expected counts per cell, independence) may be violated with small yearly sample sizes and sparse variant categories; it does not account for phylogenetic relationships among variants, sampling fraction variability, or the temporal structure of sequence data. For inferring whether shifts are driven by transmission and host-pathogen interactions (rather than mere non-random counts), more sophisticated time-scaled phylogenetic/coalescent approaches are preferable. Thus while chi-squared can indicate non-randomness, it is not the most appropriate method for the deeper inference posed.

189.Option: Compare the genetic distance between variants in consecutive years to determine if the observed shifts reflect increased evolutionary divergence
Evidence: Overall identity of the sequences ranged from 98.5–100%. All sequences clustered in the European genetic lineage V and were distributed in 5 genetic groups (A1–A5).
We estimated a mean evolutionary rate of 2.76×10−4 substitutions/site/year and the mean tMRCA for the root of 729.4 years ago.
With regard to the temporal changes in virus population we observed changing dynamics of viral variant abundances from 2011 to 2013.
189.Rationale: Comparing genetic distances year-to-year measures divergence but does not test whether frequency shifts are due to changes in transmission dynamics, host interactions, or ecological drivers as opposed to sampling variation. The passage shows low overall divergence (high identity, slow evolutionary rate) and multiple co-circulating clades; shifts in relative frequency can occur without substantial additional divergence (e.g., by demographic/ ecological processes). Therefore genetic-distance comparisons alone are insufficient to distinguish epidemiological drivers from random sampling and are not the most appropriate method for the question posed.

190.Option: Because antibiotic use is common during respiratory illness, microbiota changes during pneumonia episodes should be interpreted as evidence of treatment response rather than disease progression
Evidence: Although clinical pneumonia itself does not seem to have a predictable effect on the microbiota in this cohort, the use of antibiotics may correlate with changing proportional abundance of some taxa.
The study included 21 swabs taken during antibiotic consumption and 19 within 7 days of antibiotic use. In most cases the antibiotic consumed was a 7-day course of amoxicillin.

Tentatively, the distribution of proportional abundance values during antibiotic consumption compared to non-antibiotic samples was lower for Moraxella II and higher for Brachybacterium, Dolosigranulum and Streptococcus I (Supplementary S3 Fig). However due to the small number of antibiotic episodes captured in the cohort this pattern could not be reproduced using age-matched samples equally representing each child, and would need to be tested with a larger cross section of samples from the population.
190.Rationale: The option asserts a categorical interpretation (changes should be interpreted as treatment response rather than disease progression). The passage explicitly cautions that clinical pneumonia does not have a predictable effect on the microbiota and that antibiotic use may correlate with changes, but the sample size is too small to make definitive attributions. Therefore it is not valid to conclude changes during pneumonia episodes are solely evidence of treatment response rather than potentially reflecting disease, treatment, or both.

190.Option: The lack of consistent microbiota changes before pneumonia episodes suggests that antibiotic exposure has no meaningful impact on microbiota composition during illness
Evidence: There is no evidence in this study of clinical pneumonia being predicted by a change in the microbiota before illness.
Although clinical pneumonia itself does not seem to have a predictable effect on the microbiota in this cohort, the use of antibiotics may correlate with changing proportional abundance of some taxa.

However due to the small number of antibiotic episodes captured in the cohort this pattern could not be reproduced using age-matched samples equally representing each child, and would need to be tested with a larger cross section of samples from the population.
190.Rationale: The absence of consistent pre-illness microbiota changes does not imply antibiotics have no impact during illness. The authors note that antibiotic use may correlate with shifts in taxa abundances, but that the observations are tentative because of small numbers. Thus one cannot conclude 'no meaningful impact' of antibiotics from the lack of pre-illness changes.

190.Option: Since pneumonia episodes are not preceded by microbiota changes, any observed shifts during illness must be due to the illness itself and not confounded by antibiotic use
Evidence: There is no evidence in this study of clinical pneumonia being predicted by a change in the microbiota before illness.
Although clinical pneumonia itself does not seem to have a predictable effect on the microbiota in this cohort, the use of antibiotics may correlate with changing proportional abundance of some taxa.
The study included 21 swabs taken during antibiotic consumption and 19 within 7 days of antibiotic use. In most cases the antibiotic consumed was a 7-day course of amoxicillin.
190.Rationale: This option incorrectly rules out confounding by antibiotics. The passage indicates pneumonia is not predictably preceded by microbiota change, but also explicitly states antibiotic use may correlate with changing abundances and that many swabs were taken during or soon after antibiotic use. Therefore observed shifts during illness could be caused by antibiotics, by illness, or both; they are not necessarily attributable solely to the disease.

190.Option: Since antibiotic use is not systematically timed relative to illness onset, microbiota changes during pneumonia episodes can be reliably attributed to the disease process alone
Evidence: The study included 21 swabs taken during antibiotic consumption and 19 within 7 days of antibiotic use. In most cases the antibiotic consumed was a 7-day course of amoxicillin.
Although clinical pneumonia itself does not seem to have a predictable effect on the microbiota in this cohort, the use of antibiotics may correlate with changing proportional abundance of some taxa.

However due to the small number of antibiotic episodes captured in the cohort this pattern could not be reproduced using age-matched samples equally representing each child, and would need to be tested with a larger cross section of samples from the population.
190.Rationale: The option claims reliable attribution to disease despite non-systematic timing of antibiotics; the passage contradicts this. Because many swabs were taken during or shortly after antibiotic courses and antibiotics may alter taxa abundances, one cannot reliably attribute microbiota changes during pneumonia solely to the disease without accounting for antibiotic timing.

190.Option: Microbiota changes observed during pneumonia episodes may be attributable to antibiotic treatment rather than the illness itself, necessitating careful timing analysis to disentangle cause and effect
Evidence: Although clinical pneumonia itself does not seem to have a predictable effect on the microbiota in this cohort, the use of antibiotics may correlate with changing proportional abundance of some taxa.
The study included 21 swabs taken during antibiotic consumption and 19 within 7 days of antibiotic use. In most cases the antibiotic consumed was a 7-day course of amoxicillin.

Tentatively, the distribution of proportional abundance values during antibiotic consumption compared to non-antibiotic samples was lower for Moraxella II and higher for Brachybacterium, Dolosigranulum and Streptococcus I (Supplementary S3 Fig). However due to the small number of antibiotic episodes captured in the cohort this pattern could not be reproduced using age-matched samples equally representing each child, and would need to be tested with a larger cross section of samples from the population.
190.Rationale: The passage supports the interpretation that antibiotic exposure can influence microbiota composition and that many samples were taken during or shortly after antibiotic courses. Because clinical pneumonia did not have a predictable effect on the microbiota, observed shifts during illness could plausibly reflect antibiotic effects. The authors therefore indicate the need for careful analysis of timing (and larger samples) to disentangle antibiotic effects from disease-related changes, matching the statement in this option.

192.Option: Inadequate enforcement of government regulations prohibiting human excreta use in agriculture
Evidence: Despite laws that prohibit use of human excreta for agriculture in Vietnam, this practice remains common among certain farming groups [24].
192.Rationale: The passage documents that use of excreta persists despite prohibitive laws, which implies that regulation alone has not eliminated the practice. However, the text attributes farmers' choices more directly to economic incentives and perceived agricultural benefits (e.g., cost savings, perceived soil benefits). While inadequate enforcement may enable continued use, it is not presented as the primary or most direct influence on a farmer's decision to select a risk-increasing fertilization method.

192.Option: Cultural traditions and long-standing practices of using human excreta in agriculture
Evidence: Application of human excreta onto rice paddies as fertilizer is a common practice in northern Vietnam, where many farmers use single or double vault latrines, lack access to wastewater infrastructure, and have variable access to commercial inorganic fertilizers [1].
Despite laws that prohibit use of human excreta for agriculture in Vietnam, this practice remains common among certain farming groups [24].
192.Rationale: The passage describes the practice as common and persistent, which could reflect cultural or habitual behavior. However, the authors emphasize economic reasons and perceived agronomic benefits as the primary drivers of continued use (e.g., explicit cost-savings and beliefs about soil benefits). Thus, while tradition contributes, it is not portrayed as the most direct influence on choosing a high-risk fertilization method.

192.Option: Perceived agricultural benefits of human excreta, such as improved soil structure and long-term nutrient availability
Evidence: many farmers consider human excreta a superior source of long-term nutrition for plants and soil [9].

Human excreta is perceived as more valuable than animal manure due to differences in dietary protein content, and it is believed to improve soil structure more sustainably than inorganic fertilizers [9].
focus group participants in the RRD emphasized ease of application and benefits to soil structure, rather than the benefits of composting to protect human health [1].
192.Rationale: Perceived agronomic benefits are clearly an important motivator for farmers and are repeatedly cited in the passage. However, the question asks which factor most directly influences the likelihood of choosing a method that increases helminth risk. The passage gives the strongest, most explicit emphasis to economic necessity and quantified cost-savings as the primary driver of this choice. Therefore, while perceived benefits are influential, they are not depicted as the single most direct factor compared with economic necessity.

192.Option: Economic necessity due to limited affordability and access to commercial fertilizers
Evidence: Using human waste to fertilize crops is recognized as a way to decrease household expenditures; however, it is unclear how costs and health risks associated with STH infection interact.
Our cost analysis indicated that farmers could save 37,000–847,000 VND/yr (1.48–37.28 USD, $2017) [26] by using human excreta.
While these savings might appear low, they represent 1–22% of a farmer’s average annual income in the RRD [27].
Therefore, it is unlikely that low-income rural farmers would be willing to universally replace organic fertilizers of human origin with inorganic commercial fertilizers.
192.Rationale: The passage repeatedly and quantitatively highlights economic savings from using human excreta and frames affordability as a central reason farmers continue the practice. The authors conclude that these savings, which can represent a substantial fraction of annual income, make it unlikely low-income farmers will switch to inorganic fertilizers. Thus economic necessity is the most direct influence on selecting a fertilization method that increases risk of STH infection.

192.Option: Lack of knowledge about the health risks of handling fresh human excreta among farmers
Evidence: Some misperceptions about the reasons for safe composting might influence farmer willingness to use fresh excreta.
focus group participants in the RRD emphasized ease of application and benefits to soil structure, rather than the benefits of composting to protect human health [1].
WHO recommendations for hygienic composting are not commonly followed, as farmers harvest multiple crops per year and are unwilling or unable to store excreta for six months prior to use [5,6].
192.Rationale: The passage notes misperceptions and that farmers prioritize ease and agronomic benefits over health protection, indicating gaps in how health risks factor into decisions. Nevertheless, the text attributes the major, direct influence on choice to economic incentives (cost savings) and agronomic perceptions. Lack of knowledge contributes but is not presented as the dominant, most direct factor driving selection of high-risk fertilization methods.

193.Option: The presence of comorbidities such as chickenpox and influenza in study populations may confound cure rate estimates and must be controlled for in analysis
Evidence: Furthermore, the same study has reported additional adverse events unrelated to the treatment, including chickenpox, flu, muscle pain, and gastrointestinal symptoms [39].
In all instances, these values returned to normal levels after treatment, during a follow-up period of up to 6 months [39].
193.Rationale: While the passage notes that one study reported events such as chickenpox and flu among participants, it presents those as unrelated adverse events rather than as major confounders of cure rate estimates. The review emphasizes species, definitions of cure, and methodological heterogeneity as primary drivers of differences in observed cure rates across studies. The provided quotes show comorbid infections were noted as unrelated adverse events that resolved, not as primary influences on pooled cure-rate interpretation.

193.Option: The geographic region of the study population is the primary factor influencing cure rates due to differences in healthcare access and socioeconomic status
Evidence: studies were stratified by regions, confirming the possible influence of Leishmania species as a factor involved in the outcome, in addition to other local factors impacting the cure rates, based on the high heterogeneity remaining in the evaluation of Old-World studies (63.8%, CI: 24.3–90.7%; I2 = 87.3; p = 0.005) (Fig 2B) [16].

Another aspect that deserves mention is, while cutaneous leishmaniasis (CL) is prevalent in 89 countries across the Eastern Mediterranean region, the Americas, and Africa [42], research efforts have predominantly focused on the Old-World disease, with a particular emphasis on species like L. (L.) major and L. (L.) tropica, as demonstrated in this review.
differences between the spontaneous cure rates in the Old and New World, estimated at 60% for species such as L. (L.) major [44] and approximately 6% for L. (V.) braziliensis [45].
193.Rationale: The passage acknowledges geographic differences, but it attributes regional differences in cure rates primarily to variation in Leishmania species distribution (and species-specific spontaneous cure rates), not explicitly to healthcare access or socioeconomic status. The quoted text shows the authors link regional heterogeneity to species and local factors rather than asserting healthcare access/socioeconomic status as the primary driver. Therefore the option's asserted primary mechanism (healthcare/socioeconomics) is not supported by the passage.

193.Option: The definition of cure used in each study is the most important factor affecting the comparability of cure rates across different populations
Evidence: Only one study defined cure as complete re-epithelialization of all lesions and complete disappearance of the induration [39]. In other studies, the definition of cure was variable, in general assumed in face of 75 to 90% of epithelialization/ involution of the lesion [36,38,40]. In one study, the definition adopted for cure was not stated [37].

This discrepancy in results suggests that there may be a difference between formulations or, alternatively, it may have also been influenced by the cure definition adopted in the studies, not always clearly presented in the publication.
193.Rationale: The passage clearly states that variable cure definitions affect comparability and are an important source of heterogeneity. However, the question asks which factor most critically influences interpretation of cure rates across studies, 'particularly when comparing results from regions with varying endemic species.' In that specific context, the review emphasizes species differences (including very different spontaneous cure rates by species) as the most critical influence. Thus while cure-definition variability is important for comparability, the passage gives primacy to species as the critical determinant when comparing regions with differing endemic species.

193.Option: The number of treatment applications per week significantly affects cure rates and must be standardized across studies to ensure valid comparisons
Evidence: Based on the studies compiled in this review, it is not possible to identify one specific formulation or route for the local administration of AmB that is superior to others.

It can be highlighted, among others, the observation of the lack of evidence for association between effect and number of applications of the ointment formulation addressed by López at al., (2018) [39], in the same way that a higher concentration of the AmB solution infiltrated by Goswami et al., (2019) [38] did not affect the efficacy of the treatment.
193.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports a lack of evidence for an association between the number of ointment applications and treatment effect in at least one study, and that higher concentration did not change efficacy in another. Therefore, the review does not support the claim that application frequency is a major determinant of cure-rate differences across studies; it is not presented as the critical factor to standardize for valid cross-study comparisons.

193.Option: The species of Leishmania involved in infection is a critical determinant of baseline recovery and must be accounted for when interpreting cure rates across studies
Evidence: The Leishmania species evaluated in studies were L (L.) major (12), L (L.) mexicana (3) and L. (L.) amazonensis (2). Nguyen et al., 2019 [28] evaluated both L. (L.) major and L. (L.) mexicana.

studies were stratified by regions, confirming the possible influence of Leishmania species as a factor involved in the outcome, in addition to other local factors impacting the cure rates, based on the high heterogeneity remaining in the evaluation of Old-World studies (63.8%, CI: 24.3–90.7%; I2 = 87.3; p = 0.005) (Fig 2B) [16].
differences between the spontaneous cure rates in the Old and New World, estimated at 60% for species such as L. (L.) major [44] and approximately 6% for L. (V.) braziliensis [45].

This discrepancy in results suggests that there may be a difference between formulations or, alternatively, it may have also been influenced by the cure definition adopted in the studies, not always clearly presented in the publication.
193.Rationale: The passage repeatedly emphasizes that Leishmania species vary across studies and regions and that species differences plausibly explain much of the heterogeneity in cure rates (including very different spontaneous cure probabilities: ~60% for L. major vs ~6% for L. braziliensis). The authors explicitly stratified analyses by region to probe species influence. Therefore, accounting for the infecting Leishmania species is critical when interpreting and comparing cure rates across studies from different endemic regions.

194.Option: The presence of multiple mosquito species with high viral diversity suggests that the region may serve as a reservoir for novel arboviruses, warranting ongoing surveillance to detect potential spillover events
Evidence: Mosquitoes are major vectors that transmit numerous arboviruses to various hosts, causing disease in both humans and animals [3, 4].

A total of 394,144,760 paired-end reads (55.4 G base pair) were generated through high-throughput sequencing, with an average of about 9.9 M reads per 500 mosquitoes (2.2–23.8 M reads/500 mosquitoes).
3,014,183 reads were identified as viral reads, accounting for 0.95% (3,014,183/316,821,976) of all clean reads, which suggests a rich diversity of viruses harbored by mosquitoes.
The classified viral reads were distributed into 31 viral families and 116 viral species
Thus, the spectrum of mosquito-borne viruses is extremely diverse and varies with the time of collection, as well as the type of mosquitoes.
194.Rationale: The passage documents high viral read counts, broad taxonomic breadth (31 families, 116 species), and explicitly states that the mosquito virome is extremely diverse and varies by mosquito type. From an epidemiologic perspective, high diversity across multiple vector species increases the chance that novel or previously unrecognized arboviruses are present or could emerge (reservoir/spillover potential). That empirical finding in the passage directly supports ongoing surveillance to detect such events.

194.Option: The genetic similarity of the detected JEV strain to known pathogenic genotypes suggests that the virus may have been introduced from another region, highlighting the need for surveillance to monitor cross-border transmission
Evidence: JEV was detected in the pools of Cx.t from Gansu in July (396 reads, 41% coverage, 16.4× depth). The consensus sequences showed 98.1% of nucleotide identity to JEV Genotype-I (HM366552).

Quang Binh virus was first identified from Cx.t collected in Vietnam in 2002, and has since been reported in southern China, such as Yunnan Province, Guangdong Province, and Shanghai City, but this study was the first report of Quang Binh virus in northwestern China [41, 43, 45].

With changes in climate, urban ecology, high-speed railway and airline travel, and other economic and social activities, the reproduction of mosquito vectors and arboviruses is no longer limited to specific regions [20, 29].
194.Rationale: The passage reports a JEV sequence with high nucleotide identity to a recognized pathogenic genotype (Genotype I) and documents detection of viruses previously reported elsewhere (e.g., Quang Binh virus first identified in Vietnam) plus commentary about increased movement and dispersal. High genetic similarity to known genotypes and first detections of viruses in this region are consistent with introduction or movement of viruses across regions. That scenario supports surveillance to monitor cross-border introduction and circulation.

194.Option: The detection of JEV in pigpen environments suggests that livestock may be amplifying the virus, increasing the risk of human exposure and justifying continued surveillance
Evidence: Mosquitoes were collected between June to August of 2019 from seven cities in the Shaanxi-Gansu-Ningxia region (Fig 1). Farms, pigpens, sheep pens, and residential areas were sampled.
JEV was found in Cx.t from pigpen environments in Longnan City (Gansu Province in July).
GETV, belonging to family Togaviridae genus Alphavirus, is an important animal pathogen that causes disease in livestock such as pigs, cattle, and horses [37–39].
An outbreak was reported at a swine farm in Hunan Province in 2017[43].
194.Rationale: The passage documents detection of JEV in mosquitoes collected in pigpen environments and notes livestock-associated arboviruses (GETV) and outbreaks linked to swine. Although the text does not explicitly state 'pigs amplify JEV,' in arbovirus epidemiology pigs are well-recognized amplifying hosts for JEV; finding JEV-positive vectors in pigpens therefore plausibly indicates local amplification potential and increased human risk. The passage's direct observation of JEV in pigpen settings supports continued surveillance at livestock interfaces.

194.Option: The detection of a human-pathogenic JEV genotype in local mosquito vectors indicates active transmission risk, necessitating continued surveillance despite historical low-risk status
Evidence: JEV was detected in the pools of Cx.t from Gansu in July (396 reads, 41% coverage, 16.4× depth). The consensus sequences showed 98.1% of nucleotide identity to JEV Genotype-I (HM366552).
JEV is a significant human pathogen and GETV causes disease in animals such as pigs and horses.
The Shaanxi-Gansu-Ningxia region ... is traditionally a low epidemic area for JEV, but endured a significant outbreak of this disease in 2018, with approximately 833 cases reported [20, 21].
194.Rationale: The passage documents detection of JEV in local vectors and states the detected sequence closely matches a human-pathogenic genotype. Combined with the region's history of a substantial outbreak in 2018 despite prior low classification, detection of an active, human-pathogenic lineage in vectors constitutes direct evidence of transmission risk and supports ongoing surveillance.

194.Option: The identification of JEV in Culex tritaeniorhynchus mosquitoes during peak mosquito activity in July indicates that seasonal patterns of vector abundance may drive transmission, necessitating surveillance during high-risk months
Evidence: In total, 22,959 mosquitoes were collected during the peak mosquito activity period from June to August in the Shaanxi-Gansu-Ningxia region of China (Fig 1 and S1 Table).
JEV was detected in the pools of Cx.t from Gansu in July (396 reads, 41% coverage, 16.4× depth).
Our results also showed that mosquitoes collected in July showed greater viral diversity than those obtained in June (36 species in 18 families) or August (72 species in 25 families).
194.Rationale: The passage places the JEV detection in July, within the documented peak mosquito activity window, and notes that July collections had greater viral abundance and diversity. Seasonal peaks in vector abundance and viral detection indicate increased transmission potential during specific months, supporting targeted, ongoing surveillance during high-risk periods.

195.Option: The environmental suitability index should be used in conjunction with dengue fever prevalence data to identify areas at risk for multiple vector-borne diseases.
Evidence: The BRT method models environmental suitability of onchocerciasis transmission as a suitability index (from 0 to 1) based on the values of environmental covariates at the locations corresponding to occurrence inputs.

With over 13 000 occurrence point inputs, we exceed the number of inputs used for other global analyses of environmental suitability for disease transmission (e.g., dengue [27] and leishmaniasis [28]).
195.Rationale: The model and index are specific to onchocerciasis environmental suitability (as stated: "The BRT method models environmental suitability of onchocerciasis transmission as a suitability index ..."). The passage only cites dengue as an example of other disease suitability analyses, not as a dataset to combine with this model. There is no support in the text for combining the onchocerciasis suitability index with dengue prevalence data to identify multi-disease risk areas.

195.Option: The environmental suitability index should be used to classify IUs as either endemic or non-endemic based on a threshold of 0.71, with those above the threshold eligible for immediate MDA initiation.
Evidence: The ROC analysis identified a mean prediction of ≥0·71, best agreed with the location of occurrences, and was used to classify IUs as environmentally suitable.

we do not recommend the model results be used to exclude any location from mapping to determine program eligibility for MDA or other interventions, but rather to use the model as a tool for prioritization and comparison alongside other data sources.

Surveys of onchocerciasis prevalence would be warranted in these IUs to determine if MDA with ivermectin should continue, or else risk the reductions in onchocerciasis prevalence achieved through LF program interventions may be lost owing to interruption of MDA.
195.Rationale: Although the authors used a 0.71 threshold to classify IUs as "environmentally suitable" ("The ROC analysis identified a mean prediction of ≥0·71 ..."), they explicitly caution against using the model alone to determine program eligibility for MDA: "we do not recommend the model results be used to ... determine program eligibility for MDA ... but rather to use the model as a tool for prioritization." The passage also indicates surveys are needed to decide on MDA, so immediate MDA initiation solely based on the threshold is not supported.

195.Option: The environmental suitability index reflects the current prevalence of onchocerciasis in a population and should be used to compare transmission intensity across IUs.
Evidence: The primary limitation of this analysis is that we predict mean values of environmental suitability as an index, a measure not directly comparable to other quantities.

This analysis can only indicate how similar a location may be (relative to the covariates included in the analysis) to other locations where onchocerciasis has previously been detected; it does not predict the magnitude of infection.
195.Rationale: The authors explicitly state the index is not a measure of prevalence or magnitude of infection and is not directly comparable to quantities such as transmission intensity. Instead it indicates ecological similarity to known occurrence locations. Therefore it should not be used as a direct measure to compare current prevalence or transmission intensity across IUs.

195.Option: The environmental suitability index represents the probability of onchocerciasis transmission occurring in a given area and should be used to determine which IUs are eligible for mass drug administration.
Evidence: The BRT method models environmental suitability of onchocerciasis transmission as a suitability index (from 0 to 1) based on the values of environmental covariates at the locations corresponding to occurrence inputs.
The posterior probability (%) of an IU including a location that exceeds the 0·71 threshold used to identify areas of suitability is estimated from the 100 BRT bootstraps.

we do not recommend the model results be used to exclude any location from mapping to determine program eligibility for MDA or other interventions, but rather to use the model as a tool for prioritization and comparison alongside other data sources.
195.Rationale: While the model outputs a suitability index (and bootstrapped posterior probabilities for exceeding the threshold), this reflects environmental suitability/relative similarity rather than a direct, empirical probability of current transmission. Crucially, the authors advise the model should not be used alone to determine MDA eligibility but rather to prioritize where to conduct mapping and surveys. Thus the statement that it should be used to determine eligibility for MDA is not supported.

195.Option: The environmental suitability index should be interpreted as a relative measure of ecological similarity to known transmission zones, not as a direct indicator of current infection risk, and should guide prioritization of mapping efforts rather than determine eligibility for intervention.
Evidence: The BRT method models environmental suitability of onchocerciasis transmission as a suitability index (from 0 to 1) based on the values of environmental covariates at the locations corresponding to occurrence inputs.

This analysis can only indicate how similar a location may be (relative to the covariates included in the analysis) to other locations where onchocerciasis has previously been detected; it does not predict the magnitude of infection.

we do not recommend the model results be used to exclude any location from mapping to determine program eligibility for MDA or other interventions, but rather to use the model as a tool for prioritization and comparison alongside other data sources.

These results suggest that a large proportion of IUs considered for elimination mapping surveys as environmentally similar to areas for which onchocerciasis has been previously detected. These results suggest that a large proportion of IUs considered for mapping might be of lower priority for survey implementation...
195.Rationale: The passage repeatedly characterizes the index as an environmental suitability measure (relative ecological similarity) and explicitly warns it does not predict infection magnitude. The authors recommend using the model to prioritize and target mapping/survey efforts (not as a substitute for primary data collection or as the sole determinant of MDA eligibility). Therefore interpreting the index as a relative guide for prioritizing mapping, rather than a direct indicator of current infection risk or an automatic trigger for intervention, is consistent with the authors' guidance.

196.Option: Since there was no significant difference in age, sex, or hospitalization rates between patients from the excluded hospital and those who participated, the exclusion does not introduce meaningful bias and the findings remain valid for the broader population
Evidence: one institute, which accounted for 36.5% of all hospitalized cases in Dourados, refused to participate.
There was no significant difference in age, sex or hospitalization between patients from this hospital and those who participated.
We assume the possibility of selection bias due to the refusal of 1 hospital to participate in the study (n = 185).
196.Rationale: Although the authors report 'no significant difference in age, sex or hospitalization' between the excluded hospital and participants, the excluded institute represented a large fraction (36.5%) of all hospitalized cases. The authors themselves note the possibility of selection bias due to that refusal. Therefore it is not correct to conclude the exclusion 'does not introduce meaningful bias' — the large size of the missing hospital creates a substantial risk to generalizability despite similar measured baseline characteristics.

196.Option: The exclusion of the hospital introduces only a minor limitation because the study used a retrospective cross-sectional design, which is inherently robust to missing data from a single institution
Evidence: This study included a retrospective survey with a cross-sectional design, and we used a bottom-up approach to determine the direct medical costs of the hospitalization of dengue cases
one institute, which accounted for 36.5% of all hospitalized cases in Dourados, refused to participate.
We assume the possibility of selection bias due to the refusal of 1 hospital to participate in the study (n = 185).
196.Rationale: A retrospective cross-sectional design does not make the study 'inherently robust' to missing data from a major institution. Missing a hospital that accounted for over one third of cases can introduce selection bias and affect estimates (costs, adherence), and the authors explicitly acknowledge this possibility. Therefore the statement that exclusion is only a minor limitation due to the design is not supported.

196.Option: The exclusion of the hospital is unlikely to affect the findings because the study already accounted for differences in health care systems, and the excluded hospital was a private institution with similar practices to the others
Evidence: We stratified the dengue costs by cost type, financing source, sociodemographic characteristics, age group and outcomes (hospitalization and/or platelet transfusion with or without use of the WHO criteria).
one institute, which accounted for 36.5% of all hospitalized cases in Dourados, refused to participate.
The cost analyses indicated that the cost in the private sector was 280% higher compared with the cost in the public sector.
196.Rationale: While the study did stratify costs by health care system, the passage does not state that the excluded hospital was a private institution or that its practices were similar to the participating hospitals. Given that private vs public costs differ markedly (private costs 280% higher), exclusion of a large hospital of unknown type could materially affect findings. There is no textual evidence that the excluded hospital was private with similar practices, so the option is unsupported.

196.Option: The exclusion of a major hospital that accounted for over a third of all cases introduces substantial risk of selection bias, potentially undermining the generalizability of cost and adherence findings despite similar baseline characteristics
Evidence: one institute, which accounted for 36.5% of all hospitalized cases in Dourados, refused to participate.
There was no significant difference in age, sex or hospitalization between patients from this hospital and those who participated.
We assume the possibility of selection bias due to the refusal of 1 hospital to participate in the study (n = 185).
The cost analyses indicated that the cost in the private sector was 280% higher compared with the cost in the public sector.
196.Rationale: The excluded institute represented a large share (36.5%) of hospitalized cases, which creates substantial potential for selection bias and limits external validity. Even though measured baseline demographics were similar, unmeasured differences (case mix, management practices, adherence to WHO criteria, billing patterns, public/private mix) could materially affect cost and adherence estimates. The authors explicitly note the possibility of selection bias, and the documented marked cost differences between public and private care further support concern about generalizability.

196.Option: The exclusion of the hospital does not affect the validity of the cost estimates because the study used a fixed payment system (US $135.4 per hospitalization) that standardizes costs across institutions, minimizing the impact of missing data
Evidence: Therefore, the SUS transfers a fixed amount to accredited hospitals using Hospital Admission Authorization (Autorização de Internação Hospitalar - AIH), or US $135.4 per hospitalization for dengue cases [26], [27].

This amount includes the costs of daily hospitalization, medications, supplies, laboratory tests and X-rays and remains constant, regardless of the number of hospitalization days or tests required. However, additional fee is paid when computed tomography (CT); magnetic resonance imaging (MRI); or other special procedures, such as a transfusion or ICU use, are required. These additional costs may contribute to the variations in the total amount paid by the SUS to the hospitals for each case [26], [27].
However, this amount does not represent the real cost to the SUS of a patient with dengue and demonstrates underfunding and inadequate transfer by the SUS.
The cost analyses indicated that the cost in the private sector was 280% higher compared with the cost in the public sector.
196.Rationale: Although SUS transfers a fixed reimbursement per dengue hospitalization (US $135.4), that does not reflect actual costs (authors state it 'does not represent the real cost' and additional payments and procedures vary). Moreover, private-sector costs are much higher. Therefore missing a hospital can still bias cost estimates; fixed payments do not standardize true costs across institutions, so the option is incorrect.

197.Option: A persistent increase in NiV antigen-positive NK cells from days 6 to 16 post-infection, suggesting early viral control and immune activation
Evidence: Interestingly, the surviving animal had the most robust and persistent increase in NiV antigen positive NK cells (Fig 11E).
approximately 15% of the NK cells identified in the survivor were NiV antigen positive from 6–16 days post-infection (Fig 11E).
While the presence of viral antigen is intriguing, this does not mean these cell populations are infected and that virus is replicating.
197.Rationale: While the passage documents a persistent increase in NiV antigen–positive NK cells from days 6–16 in the survivor, the authors caution that detection of viral antigen in NK cells does not necessarily indicate productive infection or that this feature represents protective viral control. The text explicitly notes the presence of antigen is "intriguing" but "does not mean these cell populations are infected and that virus is replicating," and elsewhere the discussion raises the possibility that antigen-positive NK cells might reflect permissiveness to infection rather than direct evidence of early viral control. Therefore, although NK activation/proliferation is associated with the survivor, the specific observation of antigen-positive NK cells alone is not a clear, actionable correlate of protection to leverage for countermeasure development.

197.Option: A marked increase in CD8bright effector T cells during convalescence, consistent with clearance of residual infected cells
Evidence: The population of CD8bright effector T cells increased markedly during convalescence of the survivor as one might expect for clearance of residual infected cells.

The remaining animal, the survivor, had a nominal increase in the CD8bright population over the acute phase of disease and the population increased further as the animal convalesced (Fig 6D and 6F; S1 Table).
The survivor developed a classic antibody response and T cell response indicative of protective adaptive immunity.
197.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes a marked increase in CD8bright effector T cells during convalescence in the surviving animal and links this pattern to clearance of residual infected cells. This is a direct adaptive cellular immune response associated with recovery. Because CD8 effector responses can mediate clearance of infected cells and are measurable, they represent a plausible immunological correlate of protection that could inform medical countermeasures (for example, vaccines that induce cytotoxic T-lymphocyte responses). The authors also frame the survivor's T cell response as part of "protective adaptive immunity," supporting this option as a valid correlate.

197.Option: A coordinated surge in pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-1RA, IL-2, IL-4, IL-5, IL-8, IL-15, TNFα, MIP-1α, MIP-1β, TGFα, GM-CSF) between days 10 and 16 post-infection
Evidence: This animal had marked increases in several cytokines and chemokines between days 10 and 16 post-infection that correlate with increases in IgG production and improved clinical health.
These cytokines included IL-1β, IL-1RA, IL-2, IL-4, IL-5, IL-8, IL-15, TNFα, MIP-1α (CCL3), MIP-1β (CCL4), TGFα and GM-CSF (Figs 2 and 3).
The increases in IL-2, IL-4, IL-5, GM-CSF and MIP-1β are all potentially associated with the transition from innate to adaptive immunity.
197.Rationale: The passage documents a clear, coordinated cytokine/chemokine surge in the survivor during days 10–16 that correlated with rising IgG and improved clinical status. The authors interpret several of these mediators (IL-2, IL-4, IL-5, GM-CSF, MIP-1β) as associated with transitioning from innate to adaptive immunity. Because a timely shift to adaptive immunity was highlighted as likely important for survival, this temporally coordinated cytokine response is a credible immunological feature that indicates development of protective immunity and therefore represents a potential correlate to exploit in countermeasure development.

197.Option: A rapid and sustained increase in B cell populations beginning at day 10 post-infection, followed by a marked rise in Ki67+ B cells after day 12
Evidence: In all six animals the B cell populations decreased over the course of the acute disease (defined as 0–12 dpi) with populations starting to recover in four of the six animals beginning at 10 dpi (Fig 4).

The animal that survived the infection had an immune response that was different from the other five animals in many respects. Specifically, these differences were in the kinetics of the CD4+ and CD8+ T cell responses over the acute phase of disease, and an earlier increase in its B cell population after an initial decrease.
the percentage of Ki67+ B cells increased markedly beginning on day 12 post-infection (Fig 8A).
The survivor developed a classic antibody response ... indicative of protective adaptive immunity.
197.Rationale: The passage describes an earlier rebound of B cell numbers in the surviving animal starting around day 10 and a marked increase in proliferating (Ki67+) B cells beginning day 12, concurrent with the appearance of virus-specific IgM/IgG and neutralizing activity. The authors link the survivor's earlier B cell recovery and antibody production to a likely competitive advantage for survival. Because B cell activation and ensuing neutralizing antibody production are classic correlates of protection and are directly actionable for vaccine or antibody-based countermeasures, this observation represents a strong and biologically plausible correlate of protection.

197.Option: Sustained neutralizing antibody titers that developed after day 23 post-infection and persisted through convalescence
Evidence: no antibody response until 10 dpi at which time one of the animals, the survivor (8197), had evidence of IgM and neutralizing antibody titers (Fig 1A and 1B).
The IgM titers of 8197 peaked at 14 dpi when a NiV-specific IgG response became apparent with evidence of NiV-specific IgG1 antibodies...
The neutralizing antibody titer of 8197 plateaued at 23 dpi where it remained until the end of the study.
197.Rationale: The passage reports that neutralizing antibody titers were already detectable by 10 dpi and that the neutralizing titer "plateaued at 23 dpi where it remained until the end of the study." The option's statement that neutralizing titers "developed after day 23" is inconsistent with these facts. Although sustained neutralizing antibodies are indeed a plausible correlate of protection, the timing described in this option is incorrect relative to the passage, so the option as worded is not supported.

198.Option: The vaccine is produced in Vero-G cells, which are engineered to express Gn/Gc, allowing for amplification while preventing the generation of infectious particles
Evidence: we generated Vero-G cells stably expressing MP-12 Gn/Gc, and found the expression levels of Gn/Gc in Vero-G cells to be roughly one-fourth of the levels for MP-12-infected Vero cells at 12 h post-inoculation (p.i.) (Fig. 3A).
scMP-12 amplified efficiently in Vero-G cells and stably retained the introduced mutations in ten serial passages in this cell line under three different experimental conditions.

Inoculation of the amplified scMP-12 into naïve cells results in viral RNA synthesis, expression of viral proteins, including L, N and the fusion-defective Gn/Gc, and production of noninfectious VLPs containing the fusion-defective Gn/Gc.
198.Rationale: While the passage documents that Vero-G cells (engineered to express Gn/Gc) are used to amplify scMP-12, the prevention of infectious-particle generation is attributed to the scMP-12 single-cycle/fusion-defective design rather than merely the fact the vaccine is produced in Vero-G cells. The quoted text shows Vero-G cells permit amplification, and that scMP-12 in naïve cells produces noninfectious VLPs; it does not support the claim that production in Vero-G alone is what prevents infectious particle generation. Therefore the option overstates the role of Vero-G production and is not the most direct supporting feature.

198.Option: The vaccine undergoes single-cycle replication in host cells, producing noninfectious virus-like particles that elicit immune responses without enabling systemic spread or neuroinvasion
Evidence: To develop a safe and immunogenic RVF vaccine, we have generated a novel, single-cycle replicable MP-12 (scMP-12), which does not cause systemic infection in immunized hosts, while resulting in expression of all viral structural proteins and production of noninfectious, virus-like particles (VLPs) in naïve cells infected with scMP-12.
The scMP-12 did not show any sign of neurovirulence after intracranial inoculation into suckling mice, demonstrating its safety.
scMP-12-immunized mice elicited neutralizing antibodies and were efficiently protected from wt RVFV challenge by inhibiting wt RVFV replication in various organs and viremia.
198.Rationale: The passage explicitly states scMP-12 is single-cycle and produces noninfectious VLPs in infected naïve cells, and that it "does not cause systemic infection" and showed no neurovirulence after intracranial inoculation. Those features directly explain how the vaccine can elicit protective immunity (antigen expression and VLPs to induce neutralizing antibodies) while avoiding systemic spread or neuroinvasion.

198.Option: The vaccine is genetically stable and does not undergo recombination with host cell RNA, ensuring that no infectious virus is generated during replication
Evidence: Sequence analysis of the PCR products of scMP-12 M RNA showed that scMP-12 retained the introduced mutations after 10 passages under the three different conditions.
These results demonstrate that scMP-12 stably retained the introduced mutations.

The absence of infectious virus after 10 serial passages of scMP-12 in Vero-G cells under three different conditions demonstrated that homologous RNA recombination that can eliminate the mutations in scMP-12 M RNA did not occur between replicating scMP-12 M RNA and expressed mRNA encoding Gn/Gc in Vero-G cells, further indicating the utility and safety of Vero-G cells for preparation of the scMP-12-based vaccine.
198.Rationale: The passage provides strong evidence that scMP-12 is genetically stable and that recombination events eliminating the engineered mutations did not occur during serial passage in Vero-G cells. However, the option asserts absence of recombination with 'host cell RNA' (a broader claim) and frames genetic stability as the primary reason for lack of infectious virus. Although stability supports safety, the most direct feature accounting for both immunogenicity and lack of systemic spread/neuroinvasion is the single-cycle/fusion-defective design (see quotes in Option 1). Thus this option, while supported in part, is not the most direct explanation as stated and overgeneralizes the recombination claim.

198.Option: The vaccine induces neutralizing antibodies that recognize the Gn/Gc glycoproteins, which are essential for viral entry and are targeted by protective immunity in natural infection
Evidence: RVFV is considered to be serologically monotypic ..., and humoral immunity, particularly neutralizing antibodies that recognize Gn/Gc, is important for protection [12]–[20].
scMP-12-immunized mice elicited neutralizing antibodies and were efficiently protected from wt RVFV challenge by inhibiting wt RVFV replication in various organs and viremia.
The mean PRNT80 titers of the mice immunized with 105 PFU of scMP-12 and 105 PFU of VRP were 1∶238 and 1∶38, respectively; the difference in the PRNT80 titers was statistically significant.
198.Rationale: The passage clearly links neutralizing antibodies to protection and shows scMP-12 elicits such antibodies and protects mice. However, this option addresses the immunogenicity portion (ability to elicit protective immunity) but does not by itself explain the lack of systemic spread or neuroinvasion. The question asks for the feature that most directly supports both eliciting protection and avoiding systemic/neuroinvasive disease; neutralizing-antibody induction addresses protection but not the safety mechanism (single-cycle replication and fusion defect). Therefore this option is incomplete for the question's combined criterion.

198.Option: The vaccine is unable to replicate in naïve cells, preventing the production of infectious virus and thereby eliminating the risk of neuroinvasion
Evidence: Inoculation of the amplified scMP-12 into naïve cells results in viral RNA synthesis, expression of viral proteins, including L, N and the fusion-defective Gn/Gc, and production of noninfectious VLPs containing the fusion-defective Gn/Gc.
