223.Option: The fact that pigs spend most of their time within 50 meters of their home supports the hypothesis that transmission occurs through direct contact with tapeworm carriers rather than environmental contamination
Evidence: A GPS tracking study of pigs in rural Peru found that pigs spent an average of 70% of their time within 50 meters of their residence and interacted with human defecation areas nearly 30 minutes per day inside these 50 meters rings (compared to just 7 minutes per day outside of 50 meters) [26].
Based on these findings, we propose that 50 meter rings accurately represent T. solium transmission foci in endemic areas of rural Peru.
The idea that 50 meters is a critical distance at which pigs are exposed to increased risk of T. solium infection in this region is consistent with our understanding of pig range and behavior.
223.Rationale: The quoted passages do show pigs spend most time within 50 m of home, but they explicitly link that behavior to interaction with human defecation areas and to localized transmission foci — i.e., environmental contamination (fecal contamination) — not to ‘‘direct contact with tapeworm carriers.’' The option asserts direct contact with tapeworm carriers rather than environmental contamination, which is not supported by the passage. The passage emphasizes pig contact with fecal contamination and environmental transmission near households as the mechanism, so the option is incorrect.

223.Option: The strong association between infection and proximity to tapeworm carriers, even after adjusting for pig age, indicates that environmental contamination is the only possible transmission route
Evidence: After adjusting for pig age, pigs living less than 50 meters from a human tapeworm carrier were 4.56 times (95% CI: 1.33, 15.6) more likely to be infected with at least one cyst than pigs living more than 500 meters from a tapeworm carrier.

Although distance to human tapeworm carriers was an important predictor for T. solium infection among pigs, many infected pigs in our study did not reside in close proximity to a tapeworm carrier. In fact, only 27% (12 out of 44) of the infected pigs in this study lived within 50 meters of a tapeworm carrier, and some infected pigs lived more than 1 km from it’s the nearest identified tapeworm carrier.

It is also possible that pigs appearing distant from tapeworm carriers were infected through egg dispersion mechanisms, such as dung beetles and flies, which have been identified as possible mechanical vectors capable of dispersing T. solium eggs over long distances [27–31].
223.Rationale: While the passage documents a strong adjusted association between proximity and infection (supporting localized environmental contamination), it does not claim that environmental contamination is the only possible route. The authors explicitly note many infected pigs were not near identified carriers and propose long-distance mechanical dispersal (flies, dung beetles) and incomplete detection of carriers as alternative explanations. Thus the option's exclusive claim ('only possible transmission route') is not supported.

223.Option: A sharp decline in infection prevalence beyond 50 meters, coupled with pigs' high activity in this zone, indicates that transmission occurs primarily through localized environmental contamination near human dwellings
Evidence: The prevalence of T. solium cysticercosis (at least one viable cyst on necropsy) was greatest among pigs living within 50 meters of a tapeworm carrier, and decreased proportionally at greater distances. The prevalence of at least one viable cyst was 15.6% (12 out of 77) at < 50 meters from a tapeworm carrier, 8.3% (27 out of 325) between 50 and 500 meters, and 4.4% (5 out of 113) at > 500 meters (p < 0.01 for trend)

A GPS tracking study of pigs in rural Peru found that pigs spent an average of 70% of their time within 50 meters of their residence and interacted with human defecation areas nearly 30 minutes per day inside these 50 meters rings (compared to just 7 minutes per day outside of 50 meters) [26].
Based on these findings, we propose that 50 meter rings accurately represent T. solium transmission foci in endemic areas of rural Peru.
223.Rationale: This option correctly synthesizes the two key patterns in the data that most strongly support localized environmental contamination: (1) a marked, statistically significant decline in prevalence with increasing distance (high prevalence <50 m, lower at 50–500 m, and lowest >500 m), and (2) behavioral data showing pigs spend most of their time and contact with human defecation areas within 50 m of the home. Together these findings indicate transmission is concentrated near households (localized environmental contamination), which is the strongest support provided by the study for localized rather than primarily long-distance dispersal.

223.Option: The absence of infection in pigs located more than 500 meters from a tapeworm carrier suggests that T. solium eggs cannot survive or remain infectious beyond this distance in the environment
Evidence: The prevalence of at least one viable cyst was 15.6% (12 out of 77) at < 50 meters from a tapeworm carrier, 8.3% (27 out of 325) between 50 and 500 meters, and 4.4% (5 out of 113) at > 500 meters (p < 0.01 for trend)

Although distance to human tapeworm carriers was an important predictor for T. solium infection among pigs, many infected pigs in our study did not reside in close proximity to a tapeworm carrier. In fact, only 27% (12 out of 44) of the infected pigs in this study lived within 50 meters of a tapeworm carrier, and some infected pigs lived more than 1 km from it’s the nearest identified tapeworm carrier.

It is also possible that pigs appearing distant from tapeworm carriers were infected through egg dispersion mechanisms, such as dung beetles and flies, which have been identified as possible mechanical vectors capable of dispersing T. solium eggs over long distances [27–31].
223.Rationale: The premise of this option is false: the study did not report an absence of infection beyond 500 m — prevalence at >500 m was 4.4%, not zero. Moreover, the authors acknowledge that some infected pigs lived >1 km from identified carriers and propose long-distance mechanical dispersion (flies, dung beetles) as plausible mechanisms. Therefore the data do not support concluding that eggs cannot survive or remain infectious beyond 500 m.

223.Option: The lack of a significant association between infection and distance beyond 50 meters suggests that long-distance dispersal mechanisms are not involved in transmission
Evidence: Pigs living more than 50 meters from a tapeworm carrier, including pigs living between 50 and 100 meters from a tapeworm carrier, did not have an increased odds of infection at any cyst burden analyzed.

Although distance to human tapeworm carriers was an important predictor for T. solium infection among pigs, many infected pigs in our study did not reside in close proximity to a tapeworm carrier. In fact, only 27% (12 out of 44) of the infected pigs in this study lived within 50 meters of a tapeworm carrier, and some infected pigs lived more than 1 km from it’s the nearest identified tapeworm carrier.

It is also possible that pigs appearing distant from tapeworm carriers were infected through egg dispersion mechanisms, such as dung beetles and flies, which have been identified as possible mechanical vectors capable of dispersing T. solium eggs over long distances [27–31].
223.Rationale: While the study found no significant increased odds of infection for pigs >50 m from carriers (supporting localized risk), that non-significance does not exclude involvement of long-distance dispersal. The authors explicitly report infected pigs far from identified carriers and propose mechanical vectors and incomplete detection of carriers as possible explanations. Thus the lack of a significant association beyond 50 m does not prove long-distance dispersal mechanisms are not involved, so the option is incorrect.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is uniquely associated with increased effector memory CD4 T cells, a subset highly susceptible to HIV, in multivariable analysis, unlike other helminth species
Evidence: W. bancrofti infection was associated with significantly increased frequencies of effector memory (CD27negCD45ROpos) CD4 T cells, in univariable (mean 24.1% versus 20.0%, p = 0.0096) as well as multivariable analysis (Tables 3 and S5). This was not observed for other analyzed helminth species.
These effector memory CD4 T cells were positively correlated with the activation status (Spearman’s rho = 0.617, p<0.001, Fig 5).

we had previously shown in the same cohort that both CD27 negative effector memory CD4 T cells and HLA-DR+ memory CD4 T cells express significantly higher levels of the HIV co-receptor CCR5 compared to CD27+ central memory or naive CD4 T cells [21], which may facilitate better HIV entry into these specific cell subsets.
224.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that W. bancrofti infection is associated with significantly increased effector memory (CD27negCD45ROpos) CD4 T cells in both univariable and multivariable analyses and that this pattern was not seen with other helminth species. It also links these effector memory cells to higher activation and to higher CCR5 expression (from prior work in the same cohort), making them highly relevant HIV targets. Together, these statements provide direct evidence that W. bancrofti uniquely increases a CD4 subset particularly susceptible to HIV, supporting the conclusion that W. bancrofti contributes uniquely to HIV susceptibility beyond general helminth co-infection.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is associated with increased eosinophil counts, which are known to correlate with systemic immune activation and may contribute to HIV susceptibility
Evidence: We found a median number of 130x103/μl eosinophils in helminth free individuals compared to 400x103/μl in W. bancrofti-infected individuals and 260x103/μl in participants harboring other helminths.
As expected, there was a difference in eosinophil count between helminth free individuals and participants with one of the diagnosed helminth species.
224.Rationale: While the passage shows W. bancrofti-infected individuals had higher median eosinophil counts than helminth-free individuals (and higher than the combined other-helminth group), the manuscript does not present eosinophils as the mechanistic link to HIV susceptibility, nor does it state that eosinophil counts correlate with systemic T cell activation or HIV acquisition. The authors emphasize CD4 T cell activation and effector-memory cells as the plausible mechanism. Thus, although eosinophils are elevated, the passage does not support eosinophil counts as the strongest or a demonstrated mediator of increased HIV susceptibility.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is associated with increased expression of CCR5 on CD4 T cells, which directly facilitates HIV entry and explains the increased susceptibility
Evidence: Addressing the expression of HIV entry receptor CCR5, no difference was found regarding the percentage of CCR5 positive CD4 T cells, CCR5 positive regulatory CD4 T cells, or the mean fluorescence intensity of CCR5 on memory CD4 T cells between the different helminth infections (Tables 3, S6, S7 and S8).

we had previously shown in the same cohort that both CD27 negative effector memory CD4 T cells and HLA-DR+ memory CD4 T cells express significantly higher levels of the HIV co-receptor CCR5 compared to CD27+ central memory or naive CD4 T cells [21], which may facilitate better HIV entry into these specific cell subsets.
224.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports no difference in CCR5 expression on CD4 T cells between helminth infection groups in this study, so W. bancrofti is not shown here to be associated with increased CCR5 expression overall. The authors instead invoke prior evidence that the particular effector-memory and activated CD4 subsets (which are increased with W. bancrofti) tend to express higher CCR5; however, the direct claim that W. bancrofti increases CCR5 on CD4 T cells is not supported by the study's data. Therefore the option is incorrect.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection leads to a higher overall burden of helminth co-infection, which may drive increased immune activation and thus HIV susceptibility
Evidence: Almost all W. bancrofti-infected subjects (32 of 33) were also infected with other helminths (Table 1) impeding an evaluation of only monoinfected individuals. A mean of 2.3 helminths species were detected in W. bancrofti-infected individuals.

Therefore we compared the effect of W. bancrofti on T cell activation not only with the group of helminth free individuals, but in addition with the “background” infection of the combined helminth group to distinguish the effect of W. bancrofti from an overall helminth influence on immune activation.

The association between increased frequencies of activated HLA-DRpos and HLA-DRpos/CD38pos and effector memory (CD27negCD45ROpos) CD4 T cells in peripheral blood and W. bancrofti infection was independent of other host and environmental factors, suggesting a causal link between these two factors.

we clearly showed that the described CD4 T cell activation in W. bancrofti- (but also in A. lumbricoides and T. trichiura) -infected individuals was independent of concomitant infection with other helminths.
224.Rationale: Although W. bancrofti-infected individuals commonly had multiple helminth co-infections, the investigators explicitly compared W. bancrofti cases to a background group with similar other-helminth composition and adjusted for other helminth infections in multivariable models. The association of immune activation with W. bancrofti remained independent of concomitant infections, arguing against the idea that a higher burden of co-infection alone explains the observed increase in immune activation and HIV susceptibility. Therefore this option does not represent the strongest support for a unique contribution of W. bancrofti.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is associated with increased HLA-DRpos CD8 T cells, which are also targets for HIV replication, suggesting a similar mechanism to CD4 T cell activation
Evidence: Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD8 T cell were increased in subjects with W. bancrofti (mean 29.5%) infection compared to subjects without filarial infection (mean 20.8%). However, this difference was not significant after adjusting for study site and other confounding factors in the multivariable analysis (Tables 3 and S2).

In addition we measured increased percentage of HLA-DRpos/CD38pos CD8 T cells in W. bancrofti-infected individuals, however, there is no evidence that CD8 T cell may contribute to increased HIV susceptibility.
224.Rationale: The passage notes an unadjusted increase in HLA-DRpos CD8 T cells with W. bancrofti, but that difference did not remain significant after multivariable adjustment. Crucially, the authors state there is no evidence that CD8 T cells contribute to increased HIV susceptibility. The study emphasizes activated and effector-memory CD4 T cells as the plausible mediators. Thus the option is not supported as the primary factor explaining unique contribution to HIV susceptibility.

225.Option: A rapid decline in platelet count observed between day 1 and day 3 of illness, as it reflects early immune-mediated thrombocytopenia and predicts progression to DHF
Evidence: All spectra of dengue also had lower median peripheral platelet count during the later days of illness, although the median value in DF and UF did not meet the WHO's criterion for thrombocytopenia, i.e. lower than 100,000/mm3.
Moreover, the level of platelet count showed reverse correlation with the disease severity, i.e. highest in UF and lowest in DHF.

The median platelet count in DHF was significantly lower than the other 2 groups during day 3–6 of illness, while DF had significantly lower median platelet count compared to UF only during day 3–5 of illness.

This study shows that a decrease in peripheral platelet count occurs not only in DHF, but also in DF and UF, with a respectively less degree. It confirms that the cut off level for platelet count of 100,000/mm3 is appropriate for differentiation between DHF and non-DHF.
225.Rationale: The passage documents that platelet counts fall and that lower platelet counts (particularly during days 3–6) correlate with greater severity (DHF). However, it does not state that a rapid decline specifically between day 1 and day 3 predicts progression, and platelet decreases occur across spectra (DF and UF too). Thus the passage does not provide direct evidence that an early rapid decline in days 1–3 is the single strongest early warning sign; platelet nadirs are described later (day 3–6) and are useful for differentiation but not presented as the earliest trigger for heightened vigilance.

225.Option: Persistent or increasing gastrointestinal symptoms (anorexia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain) beyond day 2 of illness
Evidence: The exceptions are for anorexia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain and diarrhea that tended to increase in prevalence in DHF during day 3–5 of illness.

It was also revealed that most symptoms were more common in DHF and, specifically, DHF cases had significantly higher prevalence of anorexia, nausea/vomiting and abdominal pain during day 3–6 and diarrhea during day 4–6 of illness (Chi-square test; p<0.05).
It was also revealed that a higher proportion of DHF had prolonged (3 or more days) anorexia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, retro-orbital pain and petechiae.
This finding in this study that DHF cases had higher prevalence of nausea/vomiting and abdominal pain supports this guideline.
225.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies anorexia, nausea/vomiting and abdominal pain as symptoms that increase in prevalence in DHF during days 3–5 and are significantly more common in DHF during days 3–6, and notes prolonged (≥3 days) GI symptoms are more frequent in DHF. The authors highlight these GI signs as supporting WHO warning signs. Therefore persistent or increasing GI symptoms beyond day 2 are supported by the data as an early clinical feature that should prompt heightened vigilance for progression to severe dengue.

225.Option: A positive tourniquet test observed on day 1 of illness, as it indicates early plasma leakage and is a reliable predictor of severe dengue
Evidence: Positive tourniquet test was found in 72% and flushed face was found in approximately half of the dengue infected patients.

some clinical manifestations (e.g. rash, positive tourniquet test) were considered as more specific to DF/DHF and are included in clinical diagnostic criteria for DF. This can explain why these clinical manifestations were more common in DF/DHF.

Table 6 shows the occurrence of selected clinical manifestations and their predictive value for DHF among symptomatic dengue infected cases. It was found that all of these clinical manifestations had low positive predictive value but high negative predictive value for DHF.
225.Rationale: Although the tourniquet test is more common in DF/DHF and is part of diagnostic criteria, the passage notes that selected clinical manifestations (which would include tourniquet test) have low positive predictive value for DHF. The authors do not state that a positive tourniquet test on day 1 is a reliable early predictor of severe disease, so this option overstates the evidence.

225.Option: The occurrence of drowsiness or lethargy during day 2–5 of illness, as it is a common symptom in both mild and severe dengue and may indicate early neurological involvement
Evidence: Conversely, drowsiness/lethargy was significantly more common in UF during day 2–5 of illness.

On the other hand, we found that the presence of lethargy or drowsiness in the first few days of illness may not necessarily indicate severe dengue infection. The significance of drowsiness/lethargy needs to be further defined.
225.Rationale: The passage indicates drowsiness/lethargy was actually more common in undifferentiated fever (UF) during days 2–5 and explicitly cautions that lethargy or drowsiness in the first few days may not indicate severe dengue. Therefore this feature is not supported by the study as a strong early indicator requiring heightened vigilance for progression to severe dengue.

225.Option: The presence of rhinorrhea during the first 2 days of illness, as it may indicate a co-infection with a respiratory virus that could worsen dengue outcomes
Evidence: It is interesting that rhinorrhea was commonly found in this study.
Whether this symptom is one manifestation of dengue infection or is caused by co-incidental respiratory infection is still unclear.
However, it has been suggested that dengue infection should be included in the differential diagnosis of acute infection of the upper respiratory tract [14].
225.Rationale: The passage notes rhinorrhea was common but explicitly states uncertainty whether it represents dengue itself or a co-incidental respiratory infection. There is no evidence presented that rhinorrhea predicts progression to severe dengue or worsens outcomes; thus it is not supported as a clinical feature that should trigger heightened vigilance for severe disease.

226.Option: Pigs serve as the primary reservoir host for T. penetrans in this setting, given their high infection prevalence and intensity, strong correlation with human cases, and significant bidirectional association in multivariate analysis
Evidence: The study revealed that the prevalence of animal tungiasis was high and that the disease prevalence and parasite loads in humans and animals correlated. Pigs were identified as the most important domestic animal hosts for T. penetrans.
Pigs had the highest parasite load followed by goats and dogs: median = 8 lesions (inter-quartile range (IQR) = 3–30; range of 1–246 lesions per pig)
The prevalence of human tungiasis at household and village levels correlated strongly with the number of lesions in pigs at the respective levels (rho = 0.5, p<0.0001; rho = 0.8, p = 0.002).
Pig tungiasis occurred in strong association with dog tungiasis (OR = 7.4, 95% CI 1.5–36.9; p = 0.02) and human tungiasis (OR = 7.0, 95% CI 3.4–14.7; p<0.0001)
In multivariate analysis, presence of animal tungiasis in households was strongly influenced by presence of human tungiasis (OR = 6.5, p < 0.0001) and presence of pigs (OR = 5.9, p = 0.0002).
226.Rationale: Multiple lines of evidence in the study identify pigs as the major reservoirs: the authors explicitly state pigs were identified as the most important domestic hosts; pigs had the highest parasite loads; pig infection measures correlated strongly with human infection at household and village levels; and statistical models show strong associations between pig presence/pig tungiasis and human tungiasis (multivariate ORs). These findings support the inference that pigs serve as the primary reservoir host in this setting.

226.Option: Pigs are the primary reservoir host because they are the only species with a significant association between infection and the presence of other ectoparasites, suggesting shared environmental risk factors
Evidence: The reasons for the association between T. penetrans and other pig ectoparasites such as lice in this study are not known as the ecological interactions between these parasites have not been studied yet.

For households with pigs, there was no significant difference in the proportion of those with infected pigs between those that practiced ectoparasite control for pigs and those that did not (6 out of 20 vs. 48 out of 135, p = 0.42).

Although, the total number of sand flea lesions per infected pig was lower in pigs treated with ectoparasiticides than the untreated (treated median 6.5, IQR = 3–13 vs. untreated median 8, IQR = 3–39), the difference was not significant (p = 0.34).

The final [pig] model... included the variables; “presence of other poultry”, “dog tungiasis” and “pig herd size” which all increased the odds of pig tungiasis although their effects were all not significant.
226.Rationale: The option claims a significant association between pig tungiasis and other ectoparasites and that pigs are uniquely characterized by this. The passage indicates an observed association exists but its reasons are unknown and the study did not find significant effects of ectoparasite control or other ectoparasite variables on pig tungiasis; indeed the model variables related to other factors were not significant. Therefore the claimed unique, significant ectoparasite association as the basis for pigs being primary reservoirs is not supported by the text.

226.Option: Pigs are the most important reservoir host for T. penetrans because they have the highest number of lesions per infected animal and are the only species with viable lesions detected in the study
Evidence: Pigs had the highest parasite load followed by goats and dogs: median = 8 lesions (inter-quartile range (IQR) = 3–30; range of 1–246 lesions per pig)
Of the 3357 lesions in pigs, 2243 (66.8%) were viable (Fortaleza stage IIa-IIIb) and 1114 (33.3%) were non-viable (Fortaleza stage IV).
The 20 infected dogs had a total of 53 lesions of which 32 (60.4%) were viable while 21 (39.6%) were non-viable.
Of the total 40 lesions found on the two goat kids, 27 (67.5%) were viable while the other 13 (32.5%) were non-viable.
226.Rationale: While it is true pigs had the highest lesion counts and many viable lesions (supporting their epidemiologic importance), the assertion that pigs were the only species with viable lesions is false: viable lesions were also documented in dogs and goats. Thus the first part of the option (highest lesions) is supported, but the second (only species with viable lesions) is contradicted by the passage, making the option incorrect.

226.Option: Pigs are the primary reservoir host because they are the only species with a significant association between infection prevalence and household size, suggesting greater exposure in larger households
Evidence: The strong association of animal infections with large household sizes is probably also indirect and may be due to the fact that such households have many children below 15 years who constitute the most vulnerable group of humans to T. penetrans.

In multivariate analysis, presence of animal tungiasis in households was strongly influenced by presence of human tungiasis (OR = 6.5, p < 0.0001) and presence of pigs (OR = 5.9, p = 0.0002). In addition, the number of animal species reared in the household (OR = 1.6, p = 0.02) and the size of the homestead (OR = 1.4, p = 0.02) significantly increased the odds detecting animal tungiasis among households.

The odds of households to have pigs infected with T. penetrans were significantly increased if there was also human tungiasis as illustrated in Fig 7B (OR = 2.1, p = 0.001). The final model showed an excellent fit (McFadden pseudo R2 = 0.82).
226.Rationale: The passage discusses household size as a factor associated with animal tungiasis overall, and suggests this association may be indirect (linked to more children). It does not state that pigs uniquely show a significant association with household size. Pig-specific models emphasize association with human tungiasis and pig herd factors, not household size per se. Therefore the claim that pigs are the primary reservoir because they alone show a significant household-size association is not supported.

226.Option: Pigs are the primary reservoir host because they are the only species whose infection intensity correlates with the number of infected humans in the household, indicating a direct transmission pathway
Evidence: The prevalence of human tungiasis at household and village levels correlated strongly with the number of lesions in pigs at the respective levels (rho = 0.5, p<0.0001; rho = 0.8, p = 0.002).

At household level, an increase in human tungiasis intensities corresponded with an increase in the odds of occurrence of animal infections (OR = 1.8 CI 1.4–2.2, p<0.0001) and vice versa (OR = 1.3 CI 1.1–1.4, p < 0.0001).
At household level, the prevalence of tungiasis in the mostly affected animal species strongly correlated with human prevalence (dogs rho = 0.34, p = 0.0002; pigs rho = 0.5, p<0.001).

The study was cross sectional in nature; hence seasonal patterns could not be demonstrated. ... Also the study only established the human tungiasis burden in animal rearing households ... Additionally, it was only possible to demonstrate the association between human and animal tungiasis but causal relationships are difficult to determine.
226.Rationale: Although pig infection intensity does correlate strongly with human infection measures, pigs are not the only species with a correlation (dogs also show correlation, albeit weaker). More importantly, the study is cross-sectional and the authors explicitly caution that causal relationships (a direct transmission pathway) cannot be established from these data. Thus the assertion that pigs are the only species with such a correlation and that this demonstrates direct transmission is not supported by the passage.

227.Option: Use a time-series analysis to assess changes in tsetse presence over the course of the survey period to account for temporal variation in environmental conditions
Evidence: Tsetse data were obtained from a systematic entomological survey conducted from May to June, 2010, to ascertain tsetse presence and abundance.

A residual variogram was constructed to assess the presence of spatial autocorrelation in the model residuals. Autologistic regression was applied to account for the residual spatial autocorrelation [30, 31, 32, 33, 34, 35].
227.Rationale: The question asks about addressing non-independence due to geographic proximity (spatial autocorrelation). The passage describes a single short survey (May–June 2010), not a time series, and specifically identifies spatial autocorrelation as the problem addressed by autologistic (spatial) methods. Time-series analysis addresses temporal variation, not the spatial dependence that is the critical factor here.

227.Option: Match cases and controls on geographic proximity to reduce spatial clustering and ensure balanced comparison between tsetse presence and absence groups
Evidence: A residual variogram was constructed to assess the presence of spatial autocorrelation in the model residuals. Autologistic regression was applied to account for the residual spatial autocorrelation [30, 31, 32, 33, 34, 35].
Since non-spatial models fail to account for the autocorrelation effect, there was a need to apply a spatial model: in this case, autologistic regression.
227.Rationale: The study addresses spatial clustering by explicitly incorporating a spatial autocovariate (autologistic regression). Matching on geographic proximity is not described and would not be the standard approach for spatially continuous presence/absence survey data; moreover, the passage emphasizes using a spatial model (autologistic) to account for residual spatial autocorrelation rather than matching.

227.Option: Apply a non-parametric regression model such as random forest to reduce sensitivity to violations of model assumptions and better capture complex environmental interactions
Evidence: Autologistic regression was applied to account for the residual spatial autocorrelation [30, 31, 32, 33, 34, 35]. This process involves the introduction of a new explanatory variable (autocovariate).

Other methodological approaches can be used to deal with spatially autocorrelated data, such as model-based geostatistics [15], although the fitting of these models and subsequent spatial predictions are very demanding computationally.
227.Rationale: While non-parametric models (e.g., random forest) can capture complex relationships, they do not by themselves resolve spatial autocorrelation (non-independence due to proximity). The passage recommends spatial methods (autologistic, model-based geostatistics) that explicitly model spatial dependence; random forest is not presented as the solution to the spatial non-independence problem described.

227.Option: Account for spatial dependence in the data using a model that incorporates an autocovariate to adjust for neighboring observations
Evidence: A residual variogram was constructed to assess the presence of spatial autocorrelation in the model residuals. Autologistic regression was applied to account for the residual spatial autocorrelation [30, 31, 32, 33, 34, 35].
This process involves the introduction of a new explanatory variable (autocovariate). Autologistic regression involving several covariates is determined using the formula;
Since non-spatial models fail to account for the autocorrelation effect, there was a need to apply a spatial model: in this case, autologistic regression.

The autocovariate term captured part of the spatial pattern in the data observations, thus, providing a more robust estimation of the covariate effects after accounting for the spatial dependence in the observations.
227.Rationale: The passage identifies residual spatial autocorrelation as a problem and describes exactly this solution: autologistic regression with an autocovariate that summarizes neighbouring observations to account for spatial dependence. Incorporating an autocovariate is the critical factor to avoid confounding of environmental covariate effects by spatial non-independence.

227.Option: Include additional environmental covariates such as land cover and vegetation indices to improve model fit and reduce residual spatial autocorrelation
Evidence: A forward step-wise approach was applied to select the final multivariate logistic regression model. Covariates were added one after the other cumulatively and were retained if they retained statistical significance (p < 0.05).

The map of residuals and the residual variogram based on the multivariate logistic regression model revealed the existence of residual spatial autocorrelation. This situation is a problem as it violates the assumption of independence of residuals and can result in biased parameter estimates, leading to inflation of significance.
Since non-spatial models fail to account for the autocorrelation effect, there was a need to apply a spatial model: in this case, autologistic regression.

Fitting the autologistic regression model permitted us to assess the influence of spatial autocorrelation on the probability of tsetse presence. The parameters for temperature, rainfall, and cropland appeared less important (not statistically significant) after accounting for the effect of forest cover, riverine vegetation, elevation and spatial dependence in the observations.
227.Rationale: Adding covariates can improve fit, but the passage shows that even after including environmental predictors a residual spatial autocorrelation remained and required an explicit spatial model (autologistic). Thus, merely adding covariates is not the critical or sufficient action to address non-independence due to geographic proximity.

228.Option: Defining communities based on OSM polygons without a buffer may underestimate transmission risk in areas with high water contact density near community edges, leading to an overestimation of the association between environmental predictors and prevalence
Evidence: The spatial boundaries of communities were defined by Open Street Map (OSM) polygons (Fig 2) abstracted using QGIS software (version 2.12.3), an approach validated in a case study [29]. Subsequently, a buffer radius of 1 km was applied to each polygon.
The buffer distance was chosen because nearly all known contact with water bodies occurred within 1 km of community boundaries.

Most studies included relatively few RS environmental predictors, mainly normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI), land surface temperature (LST), and elevation, whereas many other vegetation- and moisture-related indices and topographic variables are available and should be considered [11,20,21].
228.Rationale: The passage states that the investigators explicitly applied a 1 km buffer because nearly all known water contact occurred within that distance, so defining communities without a buffer would likely miss relevant exposure near community edges. However, the option asserts that this would lead to an overestimation of the association between environmental predictors and prevalence. The passage provides no support for the specific claim about an overestimation (direction of bias). In epidemiologic terms, omission of relevant exposure area would more plausibly lead to under-ascertainment of exposure or attenuation of associations rather than a systematic overestimation. Because the passage does not support the option's claimed direction of bias, the option is incorrect.

228.Option: The 1 km buffer may overestimate exposure in communities with low water contact, leading to an inflated association between environmental predictors and prevalence due to non-differential misclassification
Evidence: Subsequently, a buffer radius of 1 km was applied to each polygon.
The buffer distance was chosen because nearly all known contact with water bodies occurred within 1 km of community boundaries.

Exploring different methods of defining “communities” over which risk factor variables are aggregated (e.g., varying the buffer radius within which transmission occurs) in other geographic, demographic, and cultural contexts is also recommended.
228.Rationale: The passage explains the buffer was chosen based on local data showing that nearly all known water contact occurred within 1 km, and the authors recommend exploring other buffer choices in different contexts. The option proposes that the 1 km buffer will overestimate exposure in low-contact communities and inflate associations via non-differential misclassification. The passage does not provide evidence that the chosen 1 km buffer systematically overestimates exposure in low-contact communities, nor that non-differential misclassification would inflate (rather than attenuate) associations. Therefore the specific claim that the buffer inflates the association is not supported by the passage and is incorrect.

228.Option: Using a 1 km buffer around community boundaries may misclassify exposure if actual water contact sites extend beyond this distance, potentially weakening the observed association between environmental predictors and prevalence
Evidence: Subsequently, a buffer radius of 1 km was applied to each polygon.
The buffer distance was chosen because nearly all known contact with water bodies occurred within 1 km of community boundaries.

Exploring different methods of defining “communities” over which risk factor variables are aggregated (e.g., varying the buffer radius within which transmission occurs) in other geographic, demographic, and cultural contexts is also recommended.

Most studies utilized point-prevalence data of human infections (outcome) typically measured at schools, whereas RS-based environmental data (predictors) pertain to water bodies that serve as snail habitats and potential transmission locations. Most models do not account for this spatial mismatch between exposure and outcome measures [11].
228.Rationale: The passage makes clear that the buffer choice is an explicit modeling decision and that it was chosen based on observed contact patterns, but it also recommends testing other buffer radii in other contexts. If in some communities actual water contact sites extend beyond 1 km, then the 1 km buffer would misclassify relevant exposure (omit distant contact sites). The passage highlights the problem of spatial mismatch between point-prevalence and environmental predictors; such misclassification of exposure would plausibly weaken (attenuate) observed associations between environmental predictors and prevalence. This option correctly describes that risk.

228.Option: Aggregating environmental variables within a 1 km buffer may reduce spatial resolution and obscure fine-scale transmission patterns, leading to a spurious association between environmental predictors and prevalence due to ecological fallacy
Evidence: S. haematobium infection prevalence (%) was measured in one school in each of the 73 study communities.

While S. haematobium infection prevalence was represented by point data, predictors were represented by continuous raster data (Fig 3). Therefore, extraction and aggregation of the raster data within the “community” polygons were necessary.

Schistosomiasis is known as a focal disease, meaning that neighboring villages with seemingly similar conditions can have drastically different transmission profiles and disease prevalence levels [10,18,19].

knowledge of water contact sites bridges the gap between where prevalence is measured and where transmission may have occurred. This is a critical gap in models that utilize environmental data as predictors of human infection.
228.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that prevalence was measured at a point (school) while environmental predictors are raster data that had to be aggregated across community polygons (the 1 km buffer). It also stresses that schistosomiasis is highly focal and neighboring villages can differ markedly. Aggregation across a buffer can therefore obscure fine-scale heterogeneity and create risks of ecological inference problems (ecological fallacy), where aggregated predictor–outcome relationships do not reflect individual-level transmission patterns. The option correctly identifies this potential validity threat.

228.Option: The use of a fixed 1 km buffer may introduce misclassification if the buffer size is too small in areas with high population density and extensive water use, potentially leading to an underestimation of the true association between environmental predictors and prevalence
Evidence: Subsequently, a buffer radius of 1 km was applied to each polygon. The buffer distance was chosen because nearly all known contact with water bodies occurred within 1 km of community boundaries.

Exploring different methods of defining “communities” over which risk factor variables are aggregated (e.g., varying the buffer radius within which transmission occurs) in other geographic, demographic, and cultural contexts is also recommended.
Most of these water bodies are used extensively for domestic and recreational purposes (e.g., fetching, washing, swimming, and fishing).
228.Rationale: The passage states the buffer was selected based on local knowledge but also recommends evaluating alternative buffer sizes in other contexts. In settings with intense or more distant water use, a fixed 1 km buffer that is too small could omit relevant exposure locations, causing exposure misclassification. Such non-differential misclassification of exposure would typically attenuate associations, leading to underestimation of the true relationship between environmental predictors and prevalence. The option accurately describes this plausible effect and is supported by the authors' recommendation to explore varying buffer radii.

229.Option: Exposure to rodents or their body fluids combined with living in an urban neighborhood, supporting a transmission pathway via rodent infestation in densely populated areas with poor infrastructure
Evidence: Leptospira spp. are spread in the urine of infected animals, with many implicated mammalian reservoirs and incidental hosts [6]. People are exposed to the bacteria either through direct urine-contact, or through environmental contact with urine-contaminated fresh water or mud. Bacterial transmission occurs through mucous membranes, skin abrasions, drinking contaminated water, and is suspected to occur through waterlogged skin [7].
seeing rodents or evidence of rodents in or around the house or land (pRR: 2.6; 95% CI: 1.2–6.0; p = 0.01), contact with rodents or their body fluids (pRR: 8.0; 95% CI: 1.9–34.1, p = 0.002)
Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03),

Our finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.
229.Rationale: While rodent exposure is strongly associated with seropositivity in the survey and Leptospira is transmitted in animal urine, this option lacks an explicit environmental water pathway (contaminated water or cisterns) specified in the combination. The urban neighborhood association was only significant in bivariate analysis, was subjectively classified, and did not persist in multivariate models; therefore it is a weaker and less specific indicator of a water-mediated zoonotic transmission pathway than combinations that explicitly include contaminated/untreated cistern or rainwater exposure.

229.Option: Exposure to rodents or their body fluids combined with contact with cistern water that lacks protective covers, supporting a transmission pathway via rodent urine contaminating rainwater collected in uncovered cisterns
Evidence: Leptospira spp. are spread in the urine of infected animals, with many implicated mammalian reservoirs and incidental hosts [6]. People are exposed to the bacteria either through direct urine-contact, or through environmental contact with urine-contaminated fresh water or mud. Bacterial transmission occurs through mucous membranes, skin abrasions, drinking contaminated water, and is suspected to occur through waterlogged skin [7].
seeing rodents or evidence of rodents in or around the house or land (pRR: 2.6; 95% CI: 1.2–6.0; p = 0.01), contact with rodents or their body fluids (pRR: 8.0; 95% CI: 1.9–34.1, p = 0.002)

Covering cisterns and other rainwater collection containers was significantly protective against seropositivity on bivariate analysis; this factor was not evaluated in the multivariate logistic regression model since many participants did not use cistern water or other collected rainwater as a drinking water source and would have been excluded from the model.

A cover over these rainwater collection containers would help to prevent animals, animal excrement, and debris from entering and potentially contaminating the water, however, would not address animal urine running into the cistern with rainwater from the roof.

Leptospira spp. were previously identified in cistern water associated with a post-hurricane case of leptospirosis in USVI [1], and a recent study of cistern water in USVI identified Leptospira spp. in 3/47 (6.4%) cisterns tested by PCR [54].

A recent investigation of rodent Leptospira spp. carriage in USVI identified leptospires in the kidneys of 45.7% of 140 rodents tested from the three main islands [41], which supports the findings in this investigation that rodent exposure is an important risk factor for human leptospirosis in USVI.
229.Rationale: This combination directly links a known reservoir (rodents) with a demonstrated local environmental water source (cisterns) that has tested positive for Leptospira and for which lack of covers was associated with higher seropositivity. The passage explicitly states that covers prevent animals/excrement entering cisterns and that Leptospira spp. have been detected in cistern water—together these facts support a plausible pathway of rodent urine contaminating collected rainwater in uncovered cisterns leading to human exposure.

229.Option: Exposure to rodents or their body fluids combined with contact with untreated cistern water, supporting a transmission pathway via rodent-contaminated rainwater collected in uncovered containers
Evidence: Leptospira spp. are spread in the urine of infected animals, with many implicated mammalian reservoirs and incidental hosts [6]. People are exposed to the bacteria either through direct urine-contact, or through environmental contact with urine-contaminated fresh water or mud. Bacterial transmission occurs through mucous membranes, skin abrasions, drinking contaminated water, and is suspected to occur through waterlogged skin [7].
seeing rodents or evidence of rodents in or around the house or land (pRR: 2.6; 95% CI: 1.2–6.0; p = 0.01), contact with rodents or their body fluids (pRR: 8.0; 95% CI: 1.9–34.1, p = 0.002)
Even though cisterns were often not used for drinking water by participants, many indicated contact with untreated cistern water, which could have been used for bathing, household chores, or farming.

Leptospira spp. were previously identified in cistern water associated with a post-hurricane case of leptospirosis in USVI [1], and a recent study of cistern water in USVI identified Leptospira spp. in 3/47 (6.4%) cisterns tested by PCR [54].
229.Rationale: This choice pairs the strong rodent exposure signal with direct contact with untreated cistern water, which the passage documents as a common contact route (bathing, chores) and shows Leptospira detection in cisterns. That combination provides a clear and supported water-mediated exposure pathway (rodent contamination of rainwater in collection containers leading to human contact with untreated water).

229.Option: Contact with cows or their body fluids combined with drinking untreated cistern water, supporting a transmission pathway via ingestion of water contaminated by cattle urine
Evidence: Leptospira spp. are spread in the urine of infected animals, with many implicated mammalian reservoirs and incidental hosts [6]. People are exposed to the bacteria either through direct urine-contact, or through environmental contact with urine-contaminated fresh water or mud. Bacterial transmission occurs through mucous membranes, skin abrasions, drinking contaminated water, and is suspected to occur through waterlogged skin [7].
contact with cows or their body fluids (pRR: 14.0; 95% CI: 5.6–35.2; p<0.001)

Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids (OR = 39.5, 95% CI = 9.0–172.7, p = <0.001);
Testing of cows at an abattoir in St. Croix demonstrated 25% seropositivity among cows tested, and one cow that was shedding leptospires in the urine [43].
Even though cisterns were often not used for drinking water by participants, many indicated contact with untreated cistern water, which could have been used for bathing, household chores, or farming.
Bacterial transmission occurs through mucous membranes, skin abrasions, drinking contaminated water, and is suspected to occur through waterlogged skin [7].
229.Rationale: Contact with cows is a very strong independent risk factor in this study and cows have been documented shedding leptospires. Paired with consumption (drinking) of untreated cistern water—a documented potential exposure route and medium for Leptospira—this combination plausibly supports a transmission pathway in which cattle-origin urine contaminates water that is then ingested by humans. While the explicit mechanism of cow urine entering cisterns is less directly described than for rodents, the study provides evidence that both cattle are reservoirs and cistern/untreated water is an exposure medium, making this a supported zoonotic waterborne pathway.

229.Option: Exposure to cows or their body fluids combined with contact with cistern water that lacks protective covers, supporting a transmission pathway via contaminated rainwater collected from animal-exposed environments
Evidence: Leptospira spp. are spread in the urine of infected animals, with many implicated mammalian reservoirs and incidental hosts [6]. People are exposed to the bacteria either through direct urine-contact, or through environmental contact with urine-contaminated fresh water or mud. Bacterial transmission occurs through mucous membranes, skin abrasions, drinking contaminated water, and is suspected to occur through waterlogged skin [7].
contact with cows or their body fluids (pRR: 14.0; 95% CI: 5.6–35.2; p<0.001)

Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids (OR = 39.5, 95% CI = 9.0–172.7, p = <0.001);
Testing of cows at an abattoir in St. Croix demonstrated 25% seropositivity among cows tested, and one cow that was shedding leptospires in the urine [43].

Covering cisterns and other rainwater collection containers was significantly protective against seropositivity on bivariate analysis; ... A cover over these rainwater collection containers would help to prevent animals, animal excrement, and debris from entering and potentially contaminating the water, however, would not address animal urine running into the cistern with rainwater from the roof.

Leptospira spp. were previously identified in cistern water associated with a post-hurricane case of leptospirosis in USVI [1], and a recent study of cistern water in USVI identified Leptospira spp. in 3/47 (6.4%) cisterns tested by PCR [54].
229.Rationale: This option pairs a very strong domestic-animal risk factor (contact with cows, which have documented shedding) with lack of protection of rainwater collection—an environmental factor shown to be associated with seropositivity and in which Leptospira has been detected. Together they support a plausible pathway in which animal exposure in the environment (including domestic animals) contaminates collected rainwater (especially when cisterns lack covers), producing an exposure route to humans. The passage explicitly ties both cattle and uncovered cisterns to leptospiral risk, supporting this transmission hypothesis.

230.Option: Weekly reporting by community-based lay reporters allows for the rapid identification of individual cases, enabling immediate clinical intervention
Evidence: their weekly reports, transmitted by simple smartphones, were instantaneously aggregated, analyzed and mapped by data collection centers locally (Gulu, Uganda) and remotely (Oregon, USA).
The study was not designed or approved to conduct or implement a medical intervention.

Data collection via the Magpi’s mobile platform took place for 12 consecutive weeks, from August 1st throughout October 31st 2017. During this period, the mHealth reporters were asked first to complete and store the surveys locally on their mobile devices in the field, and subsequently, depending on WiFi availability, upload them daily or weekly to a secure web-hosted database.
230.Rationale: While weekly reporting by lay reporters does provide timely information and can identify individual suspected cases, the passage explicitly states that the study "was not designed or approved to conduct or implement a medical intervention." Therefore the claim that weekly reporting "enables immediate clinical intervention" is not supported by the passage. The method enables rapid detection and reporting, but immediate clinical intervention was outside the study's design and approvals.

230.Option: Weekly, time-stamped, geolocated data collection by community-based reporters enables the construction of an epidemic curve to detect sudden increases in health events
Evidence: we customized a data collection platform named Magpi that uses mobile phones and real-time cloud-based storage with global positioning system (GPS) coordinates and time stamping [15].
The Magpi interface also made it possible to visualize NS health-related data over a defined period of time (Fig 3) with the goal to assess fluctuations and carry out temporal interpretations.

the data collected provided a real-time health geography that helped identify and track disease hotspots, drug availability, injuries, deaths, and missed and new/newly reported cases of NS with no more than 1–7 days of delay.
230.Rationale: Time-stamped and geolocated reports are the essential ingredients for constructing temporal (epidemic) curves and detecting sudden departures from expected case counts. The passage explicitly describes Magpi as providing "GPS coordinates and time stamping" and enabling visualization over time "with the goal to assess fluctuations and carry out temporal interpretations," supporting the statement that this feature would allow detection of sudden increases in events.

230.Option: High data completeness (97.3% weekly) ensures that the surveillance system can reliably detect rare events, such as the emergence of a new infectious disease
Evidence: the mHealth team completed a mean of 97.3% of surveys administered per week (29.2 ± 0.63 of 30 surveys assigned per mHealth reporter).

Overall, a total of 240 households (30 households per mHealth reporter) and an average of 326 children with NS (40.7 ± 4.57 children per mHealth reporter) were monitored every week by the mHealth team.

the data collected provided a real-time health geography that helped identify and track disease hotspots, drug availability, injuries, deaths, and missed and new/newly reported cases of NS with no more than 1–7 days of delay.
230.Rationale: High completeness (97.3% weekly) clearly increases the sensitivity of surveillance and helps detect events that might otherwise be missed. However, the option's wording that completeness "ensures" reliable detection of rare events overstates the guarantee—completeness improves but does not by itself ensure detection without temporal/spatial resolution and aggregation. The passage supports that high completion aided surveillance, but the feature that most directly identifies an early signal is the time-stamped/geolocated real-time reporting rather than completeness alone.

230.Option: The use of GPS-stamped data enables the system to track disease progression in individual patients over time, supporting early outbreak detection
Evidence: Magpi ... supports: ... d) GPS stamping and display of data-points on an electronic map;

All data acquired and integrated into the Magpi database could be exported, with no particular conversion problems, from the platform into statistical (Excel) or spatial (Magpi maps) analysis software.

The charts do not allow the reader to discern whether, for example, 10 children did not have medication for 1 week or whether 1 child did not have medication for 10 weeks (this information is made available in the weekly S1 Tables).
230.Rationale: GPS-stamped data permit spatial localization (household- or village-level mapping) and identification of geographic clusters, but GPS alone does not track clinical progression of individual patients over time. The passage indicates the system maps events and that some temporal detail is available in weekly tables, but it also notes limitations in discerning individual longitudinal patterns from aggregated visualizations. Thus the claim that GPS-stamping enables tracking of individual disease progression is unsupported.

230.Option: Real-time aggregation and mapping of health data allow for immediate detection of spatial clusters of illness, even without clinical confirmation
Evidence: their weekly reports, transmitted by simple smartphones, were instantaneously aggregated, analyzed and mapped by data collection centers locally (Gulu, Uganda) and remotely (Oregon, USA).

Displayed on a map, the data collected provided a real-time health geography that helped identify and track disease hotspots, drug availability, injuries, deaths, and missed and new/newly reported cases of NS with no more than 1–7 days of delay.
The system has wide applicability for data collection across a vast array of readily recognizable health-related conditions, including dangerous infectious diseases such as EHF.
230.Rationale: Real-time aggregation and mapping convert individual reports into spatial visualizations that can reveal clusters or unusual concentrations of illness before laboratory or clinical confirmation. The passage repeatedly emphasizes instantaneous aggregation and mapping and states that the real-time health geography "helped identify and track disease hotspots" with short delays—directly supporting this option as an enabler of early signals.

231.Option: Increase the frequency of leprosy elimination campaigns in all counties to detect asymptomatic cases and interrupt transmission
Evidence: The most common detection mode was outpatient clinic findings (2,983, 27.0%), followed by self-reported illness (2,279, 20.6%), clue investigation (1,963, 17.8%), reports by others (1,807, 16.4%), contact examination (1,272, 11.5%), focused surveys (in villages with leprosy cases) (343, 3.1%), other methods (157, 1.4%), leprosy elimination campaigns (LECs) (135, 1.2%), and group examinations (113, 1.0%) (Table 2 and Fig 2).

Contact tracing may not substantially influence the trend in new case detection [20,21] because leprosy patients are identifiable only when they have signs and symptoms of the disease, meaning that transmission might already have taken place during the asymptomatic stage [20].
231.Rationale: Mass elimination campaigns in every county are neither the most efficient nor necessarily effective way to identify transmission hotspots. The passage shows elimination campaigns (LECs) contributed a very small share of detections (1.2%), and it notes a fundamental limitation: leprosy is often only identifiable once symptomatic, so campaigns aimed at detecting asymptomatic infection are unlikely to succeed. Thus increasing campaign frequency everywhere would be low-yield for identifying ongoing transmission areas compared with targeted analytic approaches.

231.Option: Implement a province-wide screening program targeting individuals in households with confirmed leprosy cases to detect early-stage disease
Evidence: In total, 7,533 (68.2%) had a history of close contact with a leprosy patient, 3,350 (30.3%) had contact with a leprosy patient within the family, and 4,183 (37.9%) had contact with a leprosy patient outside the family.
In terms of the mode of detection, only 12% of new cases were detected by contact examination.

Consequently, it has been suggested that contact surveys should not focus only on household contacts but should be extended to entire neighborhoods or villages to target a greater spectrum of social contact networks [15].
231.Rationale: Household screening would help find cases among close contacts, since a substantial fraction had a history of contact. However, the passage emphasizes that many contacts occur outside the household and recommends extending contact surveys to neighborhoods or villages. Because the question asks for the most effective approach to identify geographic areas with ongoing transmission, household-only screening is too narrow and would miss non-household clusters; therefore it is not the optimal single approach.

231.Option: Compare the new case detection rate across counties over time to identify those with increasing incidence despite overall decline
Evidence: The new case detection rate (NCDR) was defined as the number of newly detected cases per year per 100,000 of the general population.
A total of 11,052 newly detected cases of leprosy were reported in Yunnan from 1990 to 2019, with the NCDR per 100,000 ranging from 1.93 to 0.38.
Historically, newly detected leprosy patients have been reported in all 129 counties of the 16 prefecture-level divisions of Yunnan Province.
By the end of 2019, 85.5% (100/117) of the leprosy-endemic counties had achieved the leprosy elimination target (Fig 3). Currently, 17/117 (14.5%) of the counties remain leprosy-endemic regions.
Understanding which geographical areas require intervention is fundamental for cost-effective disease control, and spatial analysis can indicate priority areas for local control programs [14,15].
231.Rationale: Comparing county-level NCDRs over time directly identifies counties where incidence is stable or rising despite overall provincial decline. The passage defines and uses NCDR, documents county-level heterogeneity (17 counties remaining endemic), and highlights the importance of identifying geographic areas requiring intervention. Thus this analytical approach is a valid and effective method to pinpoint areas with ongoing transmission.

231.Option: Perform contact tracing for all newly detected cases and map the geographic distribution of contacts to identify clusters of transmission
Evidence: In total, 7,533 (68.2%) had a history of close contact with a leprosy patient, 3,350 (30.3%) had contact with a leprosy patient within the family, and 4,183 (37.9%) had contact with a leprosy patient outside the family.
In terms of the mode of detection, only 12% of new cases were detected by contact examination.

Contact tracing may not substantially influence the trend in new case detection [20,21] because leprosy patients are identifiable only when they have signs and symptoms of the disease, meaning that transmission might already have taken place during the asymptomatic stage [20].

Consequently, it has been suggested that contact surveys should not focus only on household contacts but should be extended to entire neighborhoods or villages to target a greater spectrum of social contact networks [15].
231.Rationale: Mapping contacts can help reveal local transmission networks and clusters and is useful for targeted interventions. However, the passage notes limitations of contact tracing (many contacts are outside households; only 12% detected via contact examination; asymptomatic transmission limits effectiveness). While contact mapping is a valuable component, by itself it is not the most comprehensive or reliably effective method to identify persistent geographic hotspots compared with broader spatial analyses that integrate case counts and risk factors.

231.Option: Conduct spatial analysis integrating case distribution with socioeconomic risk factors to identify persistent transmission hotspots
Evidence: Maps showing the geographical distribution of newly detected leprosy patients were generated using the inverse distance weighted interpolation method (IDW) in ArcGIS software version 10.1 (Environmental Systems Research Institute, Inc., Redlands, CA, USA).
Understanding which geographical areas require intervention is fundamental for cost-effective disease control, and spatial analysis can indicate priority areas for local control programs [14,15].

Overcrowding, lower socioeconomic indicators, a lack of access to health services and precarious sanitation are underlying characteristics that impact the incidence of leprosy, indicating that poverty increases the likelihood of transmission of M. leprae [15].
231.Rationale: The passage explicitly endorses spatial analysis to identify priority areas and describes mapping methods used (IDW, ArcGIS). It also lists socioeconomic determinants (overcrowding, poverty, poor access to services) that influence transmission. Integrating spatial case distribution with these risk factors is therefore the most effective approach to detect persistent hotspots where transmission continues despite overall declines.

232.Option: The laboratory confirmation of rabies in two dogs, combined with the clustering of cases in suburban areas, supports the hypothesis that a single rabid dog was responsible for multiple infections
Evidence: Data collected from the Central Veterinary Laboratory in Maputo showed laboratory confirmed rabies in two dogs during the period under investigation.
Most of the dog bite victims and cases of human rabies resided in the suburban areas of Maputo and Matola cities.
232.Rationale: While laboratory confirmation that two dogs were rabid and clustering of human cases in suburban neighborhoods demonstrate local circulation of rabies and a likely focal source, confirmation in two separate dogs argues against the inference that a single rabid dog was the sole source of multiple human cases. The presence of two laboratory-confirmed rabid dogs is more consistent with at least two infected animals in the area rather than proving a single-animal source. The suburban clustering supports local transmission but does not distinguish whether infections arose from one dog or multiple dogs.

232.Option: The fact that one dog was linked to three human cases, and that the majority of bites were from dogs, indicates that a single rabid dog was likely responsible for the outbreak
Evidence: we were able to link seven cases of human rabies to bites of five dogs, as shown in Fig 4. Four of the five dogs were each linked to one rabies victim, and one was linked to three victims, two of whom were identified in the community investigation.
Most animal bites were caused by dogs (97.8%, 801/819).
All of the 14 cases of human rabies involved dog bite.
232.Rationale: Direct epidemiologic linkage showing one dog was linked to three human rabies cases is the strongest single piece of evidence supporting a single-animal source: a single rabid dog biting multiple people is a well-described transmission scenario for rabies (dogs frequently bite multiple individuals, and rabies transmits primarily via saliva during bites). The fact that nearly all bites and all rabies cases involved dogs further aligns with the biology of rabies (Lyssavirus transmitted mainly by dog bites) and supports the plausibility that one highly neurotropic, aggressive rabid dog could have caused multiple human infections in the same locality.

232.Option: The high proportion of bites from stray dogs (23.7%) suggests that multiple rabid animals were likely circulating, increasing the probability of a single dog being responsible for multiple cases
Evidence: The biting dog was known or owned in 58.3% (467/801) of dog bite cases. In 23.7% (194/801) and 17.1% (140/801) the biting dog was stray or unknown, respectively.
Almost one third of the animal bite victims and most of the human rabies cases in this study were bitten by a stray dog, corroborating findings from Nigeria and other developing countries...
232.Rationale: A high proportion of bites from stray dogs indicates that unowned animals are an important source of exposures and raises the possibility that multiple infected animals were circulating. That situation decreases, rather than increases, the specific probability that a single dog was responsible for multiple cases. Thus the evidence of many stray-dog bites argues for multiple potential sources and does not strongly support the single-animal-source hypothesis.

232.Option: The fact that only one of the 14 rabies victims received post-exposure prophylaxis suggests that the virus was highly transmissible and likely originated from a single infected animal
Evidence: No rabies victim received full post-exposure vaccination. In Matola, one victim attended a health center and received only tetanus vaccine and the parents were informed that it was sufficient.
Of the 156 victims for whom information on post-exposure vaccination was available, only 47 (30.1%) received the vaccine, of whom all except one lived in Matola City.
232.Rationale: Lack of post-exposure prophylaxis explains why bitten persons progressed to fatal rabies but does not indicate that the virus was unusually highly transmissible or that a single animal was the source. Poor access to PEP increases risk of disease following any infectious exposure and therefore contributes to multiple cases from one or many bites, but by itself it is not evidence that a single animal caused multiple infections or that transmissibility differed from typical rabies biology.

232.Option: One rabid dog was linked to three human cases, and laboratory confirmation of rabies in two dogs supports the possibility of a single source driving multiple infections
Evidence: we were able to link seven cases of human rabies to bites of five dogs, as shown in Fig 4. Four of the five dogs were each linked to one rabies victim, and one was linked to three victims, two of whom were identified in the community investigation.
Data collected from the Central Veterinary Laboratory in Maputo showed laboratory confirmed rabies in two dogs during the period under investigation.
232.Rationale: The fact that one dog was epidemiologically linked to three human cases does support the possibility that a single animal caused multiple infections. However, laboratory confirmation of rabies in two dogs does not strengthen the single-source argument; it instead demonstrates that more than one dog was confirmed rabid in the area, which is compatible with multiple infected animals circulating. Therefore the combined evidence does not specifically support a single-source conclusion and is a weaker, or potentially contradictory, basis for asserting a single-animal origin.

233.Option: A sustained Th17 response throughout infection, accompanied by high levels of IL-17A, is most strongly associated with resolution of infection and prevention of chronicity
Evidence: The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.

These results indicate that the increase in the Th17 population in the infection site during the first 14 days, followed by Th1 population increase, closely assisted by a growing Treg population until disease resolution, may represent the ideal balance of these populations when aiming at CBM remission.

High doses of IL-17 in lesions essentially represent a host strategy against fungal infections. However, an average Th17 response may overlap the regulatory role of Tregs, and this imbalance may eventually result in a less effective response to the fungus [16].

in situ studies with lesions of CBM patients revealed a higher quantification of IL-17 when compared to other mycoses such as paracoccidioidomycosis [16]. Not only that, but CBM lesions are also characterized by intense monocytic and neutrophilic response, as well as the presence of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1β, TNFα, and IL-6 [4,50,51].
233.Rationale: The passage describes Th17 as predominant only in early stages and explicitly states that remission is associated with a subsequent rise in Th1 and coordinated Treg activity, not sustained Th17. It also warns that persistently high Th17/IL-17 can overlap regulatory functions and contribute to ineffective responses and chronic inflammatory pathology. Therefore a sustained Th17 response throughout infection is not supported as the pattern most strongly associated with resolution and prevention of chronicity.

233.Option: High levels of Treg activity in the infected tissue, without concurrent effector T cell responses, are sufficient to resolve infection and prevent chronicity in chromoblastomycosis
Evidence: Tregs are characterized by the high expression of CD25 and Foxp3 transcription factor, acting both in the modulation of the immune response against pathogens and in the control of the immune response to self and non-self antigens [18–20].
These cells exert their function by releasing inhibitory cytokines such as IL-10 and TGF-β [20].

However, considering that Treg-mediated response may limit the effectiveness of the protective immune response, when not followed by an active cellular response such as Th1, the consequence may be the persistence of fungal infection [59,60].
Depletion of CD25+ cells reduced CFUs in the footpad at day 14 post-infection but delayed disease resolution
233.Rationale: The passage portrays Tregs as modulatory and necessary for a regulated response but warns that Treg activity in isolation (i.e., without concurrent effector responses like Th1/Th17) can limit protective immunity and permit persistence of fungus. The experimental depletion of CD25+ cells produced complex effects (transient CFU reduction but delayed resolution), indicating Treg activity alone is neither sufficient nor solely beneficial for resolution/prevention of chronicity.

233.Option: A balanced coexistence of Th17 and Th1 responses throughout the infection, without a clear temporal shift, is the optimal immunological pattern for resolving chromoblastomycosis
Evidence: Th17 was predominant in the infected area during the early stages of experimental murine CBM. The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.

The numbers of Th17 cells (CD4hi IL-17+) rose sharply during the early stages of the disease (until 14 days after infection) and fell gradually during later time points in both footpad (Fig 1D and S3B Fig) and dLN (Fig 1D and S4B Fig).
Consequently, the Th1/Th17 ratio is lowest and highest in the footpad at days 7 and 21 post-infection, respectively (Fig 1E and 1F).
233.Rationale: The passage emphasizes a temporal sequence—early Th17 dominance followed by later Th1 dominance coinciding with remission—rather than a stable, unchanging coexistence. Quantitative data show Th17 rises then falls while Th1 increases later; thus the optimal pattern is dynamic with a temporal shift, not a continuous balanced coexistence without shift.

233.Option: A dominant Th1 response from the onset of infection, characterized by high IFN-γ production, is the most effective pattern for preventing chronicity in chromoblastomycosis
Evidence: Th17 was predominant in the infected area during the early stages of experimental murine CBM. The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.

The numbers of Th1 (CD4hi IFN-γ+) cells in the footpad increased steadily over 21 days post-infection and then decreased (Fig 1C and S3C Fig), whereas the expansion of Th1 cells was less pronounced in popliteal lymph node (Fig 1C and S4C Fig).
Neutralization of IL-17A and IFN-γ neutralization hindered fungal cell elimination in the course of the disease.

Neutralization of IFN-γ increased CFUs at days 7 and 14 (Fig 3G). Although not found to be statistically significant, neutralization of IFN-γ showed a trend towards increased CFUs vs. control mice at later time points, allowing fungal persistence up to 28 days after infection.
233.Rationale: While IFN-γ/Th1 is clearly important (neutralization impairs clearance), the passage indicates Th17 is critical early and that Th1 becomes dominant later during remission. The experimental evidence supports a complementary and temporal role for Th1 rather than a model where an immediate, dominant Th1 response from infection onset alone is the most effective. Thus a Th1-dominant-from-onset model is not supported as the optimal pattern.

233.Option: A sequential shift from Th17 to Th1 dominance, supported by regulatory T cell activity, enables effective fungal clearance and prevents chronic disease progression
Evidence: Th17 was predominant in the infected area during the early stages of experimental murine CBM. The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.

These results indicate that the increase in the Th17 population in the infection site during the first 14 days, followed by Th1 population increase, closely assisted by a growing Treg population until disease resolution, may represent the ideal balance of these populations when aiming at CBM remission.
Foxp3+ Treg cells also expand during the infection, being more numerous in dLN (Fig 1G).
In vivo analysis with IL-17A and IFN-γ neutralization hindered fungal cell elimination in the course of the disease.
