In order to create a "non-susceptible" comparison group, we identified contact subjects from the two studies who lived in the same house as another subject who was DENV positive on day 0, but were themselves DENV negative by nested PCR and IgM ELISA on both days 0 and 15 (KPSII study), or by NS1 ELISA and IgM ELISA on both days 0 and 14 (DEVOL study).
These DENV negative "non-susceptible" subjects were presumed to have a high likelihood of exposure to DENV during the cluster investigation, yet still did not become infected.
In both the KPSII and DEVOL studies, the exposure serotype for all "non-susceptible" subjects was presumed to be the same as the infecting serotype for the index case from the same cluster.
258.Rationale: Selecting PCR-positive 'susceptible' subjects and PCR-negative 'non-susceptible' household contacts (with the exposure serotype presumed to be that of the index case) increases the likelihood that cases and controls experienced the same exposure/serotype. This reduces confounding by serotype variation and strengthens the comparison, which would help detect an association even with small numbers.

258.Option: The use of a two-week window for blood collection allowed for the detection of both pre-existing and newly formed neutralizing antibodies, increasing the likelihood of identifying protective immunity
Evidence: our study provided blood samples from within two weeks prior to virus exposure, thus minimizing the confounding factor of variations in antibody kinetics.
In all subjects, the day 0 blood sample was presumed to reflect the neutralizing antibody status just prior to exposure or infection.
No data or blood was collected after day 15.
All five subjects were PCR negative at day 0, and DENV IgM ELISA negative at days 0 and 14. No data or blood was collected after day 14.
258.Rationale: The passage states the day 0 sample was intended to measure pre-exposure neutralizing titers and that no blood was collected after day 14/15. The design sought to capture pre-existing antibodies just before exposure, not to detect newly formed antibodies. Therefore the claim that the two-week window enhanced detection of newly formed antibodies is inconsistent with the study description.

258.Option: The use of multiple DENV serotypes in the PRNT assay allowed for the detection of cross-protective immune responses, increasing the sensitivity of the association analysis
Evidence: To determine neutralizing antibody status just prior to exposure for all "susceptible" and "non-susceptible" subjects, day 0 blood samples were tested by an in-house plaque reduction neutralization test (PRNT) using all four DENV serotypes and JEV as previously described [24], [25].

Homotypic NTs were more strongly associated with protection than heterotypic NTs except in the case of DENV-2 infections, in which pre-existing heterotypic DENV-4 NTs were also positively associated with protection against DENV-2.

Two major strengths of this study design likely explain why a significant association between NTs and protection from infection was observed in contrast to previous prospective cohort studies. Whereas earlier cohort studies typically analyzed NTs in blood samples collected up to six months or more prior to the incident DENV infection, our study provided blood samples from within two weeks prior to virus exposure, thus minimizing the confounding factor of variations in antibody kinetics.
258.Rationale: While the study did perform PRNT against multiple serotypes and did find some heterotypic associations (notably DENV-4 NTs with DENV-2 protection), the authors emphasize that the proximate timing of blood draws was a major reason they could detect the association despite small sample size. Use of multiple serotypes helped characterize homotypic versus heterotypic responses but is not presented as the primary factor enabling detection with limited numbers.

258.Option: The inclusion of both children and adults in the study increased the diversity of immune responses, improving the generalizability of the association between neutralizing antibodies and protection
Evidence: Because of the small numbers, we used data from two different geographic cluster studies over multiple years. The inclusion of both adults and children with likely differing immunological backgrounds may have affected our findings for the DENV-2 analysis, possibly accounting for the higher DENV-2 NTs associated with protection.

we used data from a prospective longitudinal cohort and geographic cluster study conducted from 2004 to 2007 among children living in Muang district, Kamphaeng Phet province (KPP) in north-central Thailand.

In the DEVOL study, geographic cluster investigations were initiated by “index” cases admitted to KPP hospital who were DENV positive by nested PCR [21], [22]. In each geographic cluster, adults and children ≥6 months of age living within 200 meters of the index case were enrolled regardless of clinical status.
258.Rationale: The passage actually lists inclusion of adults (DEVOL) versus only children (KPSII) as a potential source of confounding rather than as a strength that improved detection. The authors note this could have affected interpretation (particularly for DENV-2), so increased age diversity is not presented as the reason they were able to detect a significant association with small sample size.

259.Option: In high-transmission settings, the high proportion of sub-microscopic infections indicates that routine surveillance using light microscopy is sufficient to detect most P. vivax cases and guide interventions effectively
Evidence: This suggests that a relatively larger proportion of P. vivax infections are SM in areas with low transmission intensity compared to areas with high transmission intensity, although SM P. vivax is prevalent in both type of settings.

Overall across all sites, the PCR prevalence of P. vivax was significantly higher than that of LM (Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test, P<0.0001, Fig. 1), i.e. PCR detected a significantly higher number of P. vivax infections than LM.
This suggests that without PCR or other comparably sensitive methods, an average of 69% of P. vivax infections would be undetected hence antimalarial treatment will usually not be sought.
259.Rationale: The option misstates the epidemiology and draws the opposite conclusion. The passage states that the relative proportion of sub-microscopic (SM) infections is higher in low-transmission areas (not high-transmission), and that PCR generally detects substantially more infections than LM (on average 69% of P. vivax infections would be undetected without PCR). Therefore routine LM surveillance is not sufficient in settings where SM infections are common; the option's claim that LM is sufficient in high-transmission settings is not supported by the passage.

259.Option: Since sub-microscopic P. vivax infections are asymptomatic, they do not contribute to transmission and can be safely ignored in elimination planning
Evidence: Mature gametocytes are present earlier in P. vivax infections meaning they can infect mosquitoes at an early stage of infection, with SM P. vivax gametocytes shown to be able to successfully infect mosquitoes [8], [9].
The proportion of individuals with symptoms ranged from 0.0% to 11.4%, averaging 2.8%. This means that between 88.6 and 100.0% (average 97.1%) of individuals with SM P. vivax were asymptomatic.

This suggests that without PCR or other comparably sensitive methods, an average of 69% of P. vivax infections would be undetected hence antimalarial treatment will usually not be sought. This results in a potentially large group of people carrying parasites that have the potential to transmit P. vivax [8], [9] and maintain the transmission cycle.
SM P. vivax infections represent a more important transmission reservoir due to multiple relapses occurring over a long period of time.
259.Rationale: Although SM P. vivax infections are frequently asymptomatic (the passage reports an average ~97% asymptomatic), the passage explicitly states that SM infections can transmit to mosquitoes (gametocytes present early and SM infections can infect mosquitoes), and that these undetected infections can maintain transmission and cause relapses. Thus they cannot be ignored in elimination planning.

259.Option: Because SM P. vivax infections are more common in adults, surveillance should focus on adult populations to detect the majority of transmissible infections
Evidence: Of the 16 surveys that analysed the relationship of SM P. vivax with age, eight reported the relative proportion of SM P. vivax was not associated with age, while the other eight reported that the relative proportion of SM P. vivax increased with age.

Age. In many malaria endemic settings, the adolescent population has higher proportions of asymptomatic and low density Plasmodium infections than young children due to the development of clinical immunity after repeated exposure to P. vivax parasites.
SM P. vivax is prevalent across different geographic areas with varying transmission intensities constituting, on average, 69.5% of all P. vivax infections.
259.Rationale: The evidence on age is mixed: half the surveys found no association between age and SM proportion while the other half found an increase with age. The passage does not support a universal conclusion that SM infections are predominantly in adults. SM infections are widespread across settings and age groups, so focusing surveillance only on adults would risk missing many infections.

259.Option: The negative relationship between LM prevalence and SM proportion suggests that increasing the sensitivity of light microscopy would reduce the proportion of sub-microscopic infections across all transmission settings
Evidence: A negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified (Fig. 7), with LM P. vivax prevalence identified as a significant factor for predicting the proportion of samples that are PCR positive/LM negative (SM) in cross-sectional surveys where the LM P. vivax prevalence is less than 45% (P<0.001, Deviance  = 7.32, df  = 29).
From a technical point of view, the quality of microscopy is an important factor that can contribute to the level of LM P. vivax, and thus SM P. vivax.
Overall across all sites, the PCR prevalence of P. vivax was significantly higher than that of LM ... i.e. PCR detected a significantly higher number of P. vivax infections than LM.
In order to maximize PCR sensitivity in detecting SM parasite infections, >150 µL of whole blood should be used when possible.
259.Rationale: While there is a documented negative relationship (lower LM prevalence associated with higher SM proportion), the statement overgeneralizes. Improving LM sensitivity or QA might reduce some SM classifications, but LM has inherent detection limits and even high-quality microscopy will not reach PCR-level sensitivity. The passage also notes technical constraints (blood volume, PCR sensitivity, and that the negative relationship was characterized for LM prevalence <45%), indicating that simply increasing LM sensitivity will not necessarily eliminate SM infections across all settings; PCR or comparably sensitive diagnostics are still needed.

259.Option: In low-transmission settings, surveillance based on light microscopy will substantially underestimate the true burden of P. vivax and fail to identify a major reservoir of transmissible infections, necessitating more sensitive diagnostics for elimination success
Evidence: This suggests that a relatively larger proportion of P. vivax infections are SM in areas with low transmission intensity compared to areas with high transmission intensity, although SM P. vivax is prevalent in both type of settings.

Overall across all sites, the PCR prevalence of P. vivax was significantly higher than that of LM (Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test, P<0.0001, Fig. 1), i.e. PCR detected a significantly higher number of P. vivax infections than LM.

This suggests that without PCR or other comparably sensitive methods, an average of 69% of P. vivax infections would be undetected hence antimalarial treatment will usually not be sought. This results in a potentially large group of people carrying parasites that have the potential to transmit P. vivax [8], [9] and maintain the transmission cycle.
SM P. vivax poses more challenges to malaria control programs in areas where transmission intensities are already low and are progressing toward elimination.

To accelerate malaria elimination ... some form of mass screening using PCR-based or comparably sensitive methods and radical cure approach would be required to identify and treat all SM P. vivax infected subjects.
259.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that SM infections form a larger proportion of infections in low-transmission settings, that PCR detects many more infections than LM (averaging ~69% undetected by LM), that SM infections can transmit and relapse, and that PCR-level or comparably sensitive screening (and radical cure) would be required to identify and treat these reservoirs for elimination. Therefore surveillance relying only on LM in low-transmission settings will substantially underestimate burden and miss a major transmissible reservoir, supporting the option's conclusion.

260.Option: The lack of integration of NTDs into the core curriculum's transmission-based classification may result in students failing to recognize the shared epidemiological patterns among these diseases
Evidence: The specific epidemiology of infectious diseases is divided according to the 4 main modes of transmission, namely: droplet, foodborne, contact, and arthropod borne infections.
Most of the NTDs are included in the course such as schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, soil transmitted helminthiasis (STH) as ascariasis and hookworm infections.
Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
260.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the epidemiology course is organized by transmission modes and that most NTDs are included in the course (schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, STH). Therefore the claim that NTDs were not integrated into the transmission-based classification is contradicted by the passage. The real problem described is the absence of a unifying label/concept (the term "NTDs" is not used), not a failure to place these diseases within the transmission-based structure.

260.Option: The absence of a unifying conceptual framework for NTDs in the curriculum may prevent students from recognizing these diseases as a distinct public health priority despite exposure to individual diseases
Evidence: Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
Most of the NTDs are included in the course such as schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, soil transmitted helminthiasis (STH) as ascariasis and hookworm infections.
Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs;
260.Rationale: The passage shows students were exposed to individual diseases in the course but the unifying label/concept ('NTDs') was not presented explicitly. That absence of a conceptual framework (no explicit teaching of NTDs as a group) plausibly explains why students failed to recognize these conditions collectively as a distinct public health priority despite encountering the individual diseases.

260.Option: The low awareness among students may be due to the fact that NTDs are not covered in the clinical training phase of medical education, which limits their real-world exposure
Evidence: Only 9% stated that they had seen a case of NTDs.

A study by Villafuerte-Galvez J.et.al 2008 conducted in Peru including medical students from all grades revealed that the students’ epidemiological knowledge about NTDs remained the same for the rest of studying years, while their clinical practice improved by actively participating in training, research, and publication to fight NTDs in their country [20].
students finished their epidemiology course with meager epidemiological knowledge about endemic NTDs in Egypt.
260.Rationale: The passage documents very low real-world exposure (only 9% had seen a case) and cites evidence that clinical practice and active participation improve understanding. These statements support the plausible explanation that limited clinical exposure or lack of integration of NTDs into clinical training contributes to low awareness despite completing the epidemiology course.

260.Option: The lack of emphasis on NTDs in the epidemiology course may stem from the absence of standardized national guidelines for teaching these diseases in medical education
Evidence: Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
The results of awareness assessment in this study underline the need for structured teaching programs for medical and nursing students in the context of NTDs and their preventive strategies.
Egyptian medical and nursing curricula should be updated. NTDs concepts, epidemiology, and management in detail must be integrated in the medical and nursing curricula and practical training.
260.Rationale: While the passage documents a lack of emphasis on NTDs in the course and recommends curricular updates, it does not provide any evidence about the presence or absence of standardized national guidelines for teaching NTDs. The option attributes the cause to absence of national guidelines—an assertion not supported by the quoted text—so this specific causal claim is not supported by the passage.

260.Option: The limited exposure to NTDs in the epidemiology course may be due to the course's focus on high-profile diseases such as HIV and tuberculosis, which overshadow less common but endemic conditions
Evidence: The specific epidemiology of infectious diseases is divided according to the 4 main modes of transmission, namely: droplet, foodborne, contact, and arthropod borne infections.
Most of the NTDs are included in the course such as schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, soil transmitted helminthiasis (STH) as ascariasis and hookworm infections.
Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs; the main source of their knowledge is social media, followed by epidemiology course and mass media (TV/Radio)
260.Rationale: The passage does not mention HIV, tuberculosis, or other high-profile diseases as competing priorities in the course content, nor does it state that such diseases overshadow NTDs. Instead it states that many NTDs were included in the course but the group label ('NTDs') was not used and that students primarily learned from social media. Because the specific claim about HIV/TB crowding out NTD content is not supported by any passage text, this option is not supported.

261.Option: Perform the assay within 1 hour of drug addition to minimize the impact of time-dependent changes in parasite motility and drug metabolism
Evidence: The results of Figs 12, 13, 14, 15 and 16 suggest that benznidazole produces an instantaneous effect at 1min on T. cruzi that occurs at all the tested concentrations, with a profile that remains almost the same up to at least 61min, as shown by the polynomial tendencies (Fig 13) and the resulting profile of Fig 15.

The results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes, indicating that as was seen in Figs 2, 3 and 4 (Effect of Parasite Quantity), the Biospeckle pattern under the conditions of this work, reveals the total number of parasites within the well of the VDRL plate.
desiccation may interfere by increasing drug concentration at the longer times.
261.Rationale: While the passage documents rapid drug effects (instantaneous at 1 min and similar up to ~61 min), the principal physical confounder emphasized throughout the paper is desiccation/evaporation changing liquid volume and thus apparent drug concentration. The recommendation to constrain assays to a short interval to avoid biological time-dependent changes (motility/metabolism) is not the main confounding physical process described. Therefore limiting to 1 hour for the reason stated does not directly address the key physical confounder (desiccation) identified in the passage.

261.Option: Limit the assay duration to less than 200 minutes to prevent desiccation from altering drug concentration and confounding the interpretation of parasite activity
Evidence: The results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes, indicating that ... the Biospeckle pattern under the conditions of this work, reveals the total number of parasites within the well of the VDRL plate.

In order to estimate desiccation of the LIT medium at room temperature (20–25°C), each one of 12 VDRL test wells in 4 different plates, was filled with 100μL of LIT medium... At four different times (0, 55, 120 and 270 minutes) one of the plates was used to measure the total volume and estimate the desiccation process.
desiccation may interfere by increasing drug concentration at the longer times.
261.Rationale: The passage explicitly documents evaporation/desiccation of the open VDRL wells and shows the Biospeckle signal is stable up to about 200 minutes; beyond that desiccation can alter remaining volume and thereby increase drug concentration, confounding interpretation. Limiting assay duration to below the ~200-minute stability window directly controls that physical confounder.

261.Option: Use a sealed chamber or humidified environment to prevent evaporation and maintain constant liquid volume throughout the assay
Evidence: Desiccation of the liquid in the VDRL wells

In order to estimate desiccation of the LIT medium at room temperature (20–25°C), each one of 12 VDRL test wells in 4 different plates, was filled with 100μL of LIT medium... At four different times (0, 55, 120 and 270 minutes) one of the plates was used to measure the total volume and estimate the desiccation process.
desiccation may interfere by increasing drug concentration at the longer times.

it would be necessary to establish the relationship between viability and the activity of the Biospeckle pattern, the possible optical interferences of high drug concentrations which should be avoided especially in those cases in which desiccation is also an issue to be taken care of
261.Rationale: The passage identifies evaporation/desiccation of open wells as a measurable process that can change volume and effectively increase drug concentration, thus confounding observed parasite activity. Using a sealed or humidified environment directly addresses that physical cause of confounding by preventing evaporation and keeping liquid volume constant.

261.Option: Standardize the initial volume of liquid in each well to ensure consistent baseline conditions across all experimental groups
Evidence: Unless otherwise stated, all the assays were performed with an initial volume of 100μL.

These assays allowed the selection of the conditions of the method using VDRL plates. We selected the following conditions: 100μL in the test well, parasites in exponential phase, 4x104 parasites per well, a maximum time of evaluation of the activity of the Biospeckle pattern of 204 minutes.

Since the object’s plane has a constant volume of 1.66μL due to the parasite’s dimensions ... the percentage that it represents in the constant density experiment increases as the total volume of liquid in the well decreases.
261.Rationale: The paper emphasizes use of a fixed initial volume (100 μL) as part of the standardized assay conditions and demonstrates that the object plane and measured Biospeckle signal depend on the liquid volume. Standardizing initial volume across wells prevents baseline differences in optical geometry, parasite distribution, and apparent concentration that could otherwise be mistaken for drug effects.

261.Option: Match the concentration of benznidazole across all wells to ensure that differences in activity are due to parasite viability rather than drug concentration variation
Evidence: The effect of varying amounts of benznidazole on the activity of the parasites was tested and compared to the pattern obtained from the LIT medium with benznidazole and without parasites.
desiccation may interfere by increasing drug concentration at the longer times.

it would be necessary to establish ... the possible optical interferences of high drug concentrations which should be avoided especially in those cases in which desiccation is also an issue to be taken care of
261.Rationale: While keeping known drug concentrations consistent is fundamental experimental practice, the question asks which factor must be controlled to avoid a confounding physical process. The passage highlights evaporation/desiccation (leading to unintended concentration changes) as the key physical confounder; simply matching intended benznidazole concentrations across wells does not prevent evaporation-driven concentration changes during the assay. Therefore this option does not address the main physical confounder discussed in the passage.

262.Option: Living in homes with earthen/mud floors, which reflects poor housing conditions and limits access to protective footwear
Evidence: Factors that have been reported to aggravate the problem include earthen and dusty floors, non regular use of closed footwear, common resting place outside the house and pigs on the compound [10].
A large percentage of the population (79.4%) stay in homes with earthen/mud floors [20].

The type of floor, not using closed footwear regularly, lack of a toilet and having a common resting place inside the house were retained in the model indicating they had a contribution though not significant.
262.Rationale: Earthen/mud floors are an important structural risk factor (quoted above) that increase environmental suitability for the flea. However the question asks for the factor that most directly reflects the interplay between individual behavior and structural conditions. Earthen floors are primarily a structural condition; they do not themselves represent a behavioral choice. Therefore, while relevant, this option is not the most direct reflection of the combined behavioral+structural interplay described.

262.Option: Inconsistent use of closed footwear, driven by economic constraints and shaped by high child population density
Evidence: Factors that have been reported to aggravate the problem include earthen and dusty floors, non regular use of closed footwear, common resting place outside the house and pigs on the compound [10].
In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2).
This can be attributed to the fact that they are more exposed due to them playing barefoot [10] and also getting reinfected at school in endemic areas.

The type of floor, not using closed footwear regularly, lack of a toilet and having a common resting place inside the house were retained in the model indicating they had a contribution though not significant.
Tungiasis is certainly a poverty related disease [12].
262.Rationale: Not using closed footwear is an individual behavior that directly determines exposure of the host's outer body surfaces (feet) to infective stages in the environment. The passage explicitly lists "non regular use of closed footwear" and "occasional or not using closed footwear" as associated with tungiasis and links children's barefoot play to higher exposure. This behavior is shaped by structural/economic conditions (poverty, housing) that limit access to footwear. Thus inconsistent footwear use most directly embodies the interaction between individual behavior and structural determinants that increases vulnerability to ectoparasite colonization.

262.Option: Age ≤14 years, which reflects both developmental vulnerability and high exposure due to outdoor play in endemic areas
Evidence: In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2).

In the multivariate analysis, the occurrence of tungiasis was significantly associated with variables that indicated low economic status (like monthly income <1000 adjusted OR 27.85 (4.13–187.59) and age (≤14 years) of participant adjusted OR 27.414 (10.02–74.99) (Table 3).
This can be attributed to the fact that they are more exposed due to them playing barefoot [10] and also getting reinfected at school in endemic areas.
262.Rationale: Age ≤14 years is an important risk marker and reflects developmental exposure patterns (children play barefoot). However age is a demographic attribute that proxies behavioral exposure rather than itself being the behavior-structural interaction. The question asks for the factor that most directly reflects the interplay between behavior and structural conditions; age indicates who is exposed but is less direct than a modifiable behavior (footwear use) that sits at the nexus of individual choice and material constraints.

262.Option: Lack of electricity and lack of a toilet, which are indicators of low economic status and contribute to poor hygiene practices
Evidence: In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2). Others were being illiterate or low literacy level, bathing without soap, common resting place inside the house, having rats in the compound, lack of electricity and lack of a toilet.

The type of floor, not using closed footwear regularly, lack of a toilet and having a common resting place inside the house were retained in the model indicating they had a contribution though not significant.
These findings suggest a need to design control strategies for tungiasis that are cost effective and easily accessible by the communities affected.
262.Rationale: Lack of electricity and toilets are clear structural indicators of poverty that contribute to overall vulnerability and hygiene deficits. However they are broad contextual factors and do not as directly link an individual behavioral exposure (e.g., barefooting) to the parasite's opportunity to colonize outer body surfaces. The question emphasizes the interplay between behavior and structure that directly affects exposure of the host's skin; lack of utilities is important but less proximal to the exposure pathway than footwear use.

262.Option: Bathing without soap, which reflects poor hygiene and is associated with low literacy and lack of health education
Evidence: In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2). Others were being illiterate or low literacy level, bathing without soap, common resting place inside the house, having rats in the compound, lack of electricity and lack of a toilet.
Bathing without soap is a factor that has been elucidated in previous studies as well [7, 13].
Almost a half, 45% of the participants interviewed had no knowledge on its transmission.
262.Rationale: Bathing without soap is an individual hygiene behavior influenced by education and resources and is plausibly linked to infestation intensity. However tungiasis transmission occurs through contact of skin (often feet) with contaminated soil/ground; lack of footwear and earthen floors are more proximal to that exposure pathway. Bathing without soap reflects poor hygiene but is less directly tied to the ecological exposure dynamics of ectoparasite colonization of the skin than inconsistent footwear use.

263.Option: The delayed peak in dengue cases is caused by a lag in public health reporting, with more cases being diagnosed and recorded in October due to increased surveillance during the peak transmission season
Evidence: The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).

Dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall. This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].

Dengue is one of 24 infectious diseases in Vietnam for which there is monthly mandatory reporting from all administrative levels, which, in increasing size, are communes, districts, provinces, and regions.
263.Rationale: The passage attributes the timing of the seasonal peak to climatic drivers and vector biology (wet/hot period, mosquito abundance, and the extrinsic incubation period), and explicitly states the outbreak timing (begin July, peak October). It also describes a standardized monthly mandatory reporting system, not ad hoc intensified surveillance in October. Therefore the peak is explained by real seasonal transmission dynamics rather than an artefactual reporting lag or increased surveillance in October.

263.Option: The peak in dengue cases in October is due to increased susceptibility among children who return to school after summer break, leading to higher transmission in densely populated urban areas
Evidence: The majority of notified cases (85%) occurred in individuals aged over 15 years (figure 2A).
The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).

Dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall. This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].
263.Rationale: The hypothesis that school return and increased susceptibility among children drive the October peak is inconsistent with the data: most reported cases are adults (85% over 15 years). The passage instead links the peak to climate-driven vector dynamics. There is no evidence in the passage that school return or child susceptibility timing explains the seasonal peak.

263.Option: The peak in dengue cases occurs in October because of increased human mobility during the autumn festival season, which enhances transmission in urban centers
Evidence: The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).

Dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall. This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].
All local climatic variables show a statistically significant association with dengue in the 1-year mode (figure 5, left panel).
263.Rationale: The passage attributes the seasonal timing to climatic influences on mosquito ecology and virus incubation rather than human mobility or festival-related movements. No evidence is provided linking autumn festivals or increased mobility to the October peak; instead strong evidence links temperature and rainfall seasonality (and resulting vector abundance) to the observed timing.

263.Option: The seasonal peak in dengue cases is due to a higher proportion of secondary dengue infections in October, which are more likely to result in severe clinical illness and thus be reported
Evidence: Dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall. This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].
The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).

Since dengue disease severity is modulated by age, prior dengue exposure history, and the infecting dengue serotype, the incidence of clinically apparent dengue cases is unlikely to have a linear relationship with transmission intensity [38], [39], [53].
263.Rationale: While severity can be influenced by secondary infections, the passage does not present evidence that the proportion of secondary infections specifically rises in October. Instead it links the timing of the peak to climate-driven increases in vector abundance and transmission with a 1–2 month lag. The statement that clinical incidence may not scale linearly with transmission does not support the specific claim that October peaks are driven by more secondary infections being reported.

263.Option: Seasonal increases in temperature and rainfall enhance mosquito abundance and virus transmission, leading to a delayed peak in cases due to the extrinsic incubation period
Evidence: The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).

Dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall. This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].
On average, a delay of 1–2 months was observed for rainfall and mean temperature whereas a 4–5 months delay was seen for humidity and wind velocity (figure 5, right panel).
263.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies seasonal increases in temperature and rainfall as drivers of mosquito abundance and transmission, and explains the observed delay (peak in October) by the lag introduced by vector population dynamics and the extrinsic incubation period. The reported 1–2 month delay between climate increases and case increases directly supports this explanation.

264.Option: The morphological similarity between Amphimerus sp. and Haplorchis pumilio metacercariae allows for cross-protection in human hosts, reducing the need for frequent exposure to maintain infection
Evidence: Eggs from the families Opisthorchiidae and Heterophyidae measuring <30 μm in length have been labeled as small trematode eggs because their morphological similarities prevent detailed discrimination via light microscopic diagnosis [17–19].

Because we can barely rely on morphology to distinguish eggs or metacercariae from members of the Opisthorchiidae and Heterophyidae families, we recommend to implement the PCR-RFLP molecular approach presented in this study in epidemiological surveys aiming to identify fish species involved in Amphimerus sp. life cycle (Fig 5) and to survey others regions of Ecuador where consumption of undercooked freshwater fish is frequent in order to effectively discriminate its metacercariae in a laboratory context (Fig 4).
264.Rationale: The passage documents morphological similarity that complicates diagnosis and species discrimination, but it gives no evidence that morphological similarity confers immunological cross-protection in human hosts. The cited text describes diagnostic difficulty and recommends molecular methods; it does not suggest immunity or reduced need for exposure. Therefore the claim of cross-protection and reduced exposure requirement is unsupported by the passage and biologically unfounded.

264.Option: The widespread presence of morphologically indistinguishable metacercariae in multiple edible fish species undermines accurate detection and enables sustained transmission even in low-fish-consumption communities
Evidence: All fish species were positive for Amphimerus sp. metacercariae.

We have isolated and identified metacercariae of Amphimerus sp. in four edible freshwater fish species collected in the area (i.e., R. altipinna, B. bucay, A. rivulatus, and P. aureoguttata; Fig 3 and Table 2), incriminating for the first time second intermediate hosts for Amphimerus sp.

Because we can barely rely on morphology to distinguish eggs or metacercariae from members of the Opisthorchiidae and Heterophyidae families, we recommend to implement the PCR-RFLP molecular approach presented in this study in epidemiological surveys aiming to identify fish species involved in Amphimerus sp. life cycle (Fig 5)...
The high prevalence of 35.8% in the present study corresponds to a population of mestizo inhabitants ... in this area, use of fish as a daily food source is secondary.
264.Rationale: The passage shows metacercariae of Amphimerus sp. occur in multiple edible fish species and that morphology alone is insufficient to distinguish them from other small trematode metacercariae, undermining routine detection. It also notes a high human prevalence despite fish being a secondary daily food source in this population, supporting the idea that widespread infection across multiple commonly eaten fish species (plus diagnostic challenges) can sustain human transmission even where overall fish consumption is lower.

264.Option: The presence of multiple fish species as second intermediate hosts increases the likelihood of zoonotic spillover, enabling transmission even in the absence of definitive animal hosts
Evidence: The vertebrate definitive host (e.g., mammals) ingests the metacercariae in fish bodies, and the larva inside the metacercarial cyst wall escapes and migrates from the intestines to the biliary ducts where it matures to become adult parasite and starts laying eggs [5,10,24,26].
We isolated 18 and 45 adult flukes from biliary ducts of the dog and cat livers respectively.
264.Rationale: While multiple fish species as second intermediate hosts do increase opportunities for zoonotic spillover to humans, the parasite life cycle requires definitive vertebrate hosts (which develop adult flukes and shed eggs) to be maintained. The passage explicitly describes the need for a vertebrate definitive host and documents infected dogs and cats in the area. Thus transmission cannot be sustained 'in the absence of definitive animal hosts' as the option states; that portion of the claim is biologically incorrect relative to the passage.

264.Option: The consumption of raw fish, even in small quantities, ensures consistent exposure to infective metacercariae and maintains transmission regardless of fish species diversity
Evidence: when freshwater fish are eaten, they are prepared as raw fish with salt and lemon juice (Fig 5). Consumption of infected fish to sustain or complement diets for the populations in Esmeraldas and Manabí, respectively, is an ideal means of maintaining the life cycle of Amphimerus sp. and potentially other trematodes [13,45].

We found metacercariae of Amphimerus sp. in all fish species recognized by local people as edible, belonging to three different families (i.e., Characidae, Cichlidae, and Lebiasinidae, Fig 3 and Table 2).
The high prevalence of 35.8% in the present study corresponds to a population of mestizo inhabitants ... in this area, use of fish as a daily food source is secondary.
264.Rationale: The passage supports that eating raw fish is a key route for human infection and that even occasional consumption can maintain transmission. However, the option's absolute phrasing 'regardless of fish species diversity' overstates the case. The text emphasizes that multiple fish species are infected and that species identity and preparation practices matter; fish species diversity therefore influences exposure risk. Because the option makes an overbroad claim beyond what the passage supports, it is not correct as stated.

264.Option: The high prevalence of small trematode eggs in human populations is primarily due to the use of contaminated water sources for fish collection, which sustains the life cycle across communities
Evidence: Briefly, eggs arriving into freshwater water sources (e.g., streams, lakes, ponds) are ingested by prosobranch molluscs, the first intermediate hosts, in which miracidia hatch and migrate along the digestive tract.

The vertebrate definitive host (e.g., mammals) ingests the metacercariae in fish bodies, and the larva inside the metacercarial cyst wall escapes and migrates from the intestines to the biliary ducts where it matures to become adult parasite and starts laying eggs [5,10,24,26].

PPG has a total population of ~3500 inhabitants, with an economy based mainly on cultivation of corn, coffee, and tagua nuts. As a rural population, they depend on their own vegetable crops, livestock, hunting, and fishing as food sources; they lack purified water [27,29].
264.Rationale: Although contaminated freshwater is part of the trematode life cycle (eggs entering water and infecting snails), the passage identifies ingestion of infected fish (metacercariae) as the critical transmission route to vertebrate definitive hosts and human infection. Lack of purified water is noted as a community characteristic, but the text does not attribute the high human prevalence primarily to water use for fish collection; instead it emphasizes eating infected fish and presence of infected fish species as the direct driver. Therefore the option's claim that contaminated water use for fish collection is the primary cause is not supported.

265.Option: The geographic overlap of human and animal cases in Sudan provides epidemiological evidence for cross-species transmission of actinomycetoma
Evidence: The patients were from Sudan, except for a patient from Yemen.

the clade including the potentially novel MRC012, and the clade including S. sudanensis isolates, both also included 16S rRNA sequences from strains isolated by Sengupta, Goodfellow and Hamid from lesions of donkeys with fistulous withers in Sudan (Fig 1C and 1E; NCBI accessions: EU544241.1, EU544239.1), suggesting that these strains may be capable of infecting multiple hosts thereby raising the possibility of cross-species transmission of actinomycetoma.
265.Rationale: While the passage notes that human patients and donkey isolates were both from Sudan, geographic co‑occurrence alone is weak evidence for cross‑species transmission. The authors emphasize sequence-based phylogenetic clustering (shared 16S rRNA sequences) as the basis for suggesting cross‑species potential, not mere geographic overlap. Thus, geographic overlap by itself does not most strongly support the hypothesis.

265.Option: The ability of S. sudanensis and S. kordofanensis to cause disease in both humans and animals indicates that they are naturally adapted to multiple hosts
Evidence: Streptomyces can cause mycetoma in animals [25,26], as well as fistulous withers in donkeys [27].
These relationships are discussed in more detail in light of the whole genome data considered below.
The type and only strain, MRC013T, was isolated from granulomatous material of mycetoma lesions of a patient in the Sudan.
265.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that some Streptomyces strains (including S. sudanensis–related sequences) cluster with isolates from donkey lesions, but it does not show that S. kordofanensis (MRC013) has been isolated from animals. In fact, MRC013 (S. kordofanensis) is described as 'the type and only strain' isolated from a human patient. Therefore the statement that both S. sudanensis and S. kordofanensis cause disease in both humans and animals is not supported by the text; only genetic similarity between some human isolates and previously reported donkey isolates is reported for certain lineages, not for S. kordofanensis.

265.Option: The presence of identical antibiotic resistance genes in actinomycetoma isolates from humans and donkeys suggests a shared transmission route across species
Evidence: we scanned the whole genome sequences for known antibiotic resistance determinants using AMRfinderPLUS (S3 Fig). This indicated the presence of rifampicin resistance genes in A. madurae, a result in agreement with the observed IC profiles.
The grains for culture were collected from confirmed mycetoma patients seen at the Mycetoma Research Centre, University of Khartoum.

the clade including the potentially novel MRC012, and the clade including S. sudanensis isolates, both also included 16S rRNA sequences from strains isolated by Sengupta, Goodfellow and Hamid from lesions of donkeys with fistulous withers in Sudan (Fig 1C and 1E; NCBI accessions: EU544241.1, EU544239.1), suggesting that these strains may be capable of infecting multiple hosts thereby raising the possibility of cross-species transmission of actinomycetoma.
265.Rationale: The passage describes antibiotic resistance gene findings for the sequenced human isolates and reports phylogenetic similarity to donkey 16S rRNA sequences, but it does not present data on antibiotic resistance genes from donkey isolates nor report identical resistance genes shared across human and donkey samples. Therefore there is no evidence in the text that identical resistance genes in both hosts support a shared transmission route.

265.Option: The shared phenotypic characteristics of actinomycetoma in humans and donkeys, such as granulomatous lesions and sinus formation, support the hypothesis of cross-species transmission
Evidence: Mycetoma is characterised by a painless mass with multiple sinuses which discharge material including grains which are colonies of the causal agents.
Streptomyces can cause mycetoma in animals [25,26], as well as fistulous withers in donkeys [27].

Isolates related to S. somaliensis (Fig 1A), as well as potentially novel actinomycetoma agents MRC002 and MRC012, were part of clades with 16S rRNA identified in Sudan soil samples [48], consistent with the described primary route of infection by traumatic inoculation of bacteria from the environment.
265.Rationale: Phenotypic similarity (granulomatous lesions, sinuses) between human and animal disease is consistent with a shared pathology, but it is relatively nonspecific and can arise from unrelated pathogens producing similar lesions. The passage points to genetic sequence similarity (shared 16S rRNA clustering between human isolates and donkey isolates) as the key evidence raising the possibility of cross‑species transmission. Therefore shared phenotype alone is not the most persuasive line of evidence presented.

265.Option: Genetic similarity between actinomycetoma isolates from human patients and those from donkeys with fistulous withers supports the possibility of cross-species transmission
Evidence: the clade including the potentially novel MRC012, and the clade including S. sudanensis isolates, both also included 16S rRNA sequences from strains isolated by Sengupta, Goodfellow and Hamid from lesions of donkeys with fistulous withers in Sudan (Fig 1C and 1E; NCBI accessions: EU544241.1, EU544239.1), suggesting that these strains may be capable of infecting multiple hosts thereby raising the possibility of cross-species transmission of actinomycetoma.

It is particularly interesting that isolate MRCO12 forms a well-defined clade (Fig 1E) with strains isolated from soil samples from Sudan and from an organism isolated from lesions of a donkey with withers (EU544241.1;), as mentioned above.

identification of isolates of S. sudanensis and S. warraenensis with similarity to strains previously isolated from donkey withers by Sengupta, Goodfellow and Hamid (NCBI accessions EU544231.1 to EU544241.1) suggests that some actinomycetoma causing strains may have the potential to infect multiple host species [48].
265.Rationale: The passage explicitly cites phylogenetic evidence (shared/closely related 16S rRNA sequences and clade membership) linking human clinical isolates to strains previously isolated from donkey lesions. The authors interpret these genetic relationships as indicating that 'these strains may be capable of infecting multiple hosts' and that this 'raises the possibility of cross‑species transmission.' Genetic similarity between isolates from different host species is the strongest line of evidence presented in the text for potential cross‑species transmission.

266.Option: Temporal clustering of infections in the same year led to genetic homogenization, preventing the detection of geographic differentiation in northern Ethiopia
Evidence: There was however, no indication for temporal clustering as we still found high inbreeding for NE strains isolated in the same year but with no significant differentiation between different years of isolation.

When the strains from SE were divided into two groups, SE-2007 and SE-2008, and strains from NE into three groups, NE-2007, NE-2008 and NE-2009, according to the year of their isolation, the FIS for each division remained clearly positive.
266.Rationale: The passage explicitly states there was no indication for temporal clustering and that high inbreeding persisted for NE strains isolated in the same year without significant differentiation between years. Thus temporal clustering causing homogenization is not supported by the study's findings.

266.Option: The use of microsatellite markers with limited discriminatory power prevented the detection of genetic differences between strains isolated in the same year and geographic focus in northern Ethiopia
Evidence: Microsatellite markers have proved to be the most powerful tools for population genetic studies in Leishmania [16], [26], [27].

The MLMT approach has been shown to combine high discriminatory power with good reproducibility allowing the comparison of profiles identified in different laboratories and at different times [26], [27].
Therefore, a panel of 14 microsatellite markers was used in the present study for elucidating the genetic diversity, the population structure and the reproductive system of L. donovani in Ethiopia
266.Rationale: The authors argue that microsatellite multilocus typing (MLMT) is highly discriminatory and appropriate for detecting strain-level differences. They used 14 highly polymorphic microsatellite markers for this study, so limited marker power is not the likely reason for failure to detect differentiation in the northern focus.

266.Option: The presence of high inbreeding in northern Ethiopia led to reduced genetic diversity, which masked detectable genetic differentiation between strains isolated in the same year and geographic focus
Evidence: In both East African populations identified herein, a considerable deficit of heterozygotes (He > Ho, FIS >0) was observed for both single-locus and multilocus data (Table S3).
The expected heterozygosity (He) which is a measure for genetic diversity was slightly higher in strains of L. donovani from NE/SD (He = 0.47) as compared to strains from SE/KE (He = 0.39).

Further, we analysed Ethiopian strains of L. donovani alone defined by year of isolation and geographic origin. First, we calculated FIS values for the NE (n = 41) and SE (n = 22) populations separately. These values FIS = 0.42 for NE and FIS = 0.83 for SE, were similar to those obtained when Ethiopian and Sudanese (n = 65) as well as Ethiopian and Kenyan strain (n = 30) were taken together (Table S4).
266.Rationale: Although inbreeding (positive FIS) is reported, the expected heterozygosity (He) in NE/SD is actually slightly higher than in SE/KE, indicating substantial genetic diversity remains. The passage does not attribute lack of detectable differentiation within northern-year/focus samples to reduced diversity from inbreeding; instead it points to other causes (e.g., mobility/misclassification).

266.Option: Misclassification of infection location due to frequent travel between foci obscured true genetic differentiation in northern Ethiopia
Evidence: No correlation could be found between the geographical origin of strains and the two subpopulations and the 5 clusters in population NE/SD. This is in agreement with a recent MLMT-based population genetic study on Sudanese strains of L. donovani [44] and consistent with high rate of population movement between these Sudanese and Ethiopian foci [45].

We did not re-calculate FIS values for the NE strains isolated in the same year in same focus. Because most of our patients were soldiers and immigrant workers and had to travel frequently the two foci Humera and Metema, it was impossible to accurately record where they got infected.
266.Rationale: The authors explicitly cite frequent travel of patients between northern foci and the inability to accurately record infection location as a reason that precluded re-analysis and likely obscured geographic signal. High population movement and misclassification of infection location would blur genetic differences tied to place of infection, making detection of differentiation within the same nominal focus/year unlikely.

266.Option: The Wahlund effect due to unmeasured micro-geographic sub-structuring was responsible for the lack of detectable genetic differentiation in northern Ethiopia
Evidence: To see the effect of temporal and geographic origin of strains we divided the Ethiopian L. donovani strains by year of isolation and endemic focus and re-calculated the FIS values for each subdivision. A significant decrease in FIS compared to FIS values calculated for the subpopulations inferred by STRUCTURE indicates the presence of Wahlund effect due to host, temporal or micro-geographic sub-structuring [16].

For the BAPS clusters of the population NE/SD the FIS values were lower than those estimated for the NE/SD subpopulations and clusters inferred by STRUCTURE, indicating that the latter were at least partly due to population subdivision (Wahlund effect).

We did not re-calculate FIS values for the NE strains isolated in the same year in same focus. Because most of our patients were soldiers and immigrant workers and had to travel frequently the two foci Humera and Metema, it was impossible to accurately record where they got infected.
266.Rationale: While the Wahlund effect (population subdivision) was considered and BAPS clustering reduced FIS in NE/SD, the passage indicates the main impediment to detecting differentiation within NE same-year/same-focus samples was inability to assign infection location due to frequent travel. Thus unmeasured micro-geographic sub-structuring (Wahlund effect) is not presented as the predominant reason for the specific failure described; misclassification from travel is.

267.Option: The increased risk of severe infection among individuals with intermediate and high IgG levels reflects a higher likelihood of re-infection with a more virulent strain of O. tsutsugamushi, which is more likely to cause severe disease regardless of prior immunity.
Evidence: Given the great strain diversity of O. tsutsugamushi, repeated infection with a variety of genetic variants may be a common phenomenon, possibly explaining some recent findings regarding the sero-epidemiology of scrub typhus.

Variations in strain diversity across settings, and the sequence of strains causing primary infection and re-infection may influence the risk of severe infection [32] and perhaps the way in which one infection may enhance subsequent infection.

Assuming that high initial IgG antibody levels reflect IgG antibody levels due to prior infections, we hypothesised that pre-existing antibodies to O. tsutsugamushi may increase the risk of severe infection.
In dengue fever, prior infection is known to cause more severe subsequent infections, through a mechanism called antibody-dependent enhancement.
267.Rationale: While the passage acknowledges strain diversity and that strain sequence may influence severity (first two quotes), the authors explicitly hypothesise that pre-existing antibodies could increase risk and directly invoke antibody-dependent enhancement (third and fourth quotes). The option attributes the higher severity primarily to infection with a more virulent strain independent of prior immunity, which is not the interpretation the authors emphasize as most biologically plausible; they highlight immune-enhancement mechanisms (ADE) as a key plausible explanation. Therefore this option is not the best match to the biologically plausible mechanism discussed in the paper.

267.Option: The observed association between higher initial IgG levels and increased severity is best explained by a stronger immune response triggered by more severe infection, leading to higher antibody production as a consequence rather than a cause of disease severity.
Evidence: the absence of data on pre-infection IgG levels in the cases included in this study makes it difficult to exclude the possibility of reverse causality as the main limitation of this study.

High bacterial loads in severe cases may accelerate IgG response even in those who have never been infected with O. tsutsugamushi before, which would lead to an observed association between IgG levels and risk of severe infection.
267.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes this reverse-causality explanation and frames it as an important limitation (both quoted lines). However, the question asks which interpretation is most consistent with the biological plausibility of immune enhancement mechanisms seen in other diseases. Although reverse causality is plausible and acknowledged by the authors, it represents an alternative explanation rather than the interpretation most consistent with immune-enhancement mechanisms (the authors specifically compare to dengue ADE). Therefore this option is not the answer that best aligns with immune-enhancement plausibility.

267.Option: The association between higher IgG levels and increased severity is due to the presence of maternal antibodies in young children, which may interfere with protective immune responses and lead to more severe disease upon infection.
Evidence: Clinical studies exploring IgG antibody levels in mothers of very young scrub typhus cases may be used to estimate the effect of maternal antibodies on the severity of infection in the child.
267.Rationale: The passage suggests maternal-antibody studies as a possible future direction (quoted), but it does not present evidence that maternal antibodies cause enhanced severity in scrub typhus. The main biologically plausible mechanism discussed in the paper is antibody-dependent enhancement from prior infections in the host (not maternal antibody interference). Thus the maternal-antibody interpretation is speculative and not the interpretation most consistent with the immune-enhancement mechanisms emphasized by the authors.

267.Option: The association between higher initial IgG levels and increased severity is most plausibly explained by antibody-dependent enhancement from prior infection, similar to mechanisms observed in dengue fever
Evidence: Assuming that high initial IgG antibody levels reflect IgG antibody levels due to prior infections, we hypothesised that pre-existing antibodies to O. tsutsugamushi may increase the risk of severe infection.

If IgG antibodies to O. tsutsugamushi in the early phase of illness largely reflect pre-infection levels, then our findings may suggest that a scrub typhus infection in the past may increase the risk of severe infection in subsequent episodes.
The effect of initial IgG levels on the risk of severe infection was more pronounced in cases tested within these 7 days than in those tested later (Table 3 and Fig 4).
In dengue fever, prior infection is known to cause more severe subsequent infections, through a mechanism called antibody-dependent enhancement.
267.Rationale: These quotes show the authors explicitly hypothesise that pre-existing IgG could increase risk of severe scrub typhus and that the pattern of higher risk with higher initial IgG—especially when testing occurred early (so IgG likely reflects pre-infection levels)—is compatible with a mechanism analogous to dengue antibody-dependent enhancement. Therefore this interpretation directly aligns with the biological plausibility and comparisons discussed in the paper and is the best-supported interpretation in that context.

267.Option: The higher IgG levels observed in severe cases are a result of delayed diagnosis and prolonged antigen exposure, leading to a more robust and sustained humoral response that correlates with disease severity.
Evidence: Both the 33rd and the 67th fitted OD percentiles showed a gradual increase with longer duration of fever (Fig 2B). For the median percentile, there was initially a gradual increase with an accelerated increase after day 7.
The effect of initial IgG levels on the risk of severe infection was more pronounced in cases tested within these 7 days than in those tested later (Table 3 and Fig 4).
the absence of data on pre-infection IgG levels in the cases included in this study makes it difficult to exclude the possibility of reverse causality as the main limitation of this study.
267.Rationale: The passage does show IgG levels rise with longer fever duration (first quote), and the authors acknowledge reverse causality and accelerated antibody production with severe disease (third quote). However, the observed pattern—stronger association when testing was done within 7 days (second quote)—argues against delayed diagnosis/prolonged exposure as the primary explanation for the association, because the association is actually stronger when testing was early (before substantial rise due to current infection). Thus while delayed testing and prolonged antigen exposure could contribute in some cases, this interpretation is not the most consistent with the biological enhancement mechanism emphasized by the authors.

268.Option: The expression of Rp-piwi2, Rp-piwi3, and Rp-ago3 in both germline and somatic tissues suggests a broader functional role that may increase vulnerability to environmental stressors in Rhodnius compared to species with germline-restricted expression
Evidence: we show that Rp-piwi2, Rp-piwi3 and Rp-ago3, but not Rp-piwi1, are expressed in ovaries.
In addition, the Rp-piwi2 transcripts are detected also in the somatic follicle cells, thus suggesting that the expression of this gene is not restricted to the germline tissues (Fig 4C').

Similar to Rp-vas, the Rp-piwi3 and Rp-ago3 transcripts are detected in the region of the tropharium hosting the polyploid Ts and in the ooplasm of newly formed and mature egg chambers, but not in the follicle cells (Fig 4D and 4E).
268.Rationale: The option asserts that Rp-piwi2, Rp-piwi3 and Rp-ago3 are all expressed in both germline and somatic tissues. The passage, however, indicates only Rp-piwi2 is detected in somatic follicle cells; Rp-piwi3 and Rp-ago3 are described as germline-specific and "not in the follicle cells." Because the factual premise (all three being expressed in both compartments) is incorrect according to the passage, the proposed inference about broader functional role and increased vulnerability based on that premise is not supported.

268.Option: The presence of a lineage-specific PolyQ tract in Rp-Piwi2 may confer unique functional dependence on this protein for fertility, increasing susceptibility to reproductive failure in Triatomine species compared to those lacking the tract
Evidence: we show that Rp-piwi2, Rp-piwi3 and Rp-ago3, but not Rp-piwi1, are expressed in ovaries.
Interestingly, the N-terminal region of the putative Rp-Piwi2 protein features a stretch of 18 Glutamine residues (i.e. polyQ tract) (Fig 3A and S2 Fig).

Using NCBI Blast search analyses, we found that the polyQ tract is present in Piwi-like proteins encoded in the genome of Rhodnius neglectus, Triatoma infestans and Triatoma dimidiata (Fig 3B), but it is absent in PIWI proteins of Drosophila and other animals (Fig 3A and S2 Fig).
Our functional studies using pRNAi against the Rp-piwi2 gene resulted in oogenesis arrest and complete female adult sterility.

Thus, the PolyQ tract is likely a recent acquisition in the evolution of the PIWI proteins and, based on the available sequenced genomes, appears to be restricted to blood-feeding insects of the Triatomine family.
268.Rationale: The passage documents that Rp-piwi2 is essential for oogenesis and female fertility (pRNAi causes sterility) and that Rp-Piwi2 uniquely contains an 18-residue polyglutamine (PolyQ) tract that is present in closely related Triatomine species but absent from Drosophila and other animals. A lineage-restricted protein feature that resides in a protein shown to be functionally essential provides a compelling molecular basis for species-specific dependence: if the PolyQ tract alters Piwi2 function or interactions, Triatomines could have a unique reliance on that version of the protein for fertility. Therefore, the PolyQ tract is a strong candidate molecular feature explaining differential susceptibility to reproductive failure across related species.

268.Option: The absence of the RDC complex in Rhodnius, except for a putative Rp-Rai1l protein, suggests that the piRNA pathway is less robust in Triatomine species, making them more susceptible to reproductive failure than Drosophila
Evidence: We did not find evidence of an RDC complex in Rhodnius except for a putative protein (i.e. Rp-Rai1l) with similarity to Cuff.
If piRNA clusters exist in this species, it is likely that their regulation relies on a set of proteins different from the one described in Drosophila.

However, we provide evidence that several factors involved in the transport and processing of the pre-piRNAs, including Uap56, Krimper and Maelstrom among others, are expressed during Rhodnius oogenesis.
268.Rationale: While the passage states an RDC complex like that in Drosophila was not found (aside from a Rai1-like protein), it explicitly cautions that Rhodnius may use a different set of proteins to regulate piRNA clusters and notes that many core piRNA pathway factors are present and expressed. The mere absence of the Drosophila-style RDC complex therefore does not directly imply a globally "less robust" pathway or greater susceptibility to reproductive failure; rather, it indicates pathway divergence. That makes the option's strong causal claim unsupported by the presented evidence.

268.Option: The high expression of hsp83 and uap56 in Rhodnius ovaries indicates a reliance on chaperone and RNA export functions that may make these species more vulnerable to proteotoxic stress during oogenesis compared to Drosophila
Evidence: we immediately noticed that the heat shock protein 83 (hsp83) and uap56 gene, which encode a nuclear-cytoplasmic RNA export factor, are expressed at higher levels (>500 RPKM) than other piRNA pathway components in Rhodnius ovaries (Fig 2A).
Our transcriptomic analysis revealed that several components of the piRNA pathway are conserved and expressed in the ovary of this species.
268.Rationale: Although hsp83 and uap56 are highly expressed in Rhodnius ovaries, the passage does not link elevated expression of these genes to increased vulnerability to proteotoxic stress or to interspecies differences in reproductive failure. High expression may reflect normal physiological demand (e.g., high transcription/translation during previtellogenic stages) rather than a fragility. The option infers a susceptibility that is not supported by explicit evidence in the text, so it is not the best-supported molecular feature for differential susceptibility.

268.Option: The presence of a mariner-like transposase in the intron of Rp-vas suggests that genomic instability may be a key factor in reproductive failure, making Triatomine species more susceptible than those without such elements
Evidence: the Rp-vas intron 1 harbors an Open Reading Frame (ORF) encoding a transposase enzyme from a mariner-like element (Fig 2D).
Transcripts of this ORF are readily detected in our ovarian transcriptome datasets.
The percentage of transposable elements in the Rhodnius genome is approximately 6% and two thirds of the transposons in this species belong to the mariner family [41,50,51].
It is tempting to speculate that the DNA damage and the loss of Ts observed in the Rp-piwi2 KD ovaries might be caused by the deregulation of transposable elements.
Rp-piwi2 might be required to silence these elements in the germline and, possibly, in the somatic tissues.
268.Rationale: The passage documents a mariner-like transposase ORF within an intron of Rp-vas that is transcribed, and it notes a high representation of mariner elements in the Rhodnius genome. The authors link the observed DNA damage and trophocyte loss in Rp-piwi2 knockdowns to possible deregulation of transposable elements and state that Rp-piwi2 may be required to silence these elements. A lineage-specific burden or insertion of active transposons (e.g., mariner) therefore provides a plausible mechanistic basis for increased genomic instability and reproductive failure in some species versus others that lack such elements. This makes the mariner insertion/genome composition a strong candidate feature explaining interspecies differences in susceptibility.

269.Option: Airborne transmission of C. burnetii via windborne aerosols enables long-distance spread independent of local study coverage
Evidence: Transmission to human most frequently occurs through inhalation of aerosolized bacteria that are spread in the environment by infected animals after delivery or abortion. Such windborne outbreaks can affect dozens to hundreds of people who may not have direct exposure to the infected animals.

The ability of Q fever pathogenic agents (Coxiella burnetii) to be transmitted from animal reservoirs to humans by inhalation of infected aerosols makes coxiellosis easily transmissible among different organisms over large geographical areas [165]. Indeed, airborne transmission of C. burnetii is a well-documented phenomenon in many regions across the world [166, 167].
269.Rationale: While the passage clearly documents that C. burnetii can spread long distances via windborne aerosols and cause widespread outbreaks independent of local animal contacts, the question specifically asks about diseases transmitted by hard-bodied arthropods (Ixodid ticks). Airborne aerosol spread is a major transmission route for C. burnetii but is not a transmission mechanism for most tick-borne pathogens. Therefore, although the quotes support long-distance spread of C. burnetii, they do not directly support widespread transmission of the tick‑borne diseases the question emphasizes.

269.Option: The presence of multiple tick species in Iran, including those capable of transmitting pathogens, indicates that transmission can occur across diverse ecological zones despite limited study coverage
Evidence: Ticks are considered second only to mosquitoes as important vectors of human infectious diseases across the world [99]. The obligate hematophagous arthropods parasitize every class of vertebrates in almost every region of the world [15].

According to Shemshad et al. [103], a number of Ixodid tick fauna obtained from cattle, sheep, goats, dogs and even shrubbery have been identified in different parts of Iran. The ticks include: Haemaphysalis concinna, H. sulcata, Hyalomma anatolicum, Hy. asiaticum, Hy. detritum, Hy. dromedarii, Hy. marginatum, Hy. schulzei, Rhipicephalus bursa, R. sanguineus, Ixodes ricinus and Dermacentor spp.

The presence of a number of Ixodid ticks in Iran therefore implies that there could be many natural foci of tickborne zoonoses in the country from which they may spread to other areas under changing socio-economic and climatic conditions.
269.Rationale: The passage explicitly documents a wide diversity and geographic presence of Ixodid (hard) tick species across Iran and describes ticks as major vectors that parasitize vertebrates in nearly every region. That diversity and distribution of competent tick vectors is the most direct ecological/epidemiological factor supporting potential for widespread transmission of tick-borne pathogens even though surveillance/studies have limited geographic coverage.

269.Option: The detection of C. burnetii in animal products such as milk and eggs suggests that the pathogen can be widely disseminated through food chains, enabling transmission across regions despite limited study coverage
Evidence: Among the animal products, three studies investigated C. burnetii antibodies in bulk milk samples; two from dairy cattle and one from dairy goats.

Collectively, the investigated ticks belonged to nine different species, namely: Rhipicephalus sanguineus sensu lato, R. turanicus, R. bursa, Hyalomma anatolicum, H. excavatum, H. asiaticum, H. marginatum, H. dromedarii, H. detritum.
Coxiella burnetii bacteria were detected in three (all located to the southeastern part of the country) of the four studies, with a pooled prevalence 4.8–13.1% being reported.

Coxiella burnetii bacterium has a wide range of hosts including wild and domestic mammals, birds, reptiles and arthropods [169]. However, domestic ruminants (primarily cattle, sheep and goats) are the most important and frequent source of human infection of C. burnetii [170].
269.Rationale: Detection of C. burnetii in milk and eggs in the passage demonstrates that foodborne or product-mediated dissemination is possible for C. burnetii specifically. However, the question targets diseases transmitted by hard-bodied arthropods (ticks). While contaminated animal products support spread of Q fever, they do not generalize to most tick-borne pathogens (which typically require vector-host contact). Thus this factor is relevant to C. burnetii but is not the strongest support for widespread transmission of the suite of tick‑borne diseases described.

269.Option: The presence of multiple tick-borne pathogens in the same geographic regions suggests that transmission foci are interconnected and capable of spreading across Iran through shared vector populations
Evidence: Of the 63 eligible studies that were reviewed, 36 (57.1%) reported on coxiellosis (Q fever), 15 (23.8%) on anaplasmosis, 11 (17.5%) on ehrlichiosis and 1 (1.6%) on SFG rickettsioses. Collectively, the different studies were reported in 22 (70.9%) of the 31 provinces of Iran,
However 9 of the 22 provinces had up to three diseases (anaplasmosis, ehrlichiosis and coxiellosis) reported jointly within a single province.

The presence of various pathogens was confirmed by molecular, serological and microscopic techniques conducted on specimens obtained from humans, ticks, domestic animals and animal products (milk and eggs) drawn from a number of provinces in Iran.

The presence of various pathogens was confirmed ... in 22 out of the 31 provinces in Iran as reported by various studies implies that the diseases caused by these pathogens could be highly prevalent in the country.
