Evidence: Altogether, the frequency of all detected minor variants was almost below 20% (231/237) (Fig 2A).
The major proportion of the variants in primary syphilis samples was in the 1–5% (181/237) range, and the lowest was in the 10–60% (22/237) range.

This feature may represent a logical fitness-based evolution where high-frequency sequences are better fitted to avoid immune recognition and numerous low-frequency minor variants may simply emerge and most of them would likely disappear if they were not advantageous for syphilis developing [37].
It is worth noting that the sequences appearing between the frequency of 20–80% were mainly concentrated in the V2, V5, V6 and V7 regions mostly from X-6, 8, 10, 13 (Fig 2).
296.Rationale: The option asserts minor variants are primarily below 1%, but the passage states the major proportion of minor variants lie in the 1–5% range. Therefore the factual premise of the option is incorrect. Although the passage does note many low-frequency variants (below Sanger detection), it specifies them mainly at 1–5%, not below 1%, so the option's claim is unsupported by the text.

296.Option: The high frequency of a single predominant sequence in most V regions, especially V1, indicates that immune pressure stabilizes a conserved antigenic profile early in infection
Evidence: As Fig 2A shows, there was a predominant sequence in each V region of ten samples directly from primary syphilis patients, and the proportion of this sequence was almost above 80%.
Among the seven V regions, V1 and V6 were found to have the lowest and highest variability, respectively (Figs 1 and 2A), which was consistent with the findings of previous studies [24, 33].

This feature may represent a logical fitness-based evolution where high-frequency sequences are better fitted to avoid immune recognition and numerous low-frequency minor variants may simply emerge and most of them would likely disappear if they were not advantageous for syphilis developing [37].
296.Rationale: While the passage documents a high-frequency predominant sequence in most V regions (including V1), the authors interpret this as a fitness-based outcome (better-fitted sequences surviving) rather than direct evidence that immune pressure 'stabilizes' the antigenic profile early. Moreover, the passage emphasizes that immune-driven change is seen where minor variants increase (e.g., V6 and other regions), so the mere presence of a predominant sequence does not most strongly support the hypothesis that immune pressure drives diversity during early infection.

296.Option: The increase in frequency of minor variants in V6, a region with the highest genetic diversity, during early infection suggests immune-driven selection for antigenic escape
Evidence: The number of distinct sequences in the seven regions of the tprK gene ranged from 21–76, with the highest number in V6 and the lowest in V1 across all samples (Fig 1 and S2 Table).

While the frequency of the predominant sequence in some V regions of four samples (X-6, 8, 10, 13) was lower than 60%, and the frequency ranged from 20–60%. Then the frequency was found to be decreased in the V2, V5, V6 and V7 regions, and the frequency in V6 of X-6 was even lower at 20.8%.

This observation corresponded to the lower proportion of their predominant sequences. The distribution pattern of these variants from these samples may suggest that with disease progression or increasing immunity, the balance of the original sequence distribution was broken and some V regions (e.g., V2, V5, V6 and V7) began to change. As a result, a minor variant (or a new variant) became advantageous and its frequency gradually increased, ultimately replacing the original predominant sequence, which further promoted the antigenic diversity of TprK for T. pallidum to escape immune clearance and potentially leading to the development of late syphilis, neurosyphilis or serofast status [15, 21, 38, 39].

Additionally, among these four V regions, the frequency of the predominant sequence in V6 was particularly low (Fig 2A), suggesting that V6 may be the first affected region and is involved in immune evasion during the course of infection [21, 24].
296.Rationale: The passage directly links V6 as the most diverse region and documents samples in which the predominant sequence frequency in V6 is markedly reduced (e.g., 20.8%), and it explicitly interprets such shifts as consistent with disease progression or increasing immunity driving replacement of predominant sequences by minor variants. These statements most directly support immune-driven selection producing antigenic escape in early infection, making this option correct.

296.Option: The presence of multiple length variants in V3, V6, and V7, which are the most variable regions, indicates that structural changes in TprK are driven by immune pressure to evade host recognition
Evidence: The length of the captured sequences within each V region was also found to be variable, particularly in V3, V6 and V7, with 11 or 12 forms.

The size range of the captured sequences was the largest for V3, V6 and V7, which was similar to the findings of Pinto et al. [24], demonstrating that the variations in these three regions could more easily cause changes in length.

Additionally, it was interesting that the length of all distinct sequences differed by only 3 bp or multiples of 3 bp, and previous research data also supported this pattern change [21, 24]. A change pattern characterized multiple of 3 bp matched the triplet codon in protein coding, which has made us think that this feature probably explains why it is rare to uncover a tprK frame shift.
296.Rationale: While the passage documents multiple length variants in V3, V6, and V7, it frames this finding in terms of allowed reading-frame–preserving changes (multiples of 3 bp) and does not directly attribute length variation to immune pressure. The strongest evidence for immune-driven change in the text is the rising frequency of specific minor variants in certain regions (e.g., V6) with disease progression, rather than length heterogeneity per se. Therefore, although length variation is consistent with variability, it is not presented as the most direct support for immune selection driving antigenic diversity during early infection.

296.Option: The high inter-population redundancy of amino acid sequences in V1 and V4, despite overall tprK variability, suggests that immune pressure is strongest in these regions, leading to conserved antigenic profiles across patients
Evidence: In eight cases, two or more amino acid sequences were found to be identical in one sample although they were coded by different nucleotide sequences (S3 and S4 Tables).

When distinct sequences within each V region from each strain were compared, a scenario of sequence consistency was found. As Fig 3 shows, V1 and V4 presented a strong shared sequence capacity. The sequence IASDGGAIKH in V1 was observed in five strains (5/14) and DVGHKKENAANVNGTVGA in V4 was shared across seven strains (7/14).

To further explore whether the shared scenario was usually displayed by the predominant sequence across all the strains, we involved only the predominant sequence in the V region of each sample... V1 and V4 still presented similar shared sequence abilities despite the decreased redundant sequences. The occurrence of the consistent sequence in V1 and V4 could reach five strains and six strains, respectively (Table 2).

The similar findings that were observed between the two studies using different approaches to investigate the adaptive traits of the pathogen during different human infection were exciting and clearly suggest the existence of better fitted antigenic profiles to address the immune response of the host.
296.Rationale: The passage notes that V1 and V4 show inter-population redundancy (shared, conserved sequences) and suggests these may represent better-fitted antigenic profiles to deal with host immune responses and be vaccine targets. However, conserved sequences across patients do not indicate that immune pressure is strongest there driving diversity; rather they imply constraint or convergent fitness. The authors emphasize immune-driven diversification in other regions (e.g., V6) where minor variants increase. Thus this feature does not most strongly support the hypothesis that immune pressure drives antigenic diversity during early infection.

297.Option: Implement population-wide door-to-door screening every six months to detect all cases of PKDL and leprosy in the region
Evidence: Active case finding through door-to-door screening is probably the most effective way to find the few hidden PKDL cases but unfortunately will never be sustainable.
Our teams spent more than one year, with interruptions, to screen a population of approximately 33,000 and were able to examine only three-quarters of those.
297.Rationale: Although the passage states that door-to-door screening is likely the most effective way to find hidden PKDL cases, it explicitly notes that such an approach "will never be sustainable." The study team required over a year to screen ~33,000 people and still examined only 77% of the population, which argues against recommending routine population-wide door-to-door screening every six months.

297.Option: Prioritize screening for leprosy in households with a history of VL to detect potential PKDL cases due to shared risk factors
Evidence: An important condition in the differential diagnosis of PKDL is leprosy.
For leprosy, door-to-door screening could be considered when focusing on the immediate neighborhood of households in which cases have been identified.

A combination of targeted active case finding for leprosy, with screening for PKDL in hotspots of previous transmission, and regular skin camps seems the most realistic approach to tackle both diseases. For PKDL this should be combined with active follow-up of previously treated VL cases.
297.Rationale: The passage describes leprosy as an important differential diagnosis for PKDL and recommends targeting leprosy active case finding to households and neighbors of known leprosy cases, not households with a history of VL. PKDL surveillance should focus on hotspots of previous VL transmission and active follow-up of previously treated VL patients. Prioritizing leprosy screening specifically in households with prior VL is not supported by the text and conflates distinct targeting strategies.

297.Option: Use passive case finding through health facility reporting to identify new cases of PKDL and leprosy in areas with high leprosy prevalence
Evidence: Active case finding through door-to-door screening is probably the most effective way to find the few hidden PKDL cases but unfortunately will never be sustainable.
To address both leprosy and PKDL, skin camps may be a reasonable compromise between door-to-door screening and passive case finding.
297.Rationale: The passage argues that active case finding is most effective for finding hidden PKDL cases and suggests skin camps as a compromise between intensive active screening and passive case finding. Thus, relying primarily on passive facility-based reporting would be less likely to identify the hidden cases the question targets, especially for PKDL which can be missed without active outreach.

297.Option: Conduct targeted active case finding around known leprosy cases and follow up past VL patients for PKDL to detect hidden transmission risks
Evidence: For leprosy, door-to-door screening could be considered when focusing on the immediate neighborhood of households in which cases have been identified.
The apparent gradient was confirmed in the Poisson model with village as random effect.

To address both leprosy and PKDL, a combination of targeted active case finding for leprosy, with screening for PKDL in hotspots of previous transmission, and regular skin camps seems the most realistic approach to tackle both diseases. For PKDL this should be combined with active follow-up of previously treated VL cases.
297.Rationale: This option directly matches the study's recommended approach. The passage documents strong spatial clustering of leprosy at neighborhood level and recommends focusing active leprosy case finding on immediate households and neighbors. It also recommends screening for PKDL in hotspots of previous VL transmission and "active follow-up of previously treated VL cases." Combining targeted leprosy neighborhood screening with follow-up of past VL patients addresses both the spatial pattern and the PKDL risk and therefore is the most effective strategy described.

297.Option: Establish skin camps in villages with the highest historical VL incidence to detect PKDL and leprosy cases through opportunistic screening
Evidence: To address both leprosy and PKDL, skin camps may be a reasonable compromise between door-to-door screening and passive case finding.
The skin camps in our study were very well attended.

A combination of targeted active case finding for leprosy, with screening for PKDL in hotspots of previous transmission, and regular skin camps seems the most realistic approach to tackle both diseases.
297.Rationale: The passage supports skin camps as a pragmatic, well-attended compromise to reach cases that would be missed by passive surveillance, and explicitly recommends pairing targeted active case finding with screening for PKDL in "hotspots of previous transmission" and regular skin camps. Establishing skin camps in villages with the highest historical VL incidence therefore aligns with the authors' suggested, practical strategy to detect hidden PKDL and leprosy cases.

298.Option: The route of administration of the monoclonal antibody, such as intraperitoneal versus intravenous delivery
Evidence: The 5–6 week old female Balb/C mice from Charles River, (Quebec, Canada), were injected ip with the indicated amount of ZEBOV GP-specific MAbs in 100 µl PBS at the times indicated either before or after i.p. infection with 1,000 LD50 of MA-ZEBOV.
At the indicated times post-infection the guinea pigs were treated i.p. with 1 ml of the MAb diluted in PBS.
It is possible that since the MAbs and virus were both injected ip that the MAbs might inhibit ZEBOV infection of cells and extend life.
298.Rationale: While the study used intraperitoneal (i.p.) administration and notes it might have influenced outcomes ("It is possible that since the MAbs and virus were both injected ip..."), the passage emphasizes timing (1–2 dpi vs 3 dpi) and combination/dose as the decisive determinants of complete protection. The text does not present route of administration as the primary factor determining whether delayed treatment achieves complete protection; instead it treats route as a methodological note and a possible contributor, not the strongest influence.

298.Option: The presence of a neutralizing antibody response in the host's endogenous immune system at the time of treatment
Evidence: Both T and B cell immunity was analysed and it was believed that a strong early humoral immune response may have been a factor in survival [11], [16], [17].
Additionally, in fatally infected patients EBOV-specific IgG was absent, and IgM levels were low in comparison to the survivors [16].
The passive transfer of immune sera or whole blood was tested but its effectiveness is still controversial as it has not consistently provided protection [10].
298.Rationale: The passage acknowledges that endogenous humoral responses correlate with survival (e.g., "a strong early humoral immune response may have been a factor in survival" and differences in IgG/IgM between survivors and fatalities). However, in the experimental context of passive post-exposure therapy presented here, the dominant determinants of achieving complete protection with delayed treatment were timing and the use of an effective MAb combination (not merely the host's own antibody status). Thus while host endogenous neutralizing responses are relevant, the passage supports timing (treatment within ~2 days) as the strongest influence for delayed passive therapy.

298.Option: The total dose of monoclonal antibodies administered, regardless of timing relative to infection
Evidence: Since the MAbs were most effective when given 1 day after the lethal MA-ZEBOV infection, this treatment protocol was used to determine the most effective dose for protection (Table 2). A dose response was observed, and some MAbs were more potent than others for a given dose.
The lowest doses providing complete protection from lethality for 5D2, 5E6, 7C9, and 7G4 were 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 µg, respectively.
In general, the MAbs worked best for both animal models when given after the start of the infection, particularly at 1 and 2 dpi, before efficacy started to decrease at 3 dpi.
298.Rationale: Dose clearly affects potency ("A dose response was observed" and specific lowest doses giving complete protection are reported), so dose is important. However, the passage repeatedly highlights timing as a key constraint on efficacy (best at 1–2 dpi, decreased at 3 dpi). Therefore dose alone, "regardless of timing," is not portrayed as the strongest determinant for achieving complete protection when treatment is delayed; timing relative to infection is emphasized as critical.

298.Option: The specific epitope targeted by the monoclonal antibody on the Ebola virus glycoprotein
Evidence: Each of these MAbs binds to different regions of GP1,2. 1H3 and 4G7 bind to the N- and C-terminus of GP1, respectively, whereas 2G4 binds to GP2 [36]. Targeting multiple regions of GP appears to be a successful strategy.
Some MAbs may have more affinity for their epitope, or the epitope may be more accessible in the natural conformation.

There have been several attempts at producing MAbs against ZEBOV GP however no clear pattern has emerged suggesting which primary sequence domain of GP is most immunogenic or whether neutralizing antibodies are more successful [26], [27], [31]–[34].
298.Rationale: The passage indicates that epitope specificity matters (combinations targeting multiple GP regions were successful and individual MAbs differed in potency), so epitope targeted is an important factor. However, for the question asking which factor most strongly influences the likelihood of complete protection when treatment is delayed beyond the initial hours, the data emphasize the timing window (efficacy at 1–2 dpi, drop by 3 dpi) and the benefit of combination therapy given at 2 dpi. The passage stops short of asserting epitope specificity is the single strongest determinant over timing when treatment is delayed.

298.Option: The specific timing of treatment relative to infection onset, particularly whether it occurs within 2 days post-infection, is the most critical factor for achieving complete protection
Evidence: In general, the MAbs worked best for both animal models when given after the start of the infection, particularly at 1 and 2 dpi, before efficacy started to decrease at 3 dpi.
In contrast all animals receiving the neutralizing MAb combination at 2 dpi. survived.
Overall, the neutralizing MAb combination improved survival in all treatment protocols with the 2 dpi treatment protocol being the most effective.
This is a significant extension from the 1 hour post-infection required for KZ52.
This suggests that there may be a limited time period in which to begin treatment after becoming infected.
298.Rationale: The passage repeatedly presents timing after infection as the critical determinant: multiple MAbs and combinations were most effective when administered at 1–2 days post-infection, efficacy declined by 3 dpi, and the neutralizing MAb combination delivered at 2 dpi produced complete survival. The authors explicitly note a limited window for initiating treatment and highlight 2 dpi as the most effective time point for complete protection, making timing relative to infection the strongest influence when treatment is delayed beyond the initial hours.

299.Option: Select the cut-off value that corresponds to the lowest antibody level observed in any confirmed VL case to ensure no false negatives
Evidence: A receiver-operator characteristic (ROC) curve for ELISA was constructed to determine the cut-off value with 95% confidence intervals (CI) that discriminate between VL-positive and -negative urine samples.
With the assessment of ROC curve (Fig 4), the test cut-off point, 0.1875 was selected from possible cut-offs at 95% CI.
299.Rationale: The passage explicitly states the investigators used an ROC curve to choose the diagnostic cut-off (0.1875) rather than selecting the lowest antibody level among cases. Choosing the lowest-case value would aim to eliminate false negatives but would likely produce many false positives and would not optimize the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity. The study instead selected a cut-off based on ROC analysis to discriminate between VL-positive and -negative samples, so the "lowest-case" approach is not appropriate here.

299.Option: Use a receiver-operating characteristic (ROC) curve to identify the optimal cut-off point that maximizes both sensitivity and specificity
Evidence: A receiver-operator characteristic (ROC) curve for ELISA was constructed to determine the cut-off value with 95% confidence intervals (CI) that discriminate between VL-positive and -negative urine samples.
With the assessment of ROC curve (Fig 4), the test cut-off point, 0.1875 was selected from possible cut-offs at 95% CI.

ROC curve obtained from the ELISA values for the detection of anti-Leishmania IgG antibodies in urine samples. Cut-off value (0.1875), sensitivity (97.94%), specificity (100%) and AUC (0.9984) were determined by this curve using GraphPad Prism software (version 5.0).
299.Rationale: The passage directly reports use of an ROC curve to determine the ELISA cut-off and gives the selected threshold and resulting sensitivity/specificity. ROC analysis is the standard method to choose a diagnostic threshold that balances sensitivity and specificity and to evaluate diagnostic accuracy (AUC), so this is the most appropriate method.

299.Option: Set the cut-off value at the mean antibody level of the control group plus two standard deviations to minimize false positives
Evidence: A receiver-operator characteristic (ROC) curve for ELISA was constructed to determine the cut-off value with 95% confidence intervals (CI) that discriminate between VL-positive and -negative urine samples.
With the assessment of ROC curve (Fig 4), the test cut-off point, 0.1875 was selected from possible cut-offs at 95% CI.
299.Rationale: Although the mean+2SD rule is a common heuristic to set cut-offs in some contexts, the investigators did not use that approach. The passage states they used ROC analysis to select the cut-off that discriminates positives and negatives and reports the resulting sensitivity and specificity. Mean+2SD does not explicitly optimize sensitivity and specificity across both groups and may be less appropriate than ROC-derived thresholds for diagnostic tests.

299.Option: Use the median antibody level of the VL-positive group as the cut-off to ensure equal distribution of values above and below the threshold
Evidence: A receiver-operator characteristic (ROC) curve for ELISA was constructed to determine the cut-off value with 95% confidence intervals (CI) that discriminate between VL-positive and -negative urine samples.
With the assessment of ROC curve (Fig 4), the test cut-off point, 0.1875 was selected from possible cut-offs at 95% CI.
299.Rationale: Using the median of cases ignores the control distribution and would not directly address false positives or overall discriminative performance. The passage indicates the investigators used ROC analysis, which explicitly compares case and control distributions to find an optimal threshold (yielding sensitivity 97.94% and specificity 100%), so using the median of positives would be inappropriate here.

300.Option: Respondents may have misreported their feeding behavior due to social desirability bias, especially among those with negative attitudes toward free-roaming dogs
Evidence: Although only 9% of respondents believed that feed provided by villagers was a major source of food for FRD, a larger proportion (39%) actually admitted to feeding them.

It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52], but, apparently it did not motivate them sufficiently to accept responsibility for immunisations, probably also due to issues of availability of vaccines and their cost.
Similarly, the respondents who fed them surprisingly had a lower acceptance for FRD (OR 0.08).
300.Rationale: The passage documents the contradiction between low belief that village feeding is a major food source and the higher self-reported feeding prevalence, but explicitly offers religious conviction as the likely explanation. The authors highlight religion as a driving factor and note that feeders had lower acceptance of FRD, which points to genuine contradictory attitudes (driven by cultural/religious motives), rather than asserting measurement bias such as social desirability. The passage does not present evidence that social desirability bias explains the discrepancy, whereas it does present direct textual explanation (religion). Therefore this option is not the most supported explanation by the passage.

300.Option: The inconsistency between feeding and attitudes is due to a lack of understanding about the transmission of rabies through licks and scratches, which leads to underestimation of risk
Evidence: Of these, 98.4% knew that rabies could be transmitted through animal bites, although less than half (50, 40.7%) were aware that it could also be transmitted through licks/scratches.
Although only 9% of respondents believed that feed provided by villagers was a major source of food for FRD, a larger proportion (39%) actually admitted to feeding them.

It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52], but, apparently it did not motivate them sufficiently to accept responsibility for immunisations, probably also due to issues of availability of vaccines and their cost.
300.Rationale: While the passage documents a knowledge gap about transmission via licks/scratches (supporting that some respondents lack complete understanding of transmission), the authors explicitly point to religious convictions as the likely driver of feeding despite negative attitudes. The described discrepancy (feeding yet lower acceptance) is directly attributed to cultural/religious factors in the text. Thus although limited knowledge about transmission exists, the passage presents religious motivation as the primary explanation, making this option less supported.

300.Option: Religious beliefs promoting animal care override risk awareness, leading to feeding despite negative attitudes toward free-roaming dogs
Evidence: Although only 9% of respondents believed that feed provided by villagers was a major source of food for FRD, a larger proportion (39%) actually admitted to feeding them.
Similarly, the respondents who fed them surprisingly had a lower acceptance for FRD (OR 0.08).

It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52], but, apparently it did not motivate them sufficiently to accept responsibility for immunisations, probably also due to issues of availability of vaccines and their cost.
300.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies religious convictions (predominantly Hindu beliefs tolerant of and caring for animals) as a likely influence on feeding behavior, and documents the paradox that people who feed FRD often have lower acceptance of them. These quotes directly support the interpretation that religiously motivated care overrides concerns about nuisance or disease risk, explaining the observed discrepancy between feeding practices and negative attitudes.

300.Option: Higher socio-economic status is associated with greater access to information about rabies, leading to more consistent reporting of feeding practices and attitudes
Evidence: Respondents from the high/middle socio-economic level considered FRD were useful (OR 3.09, 95%CI 1.06–8.97, p = 0.03), were likely to feed them (OR2.81, 95%CI 1.34–5.88, p = 0.005), and would take an injured stray dog to a veterinarian (OR 2.33 95%CI 1.0–5.48, p = 0.04).

It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52], but, apparently it did not motivate them sufficiently to accept responsibility for immunisations...
Although only 9% of respondents believed that feed provided by villagers was a major source of food for FRD, a larger proportion (39%) actually admitted to feeding them.
300.Rationale: The passage does report that higher socio-economic respondents were more likely to feed FRD and had more sympathetic attitudes, but it does not claim that higher SES yields more consistent reporting or that differential access to information explains the feeding-attitude discrepancy. Instead, the authors emphasize religious conviction and disposable income as drivers of feeding. Therefore the specific mechanism proposed in this option (greater access to information producing consistent reporting) is not supported by the quoted text.

300.Option: The discrepancy arises because respondents from lower socio-economic groups are more likely to feed dogs out of necessity, while higher SES groups feed them out of compassion, leading to divergent attitudes
Evidence: Respondents from the high/middle socio-economic level considered FRD were useful (OR 3.09, 95%CI 1.06–8.97, p = 0.03), were likely to feed them (OR2.81, 95%CI 1.34–5.88, p = 0.005), and would take an injured stray dog to a veterinarian (OR 2.33 95%CI 1.0–5.48, p = 0.04).

This could be exploited for the promotion of responsible dog ownership, including adopting FRD [14]. This is also corroborated by our finding that respondents from high/middle socio-economic class (OR = 2.9, p = 0.02), and those from smaller families (OR = 2.1, p = 0.07) were more likely to take an injured dog to a veterinarian. The smaller families in Shirsuphal usually comprised adults and elderly people who may be more compassionate with stronger religious beliefs on the importance of feeding and/or caring for dogs than younger members of larger families.
as a result of potentially having more disposable income than those from a lower class.
300.Rationale: The passage reports that it is the higher/middle socio-economic group—not the lower SES group—that is more likely to feed FRD, and suggests disposable income and compassion as reasons. The option's assertion that lower SES feed out of necessity while higher SES feed out of compassion contradicts the observed pattern in the text. The passage therefore does not support this proposed explanation.

301.Option: The pathogen is transmitted directly from infected fleas to their offspring and persists across life stages without requiring vertebrate hosts
Evidence: It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4].
It is unknown if vertebrate reservoirs contribute to its transmission cycle; the bacterium is transovarially and transstadially maintained in fleas.
301.Rationale: Direct evidence that the agent is passed generationally in fleas and is transovarially and transstadially maintained (i.e., persists across life stages and is transmitted from parent arthropod to offspring) is the strongest support for an arthropod-only maintenance cycle. These observations indicate the pathogen can be maintained and replicated within the non-vertebrate vector population without requiring ongoing transmission through vertebrate hosts.

301.Option: The pathogen causes severe neurological disease in humans, indicating a high pathogenic potential that likely requires sustained circulation in vertebrate populations
Evidence: Typically, it presents as a mild, febrile illness that can be syndromically confused with many other undifferentiated fevers, especially in Africa and Asia, where diagnostic resources are limited.
R. felis DNA was recovered from the CSF specimens of two fatal cases.
It is unknown if vertebrate reservoirs contribute to its transmission cycle; the bacterium is transovarially and transstadially maintained in fleas.
301.Rationale: Severity of human disease (including occasional severe or fatal cases) does not imply the pathogen requires sustained circulation in vertebrate reservoirs. The passage notes that R. felis typically causes mild febrile illness and that vertebrate reservoir involvement is unknown; moreover, documented severe cases can represent spillover events from an arthropod-maintained cycle rather than evidence of vertebrate reservoir maintenance.

301.Option: The pathogen was first isolated from cat fleas, indicating that fleas are the primary and exclusive vector, which supports a transmission cycle independent of vertebrate hosts
Evidence: Rickettsia felis was first identified as a R. typhi-like organism in the midgut epithelium of a commercial colony of cat fleas, Ctenocephalides felis [1].

It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4].
It is unknown if vertebrate reservoirs contribute to its transmission cycle; the bacterium is transovarially and transstadially maintained in fleas.
301.Rationale: Isolation from cat fleas supports the importance of fleas as a vector, but 'primary and exclusive' vector status is not established by initial isolation alone. The passage also notes the organism has been found in multiple arthropod species and explicitly states uncertainty about vertebrate reservoirs, so exclusivity to fleas is not supported.

301.Option: The pathogen is not detected in human blood samples, suggesting it is not adapted to vertebrate hosts and thus likely maintained solely in arthropods
Evidence: Because Rickettsia invade endothelial cells, DNA is typically scarce in sera or CSF [23].
Insufficient sera were available from these two cases for Rickettsia PCR.
Two of 38 CSF samples (patients E-059 and E-063) were, however, positive for Rickettsia DNA as determined by genus-specific 23S rRNA qPCR.
301.Rationale: Failure to detect pathogen DNA in blood does not reliably indicate a lack of adaptation to vertebrate hosts; rickettsiae commonly invade endothelial cells and are present at low levels in serum/CSF, making detection difficult. The passage notes paucity of detectable DNA in sera/CSF and that sera were insufficient in these cases, so negative blood tests are not strong evidence for exclusive arthropod maintenance.

301.Option: The pathogen has been detected in multiple arthropod species, including mosquitoes and fleas, suggesting a broad vector range that supports transmission without vertebrate hosts
Evidence: It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4].
It has been found in a variety of arthropod species, including some, like book lice, that are not blood-feeding and others, like mosquitoes, not proven to transmit Rickettsia [4, 24].
It is unknown if vertebrate reservoirs contribute to its transmission cycle; the bacterium is transovarially and transstadially maintained in fleas.
301.Rationale: Detection in multiple arthropod species supports widespread arthropod association and raises plausibility of arthropod-only maintenance, but it is not the most compelling single factor. Direct evidence of vertical (transovarial) and transstadial maintenance in a vector (as in Option 0) more strongly indicates the pathogen can be sustained within arthropod populations without vertebrate reservoirs. Moreover, detection in diverse arthropods does not prove those species are competent vectors or that vertebrate reservoirs are unnecessary.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate of 41.3% in elderly dengue patients justifies the use of any available laboratory test for early risk stratification
Evidence: The in-hospital CFR was 41.3% (31 patients), and these patients had a median ICU stay of 4 (IQR: 1–8) days.
From the results of the current study, APTT may be considered as a screening tool for risk stratification.
302.Rationale: While the passage reports a high in-hospital CFR of 41.3%, it does not state that this justifies using "any available laboratory test." Instead, the authors specifically highlight APTT as a candidate single test for screening. The quoted sentence that APTT may be considered as a screening tool indicates the authors advocate a specific laboratory marker rather than a blanket endorsement of any test. Therefore the option's broad claim is not supported by the passage.

302.Option: Prolonged APTT at ICU admission remains an independent predictor of in-hospital fatality even after accounting for multi-parameter severity scores
Evidence: In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [95% confidence interval: 1.91–20.54]), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 [1.07–5.74]), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 [1.69–9.07]) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, presence of AKI at ICU admission and initial APTT longer than 48 s (Table 3).

APTT prolongation > 48 s remained to be an independent predictive factor for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for APACHE II score (HR: 3.34, P = 0.006), and was marginally significant after adjusting for SOFA score (HR: 2.43, P = 0.053).
APTT, a single laboratory parameter, exhibited valuable prognostic significance.
302.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that initial APTT >48 s was an independent predictor of in-hospital fatality in multivariate analysis and that this association persisted after adjustment for APACHE II (and was marginal after SOFA). The authors also emphasize that APTT, a single laboratory parameter, showed valuable prognostic significance. This directly supports the option that prolonged APTT remains predictive even when multi-parameter scores are considered, and therefore supports using a single lab test for early risk stratification.

302.Option: High APACHE II and SOFA scores are strongly associated with in-hospital fatality and therefore should be prioritized for early risk stratification in elderly dengue patients
Evidence: In the univariate Cox model, female gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, high creatinine level, AKI at ICU admission, a high APACHE II or SOFA score, and APTT longer than 48 s were significantly associated with in-hospital fatality.

In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 ...), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 ...), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 ...) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality ...

APACHE II and SOFA scores include many clinical parameters and were not included in the final model of multivariate Cox analysis as they demonstrated collinearity with other variables. They were positively associated with acute respiratory failure, AKI, and cardiac arrest.
The area under the ROC curve (AUC) of APTT was 0.76 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.64–0.88), APACHE II score 0.84 (0.75–0.93), and SOFA score 0.85 (0.77–0.94).
302.Rationale: Although APACHE II and SOFA scores are associated with fatality in univariate analysis and have higher AUCs than APTT, the passage shows they were excluded from the final multivariate model due to collinearity and that independent predictors remaining were cardiac arrest, AKI, and APTT. The authors emphasize the practical value of a single laboratory parameter (APTT) as a screening tool. Thus the passage does not support prioritizing multi-parameter scores over a single laboratory test for early risk stratification in this elderly cohort.

302.Option: APTT prolongation is more sensitive than APACHE II and SOFA scores for detecting early signs of organ failure in elderly dengue patients
Evidence: The area under the ROC curve (AUC) of APTT was 0.76 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.64–0.88), APACHE II score 0.84 (0.75–0.93), and SOFA score 0.85 (0.77–0.94).

Though there was no significant difference between APTT and two scores (APTT vs. APACHE II, P = 0.27; APTT vs. SOFA, P = 0.16), AUCs of APACHE II and SOFA scores, which both were a constellation of multiple clinical and laboratory variables, were higher than that of APTT.
302.Rationale: The passage reports that APTT's AUC (0.76) is lower than APACHE II and SOFA (0.84 and 0.85), and explicitly states the multi-parameter scores had higher AUCs than APTT, although differences were not statistically significant. Therefore the claim that APTT is more sensitive than those scores for detecting early organ failure is not supported by the data presented.

302.Option: The presence of acute respiratory failure at ICU admission is a stronger predictor of fatality than APTT prolongation and should be used for early risk stratification
Evidence: In the univariate Cox model, female gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, high creatinine level, AKI at ICU admission, a high APACHE II or SOFA score, and APTT longer than 48 s were significantly associated with in-hospital fatality.

In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 ...), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 ...), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 ...) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality ...

In the Cox model, although respiratory failure indicative of a deterioration of clinical status and dysregulation in respiratory function, at ICU admission, was marginally significant, respiratory failure still remains to be an important prognostic factor.
302.Rationale: Respiratory failure was significant in univariate analysis but was not an independent predictor in the multivariate model; instead, cardiac arrest, AKI, and APTT prolongation were independent predictors. The passage notes respiratory failure was only marginally significant in the Cox model. Thus it does not support the assertion that respiratory failure is a stronger predictor than APTT prolongation for early risk stratification.

304.Option: Use Bayesian inference with a fixed geographic origin for each node to estimate the timing of transmission events
Evidence: The Bayesian inference of ancestral dates was performed by BactDating v1.1.0 [47] from the phylogenetic tree.

The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49]. The make.simmap function simulates the stochastic character map while treating the continent of origin as a discrete character trait of the tree tips.

The geographical transmission history was summarised from 100 simulations using the Empirical Bayes method of the make.simmap function. Thus, the posterior probabilities of the ancestral nodes were estimated and visualised. The location of the ancestral nodes was determined with posterior probability >50%.
304.Rationale: This study used Bayesian methods (BactDating) to infer dates (timing) but did not assign a "fixed geographic origin" to nodes via Bayesian inference. Instead, the authors used stochastic character mapping (make.simmap) with multiple simulations and posterior probabilities to infer geographic history. A fixed-origin assignment would ignore the probabilistic uncertainty they explicitly modelled and visualised; therefore this option does not match the methods described in the passage.

304.Option: Apply maximum parsimony to reconstruct the most likely geographic origin of ancestral nodes based on the fewest transmission events
Evidence: The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49]. The make.simmap function simulates the stochastic character map while treating the continent of origin as a discrete character trait of the tree tips.

The geographical transmission history was summarised from 100 simulations using the Empirical Bayes method of the make.simmap function. Thus, the posterior probabilities of the ancestral nodes were estimated and visualised. The location of the ancestral nodes was determined with posterior probability >50%.
304.Rationale: Maximum parsimony (minimizing number of changes) is not the method described. The authors used stochastic character mapping with multiple simulations to capture uncertainty and to estimate posterior probabilities for ancestral geographic states; parsimony would provide a single most-parsimonious reconstruction without the simulation-based posterior probabilities the passage reports.

304.Option: Use stochastic character mapping with posterior probability estimation via multiple simulations to infer geographic origin at ancestral nodes
Evidence: The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49]. The make.simmap function simulates the stochastic character map while treating the continent of origin as a discrete character trait of the tree tips.

The geographical transmission history was summarised from 100 simulations using the Empirical Bayes method of the make.simmap function. Thus, the posterior probabilities of the ancestral nodes were estimated and visualised. The location of the ancestral nodes was determined with posterior probability >50%.
304.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the authors used make.simmap (stochastic character mapping) with 100 simulations and the Empirical Bayes method to infer geographic transmission history, producing posterior probabilities for ancestral node locations. This approach directly matches the option's description of stochastic mapping with posterior probability estimation to account for uncertainty in transmissions.

304.Option: Use a parsimony-based ancestral state reconstruction with bootstrapping to assess confidence in geographic origin estimates
Evidence: The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49]. The make.simmap function simulates the stochastic character map while treating the continent of origin as a discrete character trait of the tree tips.

The geographical transmission history was summarised from 100 simulations using the Empirical Bayes method of the make.simmap function. Thus, the posterior probabilities of the ancestral nodes were estimated and visualised. The location of the ancestral nodes was determined with posterior probability >50%.
304.Rationale: The authors used simulation-based stochastic mapping and posterior probabilities rather than parsimony-based reconstruction with bootstrapping. While bootstrapping can assess phylogenetic confidence, the passage describes a probabilistic (stochastic simulation / Empirical Bayes) approach for geographic inference, not a parsimony-plus-bootstrap method.

304.Option: Perform a likelihood ratio test to compare geographic origin hypotheses at ancestral nodes using a time-calibrated phylogeny
Evidence: The Bayesian inference of ancestral dates was performed by BactDating v1.1.0 [47] from the phylogenetic tree.

The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49]. The make.simmap function simulates the stochastic character map while treating the continent of origin as a discrete character trait of the tree tips.

The geographical transmission history was summarised from 100 simulations using the Empirical Bayes method of the make.simmap function. Thus, the posterior probabilities of the ancestral nodes were estimated and visualised. The location of the ancestral nodes was determined with posterior probability >50%.
304.Rationale: Although the authors used Bayesian dating (BactDating) to obtain a time-calibrated phylogeny, they did not report using likelihood ratio tests to compare geographic origin hypotheses. Instead, they used stochastic character mapping with simulations and posterior probabilities to infer geography. A likelihood ratio test approach is not described in the passage and would not provide the same simulation-based posterior probabilities used here.

306.Option: The use of a quantitative ELISA assay to measure circulating filarial antigen levels, enabling detection of changes in adult worm burden over time
Evidence: The CFA was detected by using the commercially available ELISA kit TropBio Og4C3 (Cellabs, New South Wales, Australia), using 100 μl of the collected plasma as performed in the previous study [13].
Most filarial infected individuals in 2009 showed high positive results, reflecting a high adult worm burden. In 2019 infected individuals had low positive ODs indicating a low number of adult worms.

We have also presented analytical data which, in addition, shows a significant reduction in the sera CFA levels of RHINO study participants (Figs 3 and 4), indicating a reduced adult worm burden in the infected individuals.
306.Rationale: The passage documents that a quantitative ELISA was used and that antigen optical density (OD) values declined over time, which supports that adult worm burden decreased after MDA. However, while a quantitative assay improves measurement precision and demonstrates biologic change (reduced antigen load), it alone does not most strongly establish that the population-wide MDA campaign — rather than other temporal factors — caused the observed population-level prevalence decline. Attribution to the campaign requires a study design or analytic feature that ties the timing and coverage of MDA to within-person changes or controls for alternative explanations; the quantitative ELISA documents effect magnitude but does not by itself rule out other causes.

306.Option: The inclusion of a control group from a non-treatment region to compare changes in filarial prevalence over time
Evidence: The study was a follow-up of the population based EMINI (Evaluation and Monitoring of the Impact of New Interventions, http://www.mmrp.org/projects/cohort-studies/emini.html) cohort study, which was conducted at the National Institute for Medical Research (NIMR)—Mbeya Medical Research Centre (MMRC) ([13,18–22].
All households in Kyela who previously participated in the EMINI study ([13]) were contacted to enrol inhabitants aged 14 to 65 years into the RHINO study.
The intriguing feature in this cohort is that the very same people have been visited in 2009, 2010/11 and 2019, thus excluding a sampling bias of this study.
306.Rationale: The passage describes a single-region follow-up of the EMINI cohort in Kyela and emphasizes repeated measurement of the same individuals. It does not describe inclusion of a control population from a non-treatment region for between-region comparison. Therefore this option is not a feature of the study and cannot be the methodological feature that strengthens attribution in this particular investigation.

306.Option: The use of a fixed cohort with repeated measurements over time, which controls for both sampling bias and age-related confounding
Evidence: The study was a follow-up of the population based EMINI ... The study was carried out in selected villages from nine geographically distinct areas in the Mbeya region (S1 Fig) which included 10% of the households of each selected village.
All households in Kyela who previously participated in the EMINI study ([13]) were contacted to enrol inhabitants aged 14 to 65 years into the RHINO study.

The intriguing feature in this cohort is that the very same people have been visited in 2009, 2010/11 and 2019, thus excluding a sampling bias of this study. Admittedly this approach adds an age bias instead, but since older age is associated with higher LF-prevalence, this age bias should lead to an increased prevalence, while our study shows a greatly reduced prevalence.
306.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the same individuals were revisited in 2009, 2010/11 and 2019 and that this design excludes sampling bias, so the fixed cohort/repeated-measures feature is present and is a major strength for attributing change to the intervention. However, the option's claim that this design 'controls for ... age-related confounding' is contradicted by the passage: the authors acknowledge that the repeated-follow-up approach 'adds an age bias' (older by ~10 years) rather than controlling age confounding. Because the option asserts control of age-related confounding when the study itself notes an age-related bias, the option is not accurate as worded and must be judged incorrect despite the cohort design being an important strength.

306.Option: The statistical adjustment for age, sex, and occupation in multivariable regression models to control for potential confounders
Evidence: Furthermore we performed uni- and multi-variable log link binomial regression analyses with robust variance estimates in order to assess potential risk factors for LF infection.
Multi-variable analysis confirmed that W. bancrofti infection was associated with increasing age, male sex and filarial infection during the previous study (Table 3).
Major risk factors for the infection with W. bancrofti were male sex, older age, and occupation as a farmer.
306.Rationale: The passage documents that multivariable regression was used to assess and adjust for key predictors (age, sex, occupation) that could confound the relationship between time/MDA and LF prevalence. Statistical adjustment for these variables strengthens confidence that the observed reduction is not simply due to shifts in the population composition (for example, fewer high-risk older men or farmers) but reflects a true reduction over time consistent with the MDA campaign. Given the available options, this implemented analytic control for important confounders is an appropriate and present methodological feature that increases confidence in attributing the decline to the treatment program.

307.Option: The significant association between male sex and leptospiral IgM positivity, especially in the 20–29 age group, suggests occupational exposure to infected individuals, supporting direct transmission in high-risk professions
Evidence: The majority of these Leptospira IgM cases were among young adult males (20–29 years) living in an urban setting.
young adult males (20–29 years) are at risk of leptospirosis because of their occupational exposures.

The significant associations with male sex are consistent with the fact that most occupations at high risk (farmers, miners, recreational activities in muddy grounds, exposures to flood water, building workers) for leptospirosis are often associated with males.
307.Rationale: The passage links male sex and the 20-29 age group to occupational exposures to contaminated environments and animals (farmers, miners, exposures to flood water, etc.), not to exposures to infected humans. Those quotes support environmental/occupational exposure as the explanation for the male predominance, which contradicts the option's claim of direct person-to-person transmission in high-risk professions.

307.Option: The seasonal peak of cases during the rainy season, particularly in urban areas, indicates that transmission is driven by increased human mobility and social interaction, supporting a hypothesis of person-to-person spread
Evidence: In both urban and rural areas, most of the cases occurred during the rainy season.
Seventy-nine percent of the IgM positive cases occurred during the rainy season.
African countries have favorable environmental characteristics for the spread of leptospirosis (rainfall, animal exposure, flooding, rapid urbanization).
The seasonality of leptospirosis has been described in most parts of the world, with a significantly higher incidence during rainy months, in tropical areas
307.Rationale: The passage attributes the rainy-season peak to environmental factors (rainfall, flooding, animal exposure) known to increase exposure to Leptospira in contaminated water/soil, not to increased human-to-human contact. The quoted sentences point to environmental drivers of seasonality, so the option's inference of person-to-person spread is not supported.

307.Option: The high seroprevalence of Leptospira IgM in urban areas, particularly in Kinshasa, suggests that the disease is spreading through dense human populations, indicating possible person-to-person transmission
Evidence: We found that 59% of positive cases lived in an urban setting, and 41% lived in a rural setting.
50 out of 88 positive leptospiral IgM cases were in the province of Kinshasa.
The high seroprevalence in urban settings may relate to poor urbanization (increased risk of flooding), overcrowded populations, as well as knowledge, attitude, and practice determinants.
Leptospirosis is globally known as an urban health problem and the role of urban areas in the re-emergence of leptospirosis has been described by several authors
307.Rationale: Although seroprevalence is higher in urban areas like Kinshasa, the passage explains this pattern by environmental and infrastructure factors (poor urbanization, flooding, overcrowding) that increase environmental exposure to contaminated water/soil, not by direct human-to-human transmission. The provided quotes therefore contradict the option's implication of person-to-person spread.

307.Option: The presence of Leptospira IgM in 14 provinces across the DRC, including both urban and rural areas, indicates widespread human-to-human transmission across diverse populations and regions
Evidence: Subjects with anti-Leptospira IgM antibodies were found in 14 provinces with different type of climate, suggesting a broad distribution and circulation of Leptospira spp. in the country.

Of the 26 provinces within the country, 25 were represented in the national yellow fever surveillance dataset for the study period, and anti-Leptospira antibodies were found in samples collected from 14 provinces.
The epidemiology of leptospirosis varies significantly from one geographical area to another, depending on the climate and lifestyles of the inhabitants.
307.Rationale: Widespread detection across provinces and climates is consistent with broad environmental circulation of Leptospira and with climate- and lifestyle-associated risk, as the quotes state. The passage does not provide evidence of human-to-human spread; instead it emphasizes environmental heterogeneity and reservoirs. Thus, the option's attribution to human-to-human transmission is not supported.

307.Option: The clustering of cases among young adult males in urban areas during the rainy season, coinciding with flooding and poor sanitation, aligns with environmental exposure pathways rather than person-to-person transmission
Evidence: The majority of these Leptospira IgM cases were among young adult males (20–29 years) living in an urban setting.
young adult males (20–29 years) are at risk of leptospirosis because of their occupational exposures.
In both urban and rural areas, most of the cases occurred during the rainy season.
Seventy-nine percent of the IgM positive cases occurred during the rainy season.
Kinshasa is facing rapid urbanization with an overcrowding population and is prone to flooding after heavy rains.
The high seroprevalence in urban settings may relate to poor urbanization (increased risk of flooding), overcrowded populations, as well as knowledge, attitude, and practice determinants.
African countries have favorable environmental characteristics for the spread of leptospirosis (rainfall, animal exposure, flooding, rapid urbanization).
307.Rationale: These quoted passages collectively point to environmental exposure (rainy season, flooding, poor urban sanitation, occupational exposures involving water/muddy environments and animals) as the likely route of transmission. The demographic clustering (young adult males in urban, flood-prone areas during rainy months) and explicit statements about environmental risk factors provide direct support for environmental rather than person-to-person transmission.

308.Option: The combination of waning insecticidal efficacy and reduced use of complementary protective behaviors likely led to increased dengue exposure despite high curtain usage
Evidence: First, despite being fabricated from a material that was expected to retain high insecticide levels over the course of several years, the ITCs quickly lost insecticidal efficacy, leading to the need for mass re-treatment only 1-year after they had been originally deployed.
The 9-month serosurvey occurred before the ITCs were re-treated, which means that many were operating sub-optimally and could have potentially had reduced protective effectiveness.

Second, a contributing factor may have been a false sense of security amongst the participants in the intervention arm; i.e., perhaps the presence of the highly visible ITCs created a belief in their protective power among the treated households, who subsequently did not employ any additional measures typically used to avoid exposure to mosquito bites.

Participants that had received ITCs commented that when the curtains were first hung, the household reduced their use of mosquito repellents and stopped fumigating because they felt that it was no longer necessary due to the presence of the ITCs [17].
participants in the intervention arm were more likely to report reduced use of other mosquito products due to the feeling of protection from the ITCs compared to those in the control arm [17, 44].
308.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies both rapid loss of insecticidal efficacy of the ITCs (necessitating re-treatment) and behavioral change (reduced use of other mosquito products because households felt protected) as contributing factors. Together these mechanisms explain how high reported curtain usage and early entomological gains could nonetheless coincide with increased seroconversion: curtains were sub-optimally insecticidal during the serosurvey and households reduced complementary protections, increasing exposure risk.

308.Option: The initial entomological improvements observed in the intervention arm were not sustained due to the failure of insecticide-treated curtains to maintain efficacy over time, leading to increased dengue transmission
Evidence: entomological indicators appeared to be lower in the intervention arm, most notably at the first follow-up survey, the differences were not statistically significant and were not sustained over the course of the study.

First, despite being fabricated from a material that was expected to retain high insecticide levels over the course of several years, the ITCs quickly lost insecticidal efficacy, leading to the need for mass re-treatment only 1-year after they had been originally deployed.
After hanging for 11 months (October 2010), the same process was repeated and all except for 1 curtain fell below the 80% mortality threshold (range: 32%-98%).
308.Rationale: The trial observed early entomological reductions that were not sustained. The passage documents that ITCs lost insecticidal efficacy within a year (many curtains fell below the 80% mortality threshold), which directly explains why initial entomological improvements would not persist and could allow transmission to increase over time, producing higher seroconversion.

308.Option: The re-treatment of insecticide-treated curtains with deltamethrin likely disrupted the protective effect by causing a temporary reduction in insecticidal activity, increasing dengue exposure during the follow-up period
Evidence: Therefore, to ensure an effective intervention was present in the treated households for the remaining period of the study, curtains were re-treated with deltamethrin in the form of KO Tab 123 (Bayer) during November 2010, with a total of 3,886 curtains (91.9%) re-treated.

Cone bioassays after re-treatment with KO-Tab 123 showed that the curtains did not immediately recover to the 100% bioefficacy observed at baseline. At 1 month following re-treatment (January 2011), average mortality for 36 ITCs was 74.5% (range: 48%-94%). By 9-months following re-treatment (August 2011) average mortality from 9 tested ITCs was 97.2% (range: 87.5%-100%).
Resistance to deltamethrin was first detected during February 2011, when 24-hour mortality using the WHO bioassay dropped to 79.7% and was at a similar level (74.8%) during April 2011.
308.Rationale: The passage describes re-treatment as an attempt to restore efficacy, not as a cause of disruption. While bioassays showed that curtains did not immediately reach baseline 100% mortality at 1 month, by 9 months post-re-treatment efficacy was high, so the re-treatment did not clearly 'disrupt' protection. The timing also shows detection of resistance soon after re-treatment, but the evidence supports re-treatment as a corrective action rather than the cause of a temporary loss that increased exposure; therefore this option is not supported as the primary explanation.

308.Option: The emergence of deltamethrin resistance in the local mosquito population likely reduced the effectiveness of insecticide-treated curtains, leading to increased dengue transmission despite high usage
Evidence: Insecticide susceptibility data from CDC bottle bioassays demonstrated that the local Ae. aegypti population was fully susceptible to deltamethrin at baseline and remained fully susceptible when tested during May 2010 and July 2010. Resistance to deltamethrin was first detected during February 2011, when 24-hour mortality using the WHO bioassay dropped to 79.7% and was at a similar level (74.8%) during April 2011. Mortality fell further to 68.3% (using the CDC bottle bioassay) during August 2011.

This initial detection of resistance was worrying, because it could indicate that low concentrations of insecticide on ITCs that were not adequately loaded with deltamethrin (the reason why the re-treatment was carried out) had already begun to select for deltamethrin resistance in the local mosquito population.
The reduced insecticidal effect of the ITCs was associated with the initial detection of deltamethrin resistance in the local Ae. aegypti population.
308.Rationale: The passage documents emergence and progressive worsening of deltamethrin resistance during the study period and explicitly links reduced insecticidal effect to detection of resistance. Resistance would reduce the biological effectiveness of treated curtains even if they were present and used, providing a plausible, supported mechanism for increased dengue transmission despite high reported usage.

309.Option: The similarity of final clinical response rates between Bangladesh and India supports the generalizability of the results, suggesting that the 6-month clinical endpoint is a valid proxy for parasitological cure in both settings
Evidence: The final clinical response rate (94.2%) is similar to the final clinical response rate observed in the Phase III (94.2% final response) and Phase IV (94.6% final response) studies of PMIM in India [4,5].

The 6-month observation after treatment was the only practically feasible way to assess a cure in this study because splenic aspiration or other invasive procedures were not appropriate to conduct at the clinical study centers.
Final clinical response was defined as the absence of new clinical signs and symptoms of VL at 6 months after EOT.
309.Rationale: While the passage explicitly notes similar final clinical response rates between Bangladesh and India (supporting some generalizability), it also states that parasitological confirmation (e.g., splenic aspiration) was not feasible and that a 6-month clinical outcome was used because of practical constraints. Therefore the study authors do not claim the 6-month clinical endpoint is a validated proxy for parasitological cure; the endpoint was a pragmatic substitute. The option overstates validity of the 6-month clinical endpoint as a parasitological cure measure beyond what the passage supports.

309.Option: The use of a 6-month follow-up window is sufficient to detect late relapses because the majority of relapses occur within the first 3 months after treatment, minimizing the risk of underestimating true cure rates
Evidence: Six months after EOT (approximately Study Day 202), patients were asked to return for an assessment of final clinical response.

The 6-month observation after treatment was the only practically feasible way to assess a cure in this study because splenic aspiration or other invasive procedures were not appropriate to conduct at the clinical study centers.
Patients were not required to return for follow-up, but were encouraged to return if problems arose during this 30-day window.
309.Rationale: The passage describes that a 6-month clinical assessment was used for pragmatic reasons but does not provide any evidence about the timing distribution of relapses (for example, that most relapses occur within 3 months). Because the passage contains no data or statements about relapse timing, the claim that a 6-month window is sufficient for detecting late relapses is unsupported by the text.

309.Option: The presence of reversible hearing impairment in two patients confirms that ototoxicity is a minor risk and does not significantly affect long-term follow-up or interpretation of treatment success
Evidence: Paromomycin belongs to the aminoglycoside class of agents, which are known to cause nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.

During the study, 2 subjects reported adverse events related to hearing loss after end of treatment: moderate ear pruritus in an adult female and moderate hearing impairment and tinnitus in an adult male who also had moderate renal impairment. All of these events were reversible.
Nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity were not formally studied in this trial.
309.Rationale: Although the passage reports two reversible hearing-related adverse events, it also notes that aminoglycosides are known to cause nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity and that these toxicities were not formally studied in this trial. From the passage alone one cannot conclude that ototoxicity is a minor risk with no impact on long-term follow-up or interpretation of treatment success; the data are limited and the trial did not formally assess these outcomes.

309.Option: The reliance on clinical signs rather than parasitological confirmation for assessing cure may underestimate true relapse rates due to limited diagnostic capacity and potential treatment-related loss to follow-up
Evidence: Final clinical response was defined as the absence of new clinical signs and symptoms of VL at 6 months after EOT.

clinical response was evaluated in the following binary fashion: (a) Is the patient’s temperature less than 99.4°F in clinic at EOT visit (Yes/No); (b) Has the patient reported resolution of fever and no fever within the last 5 days? (Yes/No); (c) Has spleen size decreased from screening value? (Yes/No); (d) Is the clinical impression of the treating physician that of an adequate clinical response? (Yes/No).

The 6-month observation after treatment was the only practically feasible way to assess a cure in this study because splenic aspiration or other invasive procedures were not appropriate to conduct at the clinical study centers.
Patients were not required to return for follow-up, but were encouraged to return if problems arose during this 30-day window.
309.Rationale: The passage makes clear that cure was assessed using clinical criteria at 6 months because parasitological confirmation (e.g., splenic aspiration) was not feasible in the study setting. Clinical-only assessments can miss subclinical or parasitological relapse, and the study’s pragmatic follow-up procedures (patients not required to return) create risk of loss to follow-up. These factors together most critically influence interpretation of long-term treatment success in a resource-limited, outpatient setting, making this option the best-supported concern in the passage.

309.Option: The high compliance rate of 98.3% ensures that the final clinical response rate accurately reflects the true effectiveness of paromomycin, minimizing bias from treatment non-adherence
Evidence: A total of 118/120 subjects (98.3%) were compliant with treatment (21 daily injections over no more than 22 days).
The final clinical response rate 6 months after EOT was 94.2% (95% CI 90.0–98.4);

The 6-month observation after treatment was the only practically feasible way to assess a cure in this study because splenic aspiration or other invasive procedures were not appropriate to conduct at the clinical study centers.
Patients were not required to return for follow-up, but were encouraged to return if problems arose during this 30-day window.
309.Rationale: High observed compliance reduces bias from non-adherence and supports internal validity, but it does not by itself ensure the final clinical response rate equals true effectiveness. The study relied on clinical endpoints without parasitological confirmation and had pragmatic follow-up procedures that could allow missed relapses or loss to follow-up. Thus compliance is helpful but not the single critical factor determining interpretation of long-term success in this setting.

310.Option: Compare the proportion of patients with ECG abnormalities at 5-year intervals post-treatment to assess changes in protection over time
Evidence: A descriptive study of cardiological, parasitological and serological results in a cohort of pediatric CD patients, with at least 6 years’ post-treatment follow-up, treated with benznidazole (Bz) or nifurtimox (Nf) was conducted at the Servicio de Parasitología y Chagas, Hospital de Niños Ricardo Gutiérrez, Buenos Aires, Argentina.
The median age at diagnosis was 62.53 months (range 1–202 months, IQR 11–116), with a median follow up time of 9.8 years (6–20.2 years, IQR 7.5–12.4) (Fig 1).
The kinetics of T. cruzi serum antibodies were analyzed using survival analysis.

A weakness of our study is that it lacks an untreated control group due to the fact that the standard of care for pediatric CD is the treatment of infected children as soon as possible after diagnosis.
310.Rationale: Simply comparing proportions at fixed 5-year intervals ignores variable follow-up time and censoring (loss to follow-up or staggered entry) that are present in long-term cohorts. The passage indicates variable follow-up (median 9.8 years, range 6–20.2) and that survival methods were used for serology kinetics, implying that time-to-event methods that account for censoring are preferred. Proportion-at-interval comparisons can be biased when person-time differs across intervals and do not fully use the longitudinal information.

310.Option: Compare the incidence of cardiac abnormalities across time intervals since treatment to detect potential waning of protection
Evidence: A descriptive study of cardiological, parasitological and serological results in a cohort of pediatric CD patients, with at least 6 years’ post-treatment follow-up, treated with benznidazole (Bz) or nifurtimox (Nf) was conducted at the Servicio de Parasitología y Chagas, Hospital de Niños Ricardo Gutiérrez, Buenos Aires, Argentina.
The median age at diagnosis was 62.53 months (range 1–202 months, IQR 11–116), with a median follow up time of 9.8 years (6–20.2 years, IQR 7.5–12.4) (Fig 1).

A weakness of our study is that it lacks an untreated control group due to the fact that the standard of care for pediatric CD is the treatment of infected children as soon as possible after diagnosis.
310.Rationale: In a single-arm cohort with long follow-up and no untreated controls, estimating incidence rates (events per person-time) in successive time-since-treatment intervals is a valid approach to detect waning protection. This uses the cohort's person-time, accounts for differing follow-up, and compares risk over time within the treated population. The passage documents a long and variable follow-up period, making interval-specific incidence a feasible and appropriate method to evaluate changing rates of cardiac abnormalities.

310.Option: Use survival analysis to estimate the time to first cardiac abnormality after treatment, accounting for censoring due to loss to follow-up
Evidence: The kinetics of T. cruzi serum antibodies were analyzed using survival analysis.

A descriptive study of cardiological, parasitological and serological results in a cohort of pediatric CD patients, with at least 6 years’ post-treatment follow-up, treated with benznidazole (Bz) or nifurtimox (Nf) was conducted at the Servicio de Parasitología y Chagas, Hospital de Niños Ricardo Gutiérrez, Buenos Aires, Argentina.
The median age at diagnosis was 62.53 months (range 1–202 months, IQR 11–116), with a median follow up time of 9.8 years (6–20.2 years, IQR 7.5–12.4) (Fig 1).

A weakness of our study is that it lacks an untreated control group due to the fact that the standard of care for pediatric CD is the treatment of infected children as soon as possible after diagnosis.
310.Rationale: Survival (time-to-event) analysis is the most appropriate method to assess whether protection wanes in a treated cohort with variable follow-up and censoring. It directly models time until first cardiac abnormality, properly handles censoring and staggered entries, and allows estimation of hazard over time (including Kaplan–Meier curves or Cox models). The passage itself used survival analysis for antibody kinetics, illustrating the method's relevance to longitudinal, censored data in this cohort without an untreated control.

310.Option: Calculate the odds ratio of cardiac abnormalities in patients treated within the first year of infection compared to those treated later, using a case-control design within the cohort
Evidence: Since the standard of care for CD is the treatment of all infected children, it was not possible to include a non-treated control group due to ethical issues.

A descriptive study of cardiological, parasitological and serological results in a cohort of pediatric CD patients, with at least 6 years’ post-treatment follow-up, treated with benznidazole (Bz) or nifurtimox (Nf) was conducted at the Servicio de Parasitología y Chagas, Hospital de Niños Ricardo Gutiérrez, Buenos Aires, Argentina.

A weakness of our study is that it lacks an untreated control group due to the fact that the standard of care for pediatric CD is the treatment of infected children as soon as possible after diagnosis.
