310.Rationale: A nested case-control comparing early versus later treatment timing could be done, but it addresses timing of treatment relative to infection rather than waning of protection over time since treatment. Also, a case-control approach provides odds ratios and does not readily account for censoring and time-to-event dynamics inherent in long-term follow-up. Confounding by indication (children treated later may differ systematically) would also bias estimates. Therefore this is not the most appropriate method for assessing waning protection over time in a treated cohort.

310.Option: Perform a time-dependent Cox regression model with treatment duration as a time-varying covariate to assess changes in risk over time
Evidence: Bz (5–8 mg/kg in 2 or 3 doses) or Nf (10–12 mg/kg in 2 or 3 doses) was prescribed for 60–90 days at the time of diagnosis.
The kinetics of T. cruzi serum antibodies were analyzed using survival analysis.

A descriptive study of cardiological, parasitological and serological results in a cohort of pediatric CD patients, with at least 6 years’ post-treatment follow-up, treated with benznidazole (Bz) or nifurtimox (Nf) was conducted at the Servicio de Parasitología y Chagas, Hospital de Niños Ricardo Gutiérrez, Buenos Aires, Argentina.
310.Rationale: A Cox model allowing hazards to vary over time (or including time-varying effects) can be appropriate to evaluate waning, but the option's phrasing—'treatment duration as a time-varying covariate'—is mis-specified for this setting because treatment is a short, fixed course (60–90 days) rather than an ongoing exposure whose duration varies to drive waning. More appropriate would be a Cox model using time since treatment (or interactions with time) or modeling time-varying hazard ratios to assess changing risk. As written, the option suggests the wrong time-varying covariate, so it is not the best choice without re-specification.

311.Option: The genetic similarity between Bhutanese DENV-1 strains and a specific clade of DENV-1 from northern India strongly indicates recent cross-border transmission
Evidence: All 33 Bhutan sequences group to genotype V, using the classification of Weaver et al [21], and were located in the same group as sequences from northern India, categorized as clade IX by Dash et al [22].

Within clade IX, 32 of the Bhutan DENV-1 E gene sequences (GenBank accession no. KP849860-7 and KP849869-92) grouped with the northern Indian sub-clade from 2008–2009, while 1 Bhutan DENV-1 sequence (GenBank accession no. KP849868) grouped with the northern Indian sub-clade from 2010–2011.

Most of the Bhutan sequences grouped with the 2008–2009 northern Indian sequences, indicating that the 2013–2014 dengue viruses from Bhutan mainly originated from 2008–2009 northern India sub-clade viruses.
The report of the first dengue outbreak in Bhutan suggested that DENV was imported from India, probably as early as 2004 [7].
311.Rationale: Phylogenetic grouping of Bhutan virus sequences tightly with sequences from northern India, including placement within the same clade and sub-clade, is direct genetic evidence of shared ancestry and is the strongest indicator of cross-border introduction rather than independent local emergence. The passage explicitly interprets these phylogenetic results as indicating that the Bhutan viruses mainly originated from northern India.

311.Option: The detection of DENV-1 in Bhutan during 2013–2014, which is the same serotype that was dominant in India after 2006, supports the hypothesis of cross-border transmission due to temporal concordance
Evidence: Since 2006, DENV-1 prevalence has increased in India [22, 35, 36].

Similarly, in this study, DENV-1 was predominant in 2013–2014, further supporting the notion that dengue epidemiological patterns in Bhutan and India are closely linked with likely exchange of strains between the two countries.
Of 58 positive cases, 53 (91.4%) were DENV-1, 3 (5.2%) DENV-2 and 2 (3.4%) DENV-3; no DENV-4 was detected.
311.Rationale: Temporal concordance—Bhutan's detection of predominantly DENV-1 in 2013–2014 occurring after DENV-1 became dominant in India since 2006—provides supportive epidemiologic evidence consistent with strains being introduced from India. The passage itself notes that this pattern "further support[s] the notion" of linked epidemiology and likely exchange of strains.

311.Option: The seasonal peak of cases during the monsoon months aligns with the transmission patterns in India, suggesting that the virus is imported from India during periods of high transmission
Evidence: In both years, the number of suspected and laboratory confirmed cases peaked during the summer months (June-September), which also corresponds to the monsoon season in Bhutan (Fig 2).

A distinct seasonality for DENV infection in the 2 southwest districts of Bhutan was observed. The number of cases was found to peak during the hot and humid monsoon season, probably due to increased habitats for mosquito breeding.
311.Rationale: While the passage documents a clear seasonal peak in Bhutan during the monsoon, seasonality alone does not identify source (local emergence vs. importation). Monsoon-driven increases in vector capacity can produce local outbreaks independent of cross-border introductions. The passage does not provide direct evidence that seasonal peaks in Bhutan are synchronized with India in a way that demonstrates importation; therefore seasonality is insufficient to strongly support cross-border introduction compared with genetic/phylogenetic evidence.

311.Option: The high proportion of cases among young adults aged 19–35 years suggests that the virus is newly introduced, as this age group is more likely to travel across borders and bring in infections
Evidence: Age of patients in this study ranged from 2 to 77 years but young adults, 19–35 years, accounted for the largest age group of suspected as well as laboratory confirmed cases; 178/379 (47%) and 68/119 (55.6%), respectively.
The majority of suspected and laboratory confirmed cases occurred in young adults aged 19–35 years.
The majority of cases being adults suggest that there are still many dengue-naïve or dengue monotypic individuals among the adult population in Bhutan.
311.Rationale: Although the data show most cases were in young adults, the passage attributes this to population susceptibility (many adults being dengue-naïve or monotypic) rather than to travel-introduced infections. The passage does not present evidence linking the affected age group to border travel or importation. Thus the age distribution alone does not strongly support the hypothesis of cross-border introduction.

311.Option: The co-circulation of multiple DENV serotypes in India and the detection of DENV-1, DENV-2, and DENV-3 in Bhutan suggest that the virus is being introduced from India due to the shared serotype diversity
Evidence: Bhutan shares a 700 km border with India, which continues to report co-circulation of all 4 DENV serotypes with increasing frequency [11].
Of 58 positive cases, 53 (91.4%) were DENV-1, 3 (5.2%) DENV-2 and 2 (3.4%) DENV-3; no DENV-4 was detected.
311.Rationale: Shared serotype diversity between neighboring countries is consistent with cross-border transmission, but it is an indirect indicator and less definitive than direct genetic/phylogenetic evidence tying specific strains together. The presence of multiple serotypes in both locations could also arise from independent introductions over time or limited local circulation. Because the passage provides explicit phylogenetic evidence as the strongest indicator of importation, serotype co-occurrence alone is a weaker line of evidence and therefore not the most persuasive support for cross-border introduction.

312.Option: The spatial clustering of high-prevalence sites in Ese-Odo, despite uniform PC implementation, indicates that individual-level risk factors such as age and sex are the main drivers of transmission differences.
Evidence: Spatial analysis revealed that in Ese-Odo, more than 60% of sampled sites had prevalence rates ranging from 26% to 81%, with these high-prevalence areas widely dispersed.

Since 2013, the Federal and State Ministries of Health, in collaboration with partners such as EndFUND and Mission to Save the Helpless (MITOSATH), have implemented more than nine rounds of preventive chemotherapy (PC) with albendazole [20]. To date, an average of five effective PC rounds with therapeutic coverage exceeding 75% has been achieved.

Ese-Odo had the poorest WASH conditions among the three LGAs, with a very considerable proportion of those sampled practicing open defecation, lacking access to handwashing facilities and relying heavily on rivers as their primary water source.
Social-ecological systems, particularly those related to WASH, are critical to the transmission of STH [10].
312.Rationale: This option is incorrect. The passage explicitly documents spatial clustering of high-prevalence sites in Ese-Odo and also documents that PC rounds with high coverage were implemented, which argues against lack of chemotherapy as the sole cause. Importantly the authors link high prevalence to poor WASH infrastructure (open defecation, lack of handwashing, reliance on rivers) and emphasize social-ecological (structural) drivers. Thus the spatial clustering more strongly indicates environmental/structural drivers rather than individual-level factors like age or sex.

312.Option: The higher prevalence of moderate and heavy infections in Ese-Odo, despite effective PC, indicates that individual-level compliance with medication was lower in that area compared to Irele and Ile-Oluji.
Evidence: To date, an average of five effective PC rounds with therapeutic coverage exceeding 75% has been achieved. The three LGAs included in this assessment have met the minimum requirement of 5–6 effective PC rounds, making them eligible for an impact assessment survey.

Despite conducting six effective PC rounds (coverage >75%) out of nine since 2013, the high transmission rate in Ese-Odo raises concerns about demographic factors, WASH access, and the quality of implementation over the past decade [25].

Ese-Odo had the poorest WASH conditions among the three LGAs, with a very considerable proportion of those sampled practicing open defecation, lacking access to handwashing facilities and relying heavily on rivers as their primary water source.
The continued transmission of STH in Ese-Odo a riverine LGA is largely attributed to inadequate access to and use of water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) facilities.
312.Rationale: This option is incorrect because the passage provides evidence that effective PC rounds with adequate coverage were implemented, so there is no direct evidence that lower individual compliance explains the residual moderate/heavy infections. The authors point to poor WASH and possibly implementation quality as explanations, with emphasis on structural/environmental conditions rather than simple non-compliance.

312.Option: The significant reduction in prevalence in Irele and Ile-Oluji, despite similar baseline endemicity levels, suggests that individual-level adherence to hygiene practices was more consistent in those areas compared to Ese-Odo.
Evidence: In Irele LGA, the baseline prevalence was 51.3%, placing it in the category requiring PC twice annually for 5 years.
In Ile-Oluji LGA, the baseline prevalence was 23%, placing it in the 20–50% endemicity category, which required annual PC for 5 years.
In Irele... the current impact assessment revealed a significant reduction in overall prevalence to 9.7%, moving the LGA into the 2–10% prevalence category.
In Ile-Oluji... the current impact assessment indicated a decrease in overall prevalence to 6.4%, positioning the LGA within the 2–10% threshold.

Ese-Odo had the poorest WASH conditions among the three LGAs, with a very considerable proportion of those sampled practicing open defecation, lacking access to handwashing facilities and relying heavily on rivers as their primary water source.
312.Rationale: This option is incorrect. First, the baseline endemicity levels were not the same across the three LGAs (Irele baseline ~51.3%, Ile-Oluji ~23%, Ese-Odo ~39%), so the premise of 'despite similar baseline endemicity' is false. Second, the passage attributes differential impact to differences in WASH/infrastructure (poorest in Ese-Odo) and discusses structural factors, rather than asserting that individual-level hygiene adherence alone explains the differences.

312.Option: The persistence of high prevalence in Ese-Odo despite effective preventive chemotherapy, coupled with the strongest risk factors being tied to infrastructure access rather than individual behavior, indicates that structural and environmental conditions are the primary drivers of transmission differences.
Evidence: To date, an average of five effective PC rounds with therapeutic coverage exceeding 75% has been achieved. The three LGAs included in this assessment have met the minimum requirement of 5–6 effective PC rounds, making them eligible for an impact assessment survey.
The continued transmission of STH in Ese-Odo a riverine LGA is largely attributed to inadequate access to and use of water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) facilities.

Ese-Odo had the poorest WASH conditions among the three LGAs, with a very considerable proportion of those sampled practicing open defecation, lacking access to handwashing facilities and relying heavily on rivers as their primary water source.

Ese-Odo stood out, with only 17.7% of households using improved sanitation facilities, 25% using improved water sources, and just 9% having access to handwashing facilities. Open defecation rates were alarmingly high at 54.2%

Regression models indicated that inadequate hygiene practices, such as failure to use soap after defecation, presented the highest risk for STH infection, followed by the use of unimproved latrines and water sources.

The implementation of the preventive chemotherapy (PC) program for soil-transmitted helminthiasis (STH) has proven highly effective in Irele and Ile-Oluji, with prevalence rates reduced to less than 10%. However, in Ese-Odo, the reduction in prevalence has been limited to 66%, with current rates still around 25% and moderate-to-heavy infections persisting at 2.3%.
312.Rationale: This option is correct. The passage shows that PC with adequate coverage occurred across areas but Ese-Odo still has persistent high prevalence and moderate/heavy infections. The authors explicitly attribute ongoing transmission in Ese-Odo to inadequate WASH access and document objective infrastructure deficits (low improved sanitation, high open defecation, little handwashing access, reliance on rivers). Regression results highlight infrastructure and water/sanitation as leading risk factors. Together these facts most strongly point to structural/environmental conditions driving the observed differences rather than individual behaviors alone.

312.Option: The fact that Ese-Odo has the highest proportion of children using rivers as a water source suggests that individual exposure to contaminated water is the primary driver of transmission, independent of broader environmental conditions.
Evidence: In Ese-Odo, the majority of the population, 579 individuals (80.2%), relied primarily on rivers as their main water source.

Ese-Odo had the poorest WASH conditions among the three LGAs, with a very considerable proportion of those sampled practicing open defecation, lacking access to handwashing facilities and relying heavily on rivers as their primary water source.

Ese-Odo stood out, with only 17.7% of households using improved sanitation facilities, 25% using improved water sources, and just 9% having access to handwashing facilities. Open defecation rates were alarmingly high at 54.2%
The continued transmission of STH in Ese-Odo a riverine LGA is largely attributed to inadequate access to and use of water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) facilities.
312.Rationale: This option is incorrect because, although reliance on rivers is documented and is a risk factor, the passage links transmission to a suite of structural/environmental deficiencies (poor sanitation, high open defecation, lack of handwashing infrastructure) rather than to an isolated individual-level exposure. The authors attribute ongoing transmission to broader WASH infrastructure problems, i.e., environmental/structural conditions, not solely individual exposure to river water independent of those conditions.

313.Option: The increased abundance of certain phylotypes in the biliary microbiome is a result of direct colonization by bacteria from the liver fluke's own microbiome during infection
Evidence: Also, helminth parasites can harbor endosymbiotic microbes, in particular the rickettsia-like bacteria of trematodes [22] and symbiotic Wolbachia of filariae [23].

findings in hamsters infected with Opisthorchis viverrini demonstrated that infection with this liver fluke not only modifies the intestinal microbiota but revealed the presence of >60 phylotypes of nine phyla in the biliary system associated with the parasites [11].

We speculate that Halogeometricum and Paracoccus aminovorans may have been transported to the biliary tract with ingested dried fish and/or other fish products contaminated with metacercarie of O. felineus.
313.Rationale: While the passage documents that trematodes can harbor endosymbiotic microbes and that hamster experiments showed phylotypes associated with the parasites (supporting the biological plausibility of direct transfer), the specific inference drawn by the authors about certain environmental phylotypes (e.g., Halogeometricum, Paracoccus) emphasizes transport with ingested fish products. Thus, although parasite-associated microbiota is possible, the study's discussion does not conclude that the increased abundance of those particular phylotypes is primarily due to direct colonization from the fluke microbiome; instead it favors an environmental/dietary origin for those taxa.

313.Option: Environmental bacteria detected in the biliary microbiome likely originated from ingested dried fish contaminated with liver fluke metacercariae
Evidence: S. changbaiensis is known from forest soils [12] and Paracoccus aminovorans associates with the skin of fish [65] [66].
Flesh of salted, dried river fishes represents a dietary stable in regions of Siberia [67].

We speculate that Halogeometricum and Paracoccus aminovorans may have been transported to the biliary tract with ingested dried fish and/or other fish products contaminated with metacercarie of O. felineus.
313.Rationale: The authors explicitly link certain detected phylotypes to environmental niches (soil, fish skin) and note that salted/dried river fish are a common dietary staple in the study region; they then directly speculate that those environmental taxa may have been transported to the bile via ingestion of contaminated fish products. Therefore the inference that these bacteria likely originated from ingested dried fish is directly supported by the text.

313.Option: Bacterial phylotypes detected in the biliary microbiome of infected individuals are likely contaminants introduced during sample collection or laboratory processing
Evidence: Control DNA extractions in which 100 μl sterile water replaced biliary DNA were undertaken, in order to address laboratory and sequence-based artifacts that can occur with reagents and kits [24].
OTUs present only in reagent control samples were subtracted from O. felineus infected and O. felineus non-infected groups to eliminate reads due to contamination.

During cholecystectomy, 5–10 ml of bile was aspirated from the gallbladder under sterile conditions, three to five ml dispensed into in a sterile tube, and thereafter dispatched immediately to the laboratory.
313.Rationale: The investigators implemented multiple measures to detect and remove laboratory/reagent contamination (control extractions and subtraction of OTUs found only in reagent controls) and collected bile under sterile conditions. While contamination is always a consideration in low-biomass studies, the passage describes steps taken to minimize and account for it, so the blanket inference that the detected phylotypes are likely contaminants is not supported by the study's methods or authors' discussion.

313.Option: Bacterial phylotypes found in the biliary microbiome of infected individuals are likely due to translocation from the gut microbiota following disruption of the intestinal barrier by liver fluke infection
Evidence: findings in hamsters infected with Opisthorchis viverrini demonstrated that infection with this liver fluke not only modifies the intestinal microbiota but revealed the presence of >60 phylotypes of nine phyla in the biliary system associated with the parasites [11].

infection of hamsters with O. viverrini positively correlated with increased co-infection with Helicobacter pylori and H. bilis, both in the fecal microbiota and in the biliary tract within the gut of the liver flukes [12].

Conversely, it had long been considered that during good health the bile was sterile or at least that bile was inimical to bacteria [9], with a few reports indicating colonization of the gallbladder and bile as the consequence of reflux of the duodenal contents, blood-borne infection and infection spread through the portal-venous channels [10].
313.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that liver fluke infection alters intestinal microbiota and that in experimental models gut-associated bacteria (e.g., Helicobacter spp.) are found both in feces and the biliary tract in association with flukes. The authors also cite known mechanisms (duodenal reflux, portal spread) by which gut microbes can colonize bile. These points support the inference that some biliary phylotypes in infected individuals may arise via translocation from the gut following parasite-related disruption or altered flow, making this a plausible origin.

313.Option: The biliary microbiome of individuals infected with O. felineus reflects a shift toward a more pathogenic microbial community due to chronic inflammation from liver fluke infection
Evidence: Most phylotypes that differed were detected in higher abundance (i.e. absolute read counts) in bile from the O. felineus-infected participants.

Haemophilus parainfluenzae also increased in O. felineus infected samples; this pathogen is associated with liver abscess [62], and liver abscess represents a serious complication of opisthorchiasis felinea [63].
The same predicted functional pathways characterized the O. felineus infected and O. felineus non-infected bile, so that functional differences were not evident.
313.Rationale: Although the study documents increased abundance of several taxa (including some with known pathogenic potential) in infected individuals, the authors found no differences in predicted functional metagenomic pathways between infected and uninfected groups. The presence of some potentially pathogenic species does not by itself demonstrate an overall shift to a more pathogenic community driven by chronic inflammation. The data support compositional changes, but not a demonstrated functional/pathogenic shift attributable to inflammation.

314.Option: A statistically significant increasing trend in placental lesion severity with higher numbers of P. vivax infections during pregnancy
Evidence: Women were divided according to the number of P. vivax (and not P. falciparum) infections experienced during pregnancy and the following group order was established: “no infection”, “1 infection” and “2+ infections”.

All histological parameters were evaluated between these groups and Cuzick's trend test across ordered groups was performed to assess presence of a trend with any of the parameters according to the number of P. vivax infections (Table 4). Both syncytial knots (z = 2.35; P = 0.019) and placental barrier thickness (z = 2.63; P = 0.008) were elevated with increased frequency of P. vivax infections.

Also, applying the score to the different groups, according to the number of P. vivax infections experienced during pregnancy, showed an increase in score values with increasing numbers of P. vivax infections (Cuzick's trend-test: z = 2.76; P = 0.006).

By developing a histology score based on the most significant placental histology parameters associated with P. vivax, we show that women from an endemic region of the Brazilian Amazon, who were diagnosed with P. vivax during pregnancy, had higher levels of placental lesions than non-infected pregnant women and that these lesions increase in frequency with increasing exposure to the parasite.
314.Rationale: The strongest indication that pathology is linked to cumulative exposure is a dose–response or trend with increasing number of infections. The passage reports statistically significant Cuzick trend test results showing higher values of specific placental lesions (syncytial knots and barrier thickness) and an increasing vivax-score as the number of P. vivax infections rises (P-values 0.019, 0.008, and 0.006). Those significant trends directly support cumulative exposure rather than a single infection effect.

314.Option: Higher mean placental lesion scores in women with P. vivax infection compared to uninfected women, regardless of the number of infections
Evidence: Placentas belonging to the “vivax” group exhibited higher scores than those from the “no plasmodium” group (Mann-Whitney, P = 0.027).

When women were divided according to the number of P. vivax ... infections ... applying the score to the different groups ... showed an increase in score values with increasing numbers of P. vivax infections (Cuzick's trend-test: z = 2.76; P = 0.006).

we observed that certain histological parameters were increased in women who had experienced two or more P. vivax infections during pregnancy when compared to women who had experienced a single episode of P. vivax or women who were not diagnosed with Plasmodium sp. during pregnancy (Table 4).
314.Rationale: While the passage does state that vivax-group placentas had higher mean scores than uninfected placentas (P=0.027), the evidence also shows that lesion severity increases with the number of infections. The phrase 'regardless of the number of infections' is not supported: the data indicate an exposure-response relationship (higher scores with more infections). Thus the observed higher mean alone does not most strongly indicate cumulative exposure, so this option is incorrect for the question's emphasis.

314.Option: A statistically significant increase in placental barrier thickness and syncytial knotting in women with P. vivax infection compared to uninfected women, with no evidence of confounding by parity
Evidence: A significant increase in the percentage of syncytial knots (ANOVA, P = 0.006) (Figs. 1A and 1B) was observed in the placentas from the P. falciparum-exposed women. The values of syncytial knotting were increased in women in the “falciparum” group versus women in the “no-plasmodium” group, while women in the “vivax” group exhibited intermediate syncytial knotting values.

This same pattern was also observed for the presence of syncytial rupture (ANOVA, P = 0.003) ... and the thickness of the placental barrier (ANOVA, P = 0.08) ... although no statistical significance was obtained with this last parameter.
No significant differences were observed between the “no plasmodium” and “vivax” groups when they were sub-divided according to parity (data not shown).
314.Rationale: The option asserts a statistically significant increase in those parameters comparing vivax-infected versus uninfected women. The passage shows that syncytial knotting was most pronounced in falciparum placentas with vivax intermediate (not a clear significant difference vivax vs uninfected), and placental barrier thickness versus groups had ANOVA P=0.08 (not significant). The passage does note no differences by parity between groups (supporting lack of parity confounding), but the core claim of statistically significant increases comparing vivax to uninfected is not supported. Therefore the option is incorrect.

314.Option: A significant association between P. vivax infection and lower infant birth weight in primigravidae, suggesting that repeated exposure may be more harmful
Evidence: When women in the “no plasmodium” and “vivax” groups were sub-divided into those with one pregnancy (primigravidae) and those with two or more pregnancies (multigravidae) the only significant difference was of birth weight of the primigravidae, with the mean birth weight of infants in the “no plasmodium” group 350 g higher than that of the “vivax” group (student's t-test, P = 0.03)

The biological significance of this finding, although similar to previous results [5], [7], [27], could not be determined in this study since birth weight is a multidimensional outcome that depends not only on exposure and immunity to malaria [28], [29], but also on anthropometric features of both the parents and the infant [30], [31].

we observed that certain histological parameters were increased in women who had experienced two or more P. vivax infections during pregnancy when compared to women who had experienced a single episode of P. vivax or women who were not diagnosed with Plasmodium sp. during pregnancy (Table 4).
314.Rationale: Although there is a reported lower mean birth weight among primigravidae with vivax infection (P=0.03), that finding reflects parity-specific vulnerability, not evidence that pathology is linked to cumulative (multiple) infections. The passage itself cautions about interpreting the biological significance of the birth-weight difference. The stronger evidence for cumulative exposure is the documented trend of histological lesions with number of infections. Thus this option does not most strongly indicate cumulative exposure and is incorrect.

314.Option: A significant difference in placental lesion scores between women with one P. vivax infection and those with two or more infections, but not between uninfected women and those with one infection
Evidence: we observed that certain histological parameters were increased in women who had experienced two or more P. vivax infections during pregnancy when compared to women who had experienced a single episode of P. vivax or women who were not diagnosed with Plasmodium sp. during pregnancy (Table 4).

Women were divided according to the number of P. vivax ... infections ... 'no infection', '1 infection' and '2+ infections'. All histological parameters were evaluated between these groups and Cuzick's trend test across ordered groups was performed ... Both syncytial knots (z = 2.35; P = 0.019) and placental barrier thickness (z = 2.63; P = 0.008) were elevated with increased frequency of P. vivax infections.

Placentas belonging to the “vivax” group exhibited higher scores than those from the “no plasmodium” group (Mann-Whitney, P = 0.027). Also, applying the score to the different groups, according to the number of P. vivax infections experienced during pregnancy, showed an increase in score values with increasing numbers of P. vivax infections (Cuzick's trend-test: z = 2.76; P = 0.006).
314.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that several histological parameters and the composite vivax-score increase as the number of P. vivax infections rises, and it notes that parameters were higher in women with two or more infections compared with those with a single episode or with no diagnosis. That pattern (2+ infections showing greater lesion scores than 1 infection, and the trend test significance) most directly supports cumulative exposure driving pathology; the lack of consistent significant differences between uninfected and single-infection groups is also implied by statements that changes are strongest with repeated infections. Therefore this option correctly captures the evidence.

315.Option: Neutrophil recruitment is the primary determinant of worm burden, with S100A8/A9 acting as a chemotactic signal to enhance early larval clearance
Evidence: S100A8/A9 exhibits chemotactic properties on neutrophils [60] and is expressed in almost 20% of the cytosolic protein content in murine neutrophils and 45% in human neutrophils,
Subcutaneous L. sigmodontis infection in S100A8/A9-/- mice resulted in a significantly reduced worm burden compared to WT infected animals (Fig 1B).

Comparable total numbers of neutrophils in the thoracic cavity and BAL of S100A8/A9-/- mice upon L. sigmodontis infection is in contrast to previous studies demonstrating a S100A8 and S100A9-dependent recruitment of murine and human phagocytes to the site of inflammation.

Depletion of neutrophils led to a significantly increased worm recovery in S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to isotype-treated S100A8/A9-/- controls, resulting in a worm burden that was comparable to isotype-treated WT controls (Fig 5).
315.Rationale: Although S100A8/A9 has been described as chemotactic for neutrophils, the study's primary findings do not support the statement that S100A8/A9 enhances early larval clearance via increased neutrophil recruitment. In this investigation, S100A8/A9-/- mice had a reduced worm burden (not increased) and total neutrophil numbers in thoracic cavity and BAL were comparable between genotypes, indicating recruitment per se was not the primary determinant. Instead, protection in S100A8/A9-/- mice was linked to increased neutrophil activation/function (and was lost when neutrophils were depleted), not to S100A8/A9-driven recruitment enhancing clearance. Therefore the option's causal claim is not supported by the passage.

315.Option: S100A8/A9 promotes vascular permeability to facilitate larval migration through the lung endothelium during early infection
Evidence: We assessed the vascular permeability in S100A8/A9-/- mice and WT controls, as this was previously shown to facilitate L3 migration as well [58]. Intradermal injection of filarial adult worm extract (LsAg) led to an increased permeability in WT and S100A8/A9-/- animals compared to PBS controls (Fig 6C). However no statistically significant differences were observed between LsAg-treated WT and S100A8/A9-/- mice, suggesting that the absence of S100A8/A9 does not impact vascular permeability.
315.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that vascular permeability after LsAg injection increased compared to PBS but did not differ between WT and S100A8/A9-/- mice. Thus there is no evidence that S100A8/A9 promotes vascular permeability to facilitate larval migration in this model; the experimental data argue against differential effects of S100A8/A9 on vascular permeability.

315.Option: The protective effect of S100A8/A9 deficiency is mediated by enhanced eosinophil activation and increased mucus production in the lung
Evidence: L3 inoculation in S100A8/A9-/- mice resulted in a significantly increased total cell number of eosinophils (Fig 2B), neutrophils (Fig 2C) and macrophages (Fig 2D) in comparison to PBS-injected S100A8/A9-/- mice.

Lung pathology in S100A8/A9-/- mice contained by trend less inflamed/congested blood vessels (p = 0.14; S7E Fig) and less bronchi with goblet cells in comparison to infected WT animals (p = 0.22; S7F Fig).

Depletion of neutrophils led to a significantly increased worm recovery in S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to isotype-treated S100A8/A9-/- controls, resulting in a worm burden that was comparable to isotype-treated WT controls (Fig 5).

L3 larval motility was significantly reduced in vitro in the presence of S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils compared to WT neutrophils in a DNA and NET formation-dependent manner, a mechanism that was previously described as neutrophil response to third stage larvae of several helminth species [41,62].
315.Rationale: Although S100A8/A9-/- mice show some increases in local cell counts including eosinophils, the study links the protective phenotype chiefly to neutrophil activation/function (increased neutrophil-associated chemokines, neutrophil enzyme release, NET-dependent inhibition of L3 motility) and demonstrates that neutrophil depletion abrogates protection. Moreover, mucus-producing goblet cells in the lung (a marker of mucus production) were by trend reduced, not increased, in S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to WT. Therefore the evidence does not support eosinophil activation and increased mucus as the primary mediators of protection.

315.Option: S100A8/A9 acts as a systemic immunosuppressant that dampens all innate immune responses, leading to increased larval survival in wild-type mice
Evidence: Our data indicates an anti-inflammatory role of S100A8/S100A9 on neutrophil activation, which reduced inflammatory responses and facilitated the migration of infective L3 larvae into the thoracic cavity.
Collectively our data suggest that S100A8/A9 inhibits L3-induced inflammation by decreasing chemokine production and neutrophil activation.
However, the impact of S100A8/A9 may differ depending on the inflammatory milieu and location [2,3].

Intranasal depletion of neutrophils in S100A8/A9-/- animals during the migratory phase of L. sigmodontis increased the worm recovery from the thoracic cavity, indicating that neutrophils mediate protection in S100A8/A9-/- animals, although the specific location of this neutrophil-dependent effect could not be determined as a systemic neutrophil depletion occurred.
315.Rationale: While the passage shows S100A8/A9 has an anti-inflammatory effect (particularly dampening neutrophil activation and chemokine production) that can facilitate larval migration, it does not support the sweeping claim that S100A8/A9 is a systemic immunosuppressant that dampens all innate responses. The authors note tissue- and context-specific effects and specifically describe modulation of neutrophil activation rather than global innate suppression. Therefore the option is overly broad and not supported by the data.

315.Option: S100A8/A9 modulates neutrophil activation to balance protective immunity and excessive inflammation during early larval migration
Evidence: Our data indicates an anti-inflammatory role of S100A8/S100A9 on neutrophil activation, which reduced inflammatory responses and facilitated the migration of infective L3 larvae into the thoracic cavity.

Subcutaneous L. sigmodontis infection in S100A8/A9-/- mice resulted in a significantly reduced worm burden compared to WT infected animals (Fig 1B). The reduced number of worms was associated with a significant increase in thoracic cavity cells in the S100A8/A9-/- mice (Fig 1C), while total cell counts of CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, neutrophils, macrophages, and eosinophils in the thoracic cavity were not different between the two mouse strains (Fig 1D). However, levels of neutrophil-associated chemokines, i.e. CXCL-1 (Fig 1E), CXCL-2 (Fig 1F), CXCL-5 (Fig 1G), and elastase (Fig 1H) were significantly increased in the thoracic cavity lavage of S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to WT controls.

Myeloperoxidase (MPO) as well as elastase, enzymes which are abundantly expressed in neutrophils, were increasingly released (MPO: p<0.05; elastase: p = 0.11) from bone marrow-derived neutrophils of S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to WT neutrophils at baseline conditions in vitro (Fig 4A and 4B).

L3 larvae cultured with S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils showed an earlier inhibition of L3 motility compared to L3 larvae cultured with neutrophils of WT mice (Fig 4E). Addition of DNase abrogated the inhibitory effect of S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils on L3 motility, suggesting that the effect is DNA trap-dependent.

Depletion of neutrophils led to a significantly increased worm recovery in S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to isotype-treated S100A8/A9-/- controls, resulting in a worm burden that was comparable to isotype-treated WT controls (Fig 5).

Collectively our data suggest that S100A8/A9 inhibits L3-induced inflammation by decreasing chemokine production and neutrophil activation. Given that the phenotype of the S100A8/A9-/- mice with a reduced worm burden was abolished following neutrophil depletion and S100A8/A9 deficient neutrophils were shown to impair L3 motility more efficiently in vitro, it can be assumed that neutrophils are involved in the enhanced clearance of L3 larvae in S100A8/A9 deficient mice.
315.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence that S100A8/A9 influences neutrophil activation: S100A8/A9 has an anti-inflammatory effect on neutrophil activation, S100A8/A9-/- mice show increased neutrophil-associated chemokines and neutrophil enzyme release, S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils more effectively inhibit L3 motility via NET/DNA-dependent mechanisms, and neutrophil depletion removes the protective phenotype. Together these findings support the interpretation that S100A8/A9 modulates neutrophil activation to tune the balance between protective (enhanced neutrophil-mediated parasite clearance) and potentially pathogenic/excessive inflammation during early larval migration.

316.Option: The lack of detectable viremia indicates that T-705 may have induced a sterilizing immunity, preventing any viral replication from occurring.
Evidence: All animals of the treatment group survived the infection and showed no signs of disease. Virus was detected neither in blood nor in the organs throughout the observation period.

To provide evidence for infection of the animals in the T-705 treatment groups, the development of CCHFV-specific antibodies was measured 21 days p.i. Only 1/10 (10%) of the animals treated post-exposure, but 10/10 (100%) of the animals treated from day 1 or 2 p.i. developed antibodies, indicating that virus replication under post-exposure treatment with T-705 was even not sufficient to elicit antibodies.
If given immediately post-exposure, the drug suppresses virus replication below the level required to elicit antibodies.
316.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that although virus was undetectable, antibody testing showed that many animals treated post-exposure did not develop antibodies because viral replication was suppressed below the level required to elicit an immune response. This indicates that absence of detectable viremia does not necessarily mean sterilizing immunity (i.e., complete prevention of any replication); rather, the drug can suppress replication to levels below detection and below the threshold needed to trigger antibody production. Therefore the sterile-immunity interpretation is not supported by the data.

316.Option: The absence of detectable viremia reflects complete suppression of viral replication below the threshold for immune activation, not a failure of infection or treatment
Evidence: All animals of the treatment group survived the infection and showed no signs of disease. Virus was detected neither in blood nor in the organs throughout the observation period.

To provide evidence for infection of the animals in the T-705 treatment groups, the development of CCHFV-specific antibodies was measured 21 days p.i. Only 1/10 (10%) of the animals treated post-exposure, but 10/10 (100%) of the animals treated from day 1 or 2 p.i. developed antibodies, indicating that virus replication under post-exposure treatment with T-705 was even not sufficient to elicit antibodies.
If given immediately post-exposure, the drug suppresses virus replication below the level required to elicit antibodies.
316.Rationale: The passage directly supports this interpretation: high-dose T-705 given post-exposure rendered virus undetectable in blood and organs, and the authors note that drug treatment suppressed replication below the level required to elicit antibodies in many animals. Thus lack of detectable viremia should be interpreted as profound suppression of replication (possibly below detection and immune-activation thresholds) rather than as evidence that infection never occurred or that treatment failed.

316.Option: The lack of detectable viremia may indicate that the animals were not truly infected, suggesting a flaw in the infection protocol or inoculation dose.
Evidence: Animals died from the infection even after inoculation with 0.3 FFU (inoculum, died/infected: 0.3 FFU, 4/5; 1 FFU, 5/5; 3 FFU, 4/5; 10 FFU, 6/8; 100 FFU 13/13; 1,000 FFU, 8/8; 10,000 FFU, 3/3).

To provide evidence for infection of the animals in the T-705 treatment groups, the development of CCHFV-specific antibodies was measured 21 days p.i. Only 1/10 (10%) of the animals treated post-exposure, but 10/10 (100%) of the animals treated from day 1 or 2 p.i. developed antibodies, indicating that virus replication under post-exposure treatment with T-705 was even not sufficient to elicit antibodies.
316.Rationale: The mouse model and inoculation protocol are well established and highly infectious at very low doses (the passage documents infection and death even after 0.3 FFU), so a failure to infect is unlikely. The authors specifically measured antibody responses to confirm infection and interpreted absent antibodies in treated animals as evidence that replication was suppressed by the drug, not that infection never occurred. Therefore interpreting lack of viremia as a flaw in infection protocol is not supported.

316.Option: The lack of detectable viremia suggests that the high dose of T-705 may have caused immune suppression, preventing the detection of viral antigens.
Evidence: All animals of the treatment group survived the infection and showed no signs of disease. Virus was detected neither in blood nor in the organs throughout the observation period.

To provide evidence for infection of the animals in the T-705 treatment groups, the development of CCHFV-specific antibodies was measured 21 days p.i. Only 1/10 (10%) of the animals treated post-exposure, but 10/10 (100%) of the animals treated from day 1 or 2 p.i. developed antibodies, indicating that virus replication under post-exposure treatment with T-705 was even not sufficient to elicit antibodies.
If given immediately post-exposure, the drug suppresses virus replication below the level required to elicit antibodies.
316.Rationale: The passage attributes absence of detectable virus and low antibody responses to suppression of viral replication by T-705, not to immune suppression caused by the drug. There is no mention of drug-induced immune suppression; instead, the interpretation is that viral replication was reduced below thresholds for detection and immune activation. Thus immune suppression as the cause is not supported by the data presented.

316.Option: The lack of detectable viremia may be due to the timing of sample collection, as viral load may have been transiently suppressed and later rebounded.
Evidence: All animals of the treatment group survived the infection and showed no signs of disease. Virus was detected neither in blood nor in the organs throughout the observation period.

To provide evidence for infection of the animals in the T-705 treatment groups, the development of CCHFV-specific antibodies was measured 21 days p.i. Only 1/10 (10%) of the animals treated post-exposure, but 10/10 (100%) of the animals treated from day 1 or 2 p.i. developed antibodies, indicating that virus replication under post-exposure treatment with T-705 was even not sufficient to elicit antibodies.

To determine the efficacy of the drug at an advanced stage of the infection, time-of-addition experiments were performed (Fig. 6). Treatment with a high dose of the drug was commenced 1 day or 2 days after virus inoculation and continued until day 8. Survival was 100% in both groups and animals showed hardly any signs of disease. Only if treatment started 2 days p.i., minor changes in weight, temperature, and AST were seen at day 3. Virus remained undetectable in blood and organs in both time-of-addition groups throughout the observation period.
316.Rationale: The investigators explicitly report that virus remained undetectable in blood and organs throughout the observation period, including in time-of-addition experiments where treatment was started at different times post-inoculation. They also assessed antibody responses at 21 days p.i. to check for evidence of infection or rebound. Thus the data argue against a simple artifact of sampling timing or a later viral rebound during the monitored period; instead, the drug appears to have sustained suppression of detectable replication in these experiments.

317.Option: Parasite-induced increases in vector biting rates enhance transmission while reducing vector fitness, creating a stable equilibrium in natural populations
Evidence: Perhaps the most elegant demonstration that the interests of parasite and vector are not entirely aligned is parasite-induced increases in biting rates in mosquitoes [5–9], sand flies [10] and tsetse flies [11,12]—this is likely to increase transmission (and thereby fitness) of the parasite while the vector is liable to suffer a reduction in fitness due to excessive energy expenditure and increased risk of mortality when attempting to bite.
317.Rationale: The passage clearly supports that parasite-induced increases in biting rates can increase parasite transmission while harming vector fitness (quote above). However, it does not state that this mechanism "creates a stable equilibrium in natural populations." The passage emphasizes trade-offs and examples of altered behavior increasing transmission, but it does not claim stability of equilibrium as the critical balancing factor. Therefore the first half of the option is supported, but the asserted conclusion about creating a stable equilibrium is not supported by the passage; overall the option is not correct as the asked "most critical" factor in natural settings.

317.Option: The duration of the vector's feeding cycle determines the window for parasite transmission, thereby balancing parasite fitness and vector survival
Evidence: Trypanosoma cruzi has been reported to take approximately up to month at 28°C to colonize the intestine, reach the rectum and differentiate into infective stages [57, 58]. As triatomines will only feed again after they have moulted, it would benefit the parasite if their moult, and thus the next bloodmeal, were delayed until such a time as the parasite is in the right place and life stage to be transmitted to a vertebrate host. Such a delay would then favor parasite transmission.
317.Rationale: The passage supports that timing of feeding (via delay of moulting and thus the next bloodmeal) can influence the parasite's opportunity for transmission. However, the question asks which factor most critically influences the balance between parasite fitness and vector survival in natural settings; the passage repeatedly highlights temperature-dependent parasite growth and vector thermal preference as the central determinant of this trade-off. While feeding-cycle timing is relevant and discussed as adaptive for transmission, it is not presented as the most critical factor—the authors emphasize temperature as key—so this option is not correct as the single most critical factor.

317.Option: The presence of gut symbionts such as Rhodococcus rhodnii modulates parasite-induced virulence by competing for nutritional resources in the vector's intestine
Evidence: Previous studies showed no effect of T. cruzi on the development of Nocardia sp. and Rhodococcus rhodnii, gut symbionts of Triatoma infestans and Rhodnius prolixus, respectively [25].
as a consequence of living only in the insect intestinal tract, T. cruzi probably competes with its host for nutritional resources.
317.Rationale: The passage notes (1) prior studies found no effect of T. cruzi on the development of gut symbionts such as Rhodococcus rhodnii, and (2) that T. cruzi probably competes with its host for nutritional resources. It does not provide evidence that gut symbionts modulate parasite-induced virulence by competing for resources; indeed the cited study found no effect on symbiont development. Therefore the specific claim that symbionts like R. rhodnii modulate virulence in this way is not supported by the passage.

317.Option: The parasite's ability to persist in the vector over long periods ensures sustained transmission, thereby minimizing harm to the vector and maintaining fitness balance
Evidence: At this range of temperatures, the parasite has a high in vitro growth rate (Fig. 2) so we hypothesize that its strategy in the vector is close to unrestrained growth, trading off an increased chance of transmission (due to a high population density in the intestine) with the cost of killing its vector and so effecting zero transmission.
In fact, it has already been demonstrated that triatomines can eliminate T. cruzi infections after long periods of starvation [39].
317.Rationale: The passage contradicts the notion that persistence necessarily minimizes harm. It describes a trade-off in which high parasite growth can kill the vector (leading to zero transmission), and also notes that vectors can eliminate infections after long starvation. Thus persistence does not guarantee minimized harm or maintenance of fitness balance; on the contrary, high within-vector growth (persistence/growth) at certain temperatures can harm vectors. Therefore this option is not supported.

317.Option: Temperature-dependent parasite growth rate relative to vector thermal preference determines the trade-off between transmission success and vector survival
Evidence: Temperature is a factor of particular importance in host-parasite interactions, especially when the host is ectothermic.
We therefore chose to conduct our study under four thermal regimes, and to use a comparatively narrow range of temperatures to keep the test conservative.
The population growth of T. cruzi in vitro increased consistently with increasing temperature (Fig. 2; p<0.0001 for temperature effect).

This accelerated mortality of R. prolixus infected with T. cruzi, under conditions of starvation (commonly experienced by these insects—[28]), was seen over a narrow range of temperatures (i.e., at 24 and 27°C but not at 21 or 30°C).
R. prolixus prefers temperatures of 25.0–25.4°C when offered a choice and performs best around these temperatures [40].

At this range of temperatures, the parasite has a high in vitro growth rate (Fig. 2) so we hypothesize that its strategy in the vector is close to unrestrained growth, trading off an increased chance of transmission (due to a high population density in the intestine) with the cost of killing its vector and so effecting zero transmission.
317.Rationale: The passage repeatedly identifies temperature as the key determinant of the balance between parasite fitness and vector survival: parasite growth rates rise with temperature, vector mortality due to infection is elevated at temperatures near the vector's thermal preference (24–27°C, with the insect preferring ~25°C), and this produces a trade-off where high within-vector parasite growth increases transmission potential but can kill the vector. These explicit statements support that temperature-dependent parasite growth relative to vector thermal preference is the most critical factor in the natural balance described.

318.Option: A genetic variant in the chitotriosidase gene leading to reduced enzyme activity
Evidence: After traumatic inoculation of the causative agent into the subcutaneous tissue, usually in the sole of the foot, the disease progresses and invades the deep structures and the skin. Multiple nodules and sinuses discharging pus are formed and grains develop.

Chitinases hydrolyze chitin, which is the main component of the fungal cell wall. Decreased chitinase activity could, therefore, result in an enhanced susceptibility towards infections by chitin bearing fungi.

In the gene encoding for chitotriosidase, a 24-bp insertion in exon 10 was described previously [12, 14, 19, 20]. It was described elsewhere that chitotriosidase activity is reduced when patients are heterozygous for this insertion and completely absent when they were homozygous for this allele [12, 14].

The insertion containing allele was found significantly more frequently in the patients than in the control group (p = 0.004). Based on these data, the 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene does increase the risk for invasive mycetoma disease caused by M. mycetomatis (odds ratio 2.9; 95% CI 1.4–6.1).

Next to demonstrating that AMCase and chitotriosidase were present at the site of infection, we also provided evidence that a polymorphism in the gene for chitotriosidase, resulting in impaired enzyme activity, significantly increased the risk for the development of eumycetoma.
318.Rationale: The question describes a hereditary (genetic) phagocyte-related defect that leads to susceptibility to granulomatous, tissue-destructive infections with sinus tract formation — the clinical picture of mycetoma in the passage. The passage directly reports a 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene that reduces or abolishes enzyme activity and that this allele is significantly more frequent among patients, with an OR 2.9 (95% CI 1.4–6.1). Chitotriosidase is produced by phagocytic cells and degrades fungal chitin; reduced activity therefore represents a host genetic (hereditary) phagocyte-related dysfunction that increases risk of the described disease manifestations.

318.Option: The presence of chitin in the fungal grain, which triggers a host immune response involving chitinases
Evidence: This figure shows that the chitin is mainly found in the hyphae inside the grain.
Chitinases are part of the innate immune system and, in general, their production by macrophages and other cells is upregulated by the host’s tissue exposure to chitin.
Since chitin is present in the hyphae inside the grain, we hypothesized that the host produces chitinases, in reaction to exposure to chitin.
318.Rationale: Presence of chitin in the fungal grain is a pathogen/fungal characteristic that elicits a host response (production of chitinases), but it is not a host genetic/phagocyte defect. The question asks for a host factor that would indicate higher risk for hereditary phagocyte dysfunction; chitin in the grain indicates exposure and a stimulus for immune response, not an inherited impairment of phagocyte function.

318.Option: A polymorphism in the AMCase gene that results in decreased enzyme activity
Evidence: Neither the A50G and the A290G SNP, nor the 10-bp insertion in the 5’UTR region of AMCase is associated with invasive mycetoma

Allele frequencies of A50G and A290G in the patients were similar to those in the controls (p = 0.65; odds ratio 1.2; 95% CI 0.6–2.1; and p = 0.72; odds ratio 1.1; 95% CI 0.7–1.8, respectively). Furthermore, no difference was found in the presence of the 10-bp insertion between patients and controls (p = 0.72; odds ratio 1.1; 95% CI 0.7–1.8).

Next to demonstrating that AMCase and chitotriosidase were present at the site of infection, we also provided evidence that a polymorphism in the gene for chitotriosidase, resulting in impaired enzyme activity, significantly increased the risk for the development of eumycetoma. Increased or decreased activity of the alternative human chitinase, AMCase, did not have a significant influence on the risk for eumycetoma.
318.Rationale: Although AMCase is a human chitinase, the study found no association between AMCase polymorphisms (A50G, A290G, or the 10-bp insertion) and risk of invasive mycetoma. Therefore an AMCase polymorphism resulting in decreased activity would not most likely indicate the hereditary phagocyte dysfunction described; the evidence implicates chitotriosidase, not AMCase.

318.Option: A Th2-polarized immune response that results in increased production of matrix metalloproteinases and tissue inhibitor of MMP-1
Evidence: The development of mycetoma is associated with a Th2 response.

Sandler et al [28] showed that mice with a Th2 response have increased expression of matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) and of tissue inhibitor of MMP-1 (TIMP-1). Furthermore, AMCase was induced in Th2-polarized mice [28], which was confirmed by several other studies [29–31].

Based on the data of this study and of other studies, we created the following hypothesis. When a host is exposed to M. mycetomatis, it will try to eliminate the pathogen. Most individuals will respond with a Th1 polarized response. In this response acute inflammation develops, chitotriosidase is produced and no grain is formed. When the response is mainly Th2-polarized, a chronic inflammatory process in which both chitotriosidase and AMCase are produced, results in the formation of granulomas with grains.
318.Rationale: A Th2-polarized response is associated with granuloma/grain formation and with increased MMP/TIMP expression in experimental models, and thus relates mechanistically to disease pathology. However, the question specifies a hereditary phagocyte dysfunction (i.e., an inherited defect in phagocyte function) that predisposes to recurrent infections and tissue-destructive granulomatous disease. Th2 polarization is an immunologic phenotype (acquired or environmentally influenced), not a hereditary phagocyte genetic defect; the study implicates a genetic chitotriosidase variant as the hereditary risk factor.

318.Option: A history of schistosomiasis, which is associated with a Th2-polarized immune response
Evidence: Several environmental and patient-related factors have been described to influence the risk for the development of mycetoma, including concurrent schistosomiasis [5].
Indirect evidence for a Th2 response was also found in the association between schistosomiasis, associated with a Th2 response, and eumycetoma [5].
318.Rationale: Concurrent schistosomiasis is an environmental/co-infection factor associated with a Th2-biased immune response and has been linked epidemiologically to increased mycetoma risk. However, it is not a hereditary phagocyte dysfunction. The question asks for a host factor that would most likely indicate a higher risk for developing hereditary phagocyte dysfunction; schistosomiasis is an acquired infection/co-factor, not an inherited defect in phagocyte function.

319.Option: The high prevalence of trachoma in Njoge, where most positive environmental samples were found, indicates that a single dominant transmission route is responsible for sustained disease in hyperendemic areas
Evidence: The majority of our positive environment swabs came from the one village, Njoge, with the highest prevalence of trachoma.

This difference suggests that the practices of children and caretakers in Njoge may contribute to diverse environmental presence of Chlamydia, which in turn may be contributing to the high levels of trachoma.
This difference has been attributed to the multiple routes of transmission in hyperendemic areas such that elimination of any one source has minimal effect on transmission [14].
319.Rationale: The option asserts a single dominant route based on Njoge's high prevalence, but the passage explicitly interprets the Njoge findings as indicative of diverse environmental presence and attributes differences to multiple routes of transmission. The quoted sentences show that the authors conclude practices in Njoge likely contribute to diverse environmental contamination and that in hyperendemic areas multiple routes operate such that eliminating one source has little effect. Therefore the option is inconsistent with the passage and is incorrect.

319.Option: The fact that all positive environmental samples were collected from households with children who had active trachoma suggests that environmental contamination is a consequence of infection rather than a driver of transmission
Evidence: Obtaining at least one positive environmental swab from the household was associated with village of residence (ranging from 0% in Mkutani and Banyi Banyi to 34% in Njoge, p = 0.011) and presence of ocular infection in the index child (percent positive swabs 34% vs. 3%, p = 0.001) (Table 4).

Not surprisingly, positive environmental swabs were associated with the presence of ocular infection in the index child, which no doubt increased the likelihood that we would find contaminated surfaces.
The findings do not mean that the environmental source contained live organisms capable of transmission, as we did not test for replicating organisms or load.
319.Rationale: While the passage reports a strong association between presence of ocular infection in the index child and finding positive environmental swabs and cautions that contamination may reflect current infection, the statement in the option is both factually overstated (the passage does not claim "all" positives were from households with active trachoma) and does not address the question about multiple, overlapping pathways. The authors explicitly discuss environmental contamination as a potential contributor to transmission (not solely a consequence) and emphasize multiple routes in hyperendemic areas. Thus this option does not represent the factor that most strongly suggests multiple overlapping pathways and is incorrect.

319.Option: The presence of C. trachomatis DNA on household furniture and children’s hands indicates that flies are the primary vector for transmission in hyperendemic areas
Evidence: Locations with the highest proportion of positive swabs were household furniture (18%) and children’s hands (8%).

In studies of the contribution of flies to transmission of trachoma and infection, flies seem to play a larger role in transmission in areas of low prevalence, like The Gambia, but not in areas of high prevalence [14, 18].
This difference has been attributed to the multiple routes of transmission in hyperendemic areas such that elimination of any one source has minimal effect on transmission [14].
319.Rationale: Although environmental contamination on furniture and hands is documented, the passage explicitly states that flies are more important in low-prevalence settings and not in high-prevalence (hyperendemic) areas. The authors instead argue that multiple routes operate in hyperendemic settings. Therefore concluding that flies are the primary vector in hyperendemic areas is not supported by the passage and is incorrect.

319.Option: The presence of C. trachomatis DNA on both children's hands and household furniture, combined with uneven environmental contamination across villages despite similar infection rates, suggests multiple transmission pathways are operating in hyperendemic areas
Evidence: Samples of the household environment showed that C. trachomatis DNA was present on household objects, children, and adults (Fig 1). Locations with the highest proportion of positive swabs were household furniture (18%) and children’s hands (8%).
The 23 positive environmental swabs were obtained from 13 households (Table 3). Twenty-one of the positive swabs, collected from 11 households, were from the same village, Njoge.

This difference suggests that the practices of children and caretakers in Njoge may contribute to diverse environmental presence of Chlamydia, which in turn may be contributing to the high levels of trachoma.
This difference has been attributed to the multiple routes of transmission in hyperendemic areas such that elimination of any one source has minimal effect on transmission [14].
319.Rationale: The combination of (1) detection of chlamydial DNA on multiple types of environmental sites including hands and furniture, (2) concentration of positives in one village despite similar infection prevalences elsewhere, and (3) the authors' explicit interpretation that hyperendemic settings involve multiple routes of transmission supports the conclusion that transmission dynamics involve multiple, overlapping pathways rather than a single dominant route. The cited sentences directly support that inference, making this option correct.

319.Option: The detection of C. trachomatis DNA on children's hands and household furniture suggests that direct person-to-person transmission is the primary mechanism, with environmental surfaces playing a minor role
Evidence: Samples of the household environment showed that C. trachomatis DNA was present on household objects, children, and adults (Fig 1). Locations with the highest proportion of positive swabs were household furniture (18%) and children’s hands (8%).
This difference has been attributed to the multiple routes of transmission in hyperendemic areas such that elimination of any one source has minimal effect on transmission [14].

Our study provides suggestive evidence that in high prevalence villages, C. trachomatis may be contaminating environmental objects, including hands and clothing. These findings suggest that behavior change communication around hygiene may need to be enlarged to focus on keeping ocular and nasal secretions off faces and hands, and promoting washing of clothing as well.
319.Rationale: The detection of DNA on hands and furniture indicates environmental contamination and the authors interpret these findings as evidence that environmental objects may contribute to transmission. They explicitly argue for multiple routes in hyperendemic areas and recommend hygiene interventions addressing both hands and clothing. Thus the passage does not support the claim that direct person-to-person transmission is the primary mechanism with environmental surfaces playing a minor role; instead it supports environmental surfaces as potentially important contributors alongside other routes. Therefore this option is incorrect.

320.Option: Increase sampling frequency at all locations to compensate for delays in clinical reporting and ensure detection of asymptomatic cases regardless of flow dynamics
Evidence: If targeted upstream sampling at shared toilets or at the individual street level is not feasible, then an environmental surveillance sampling plan aiming to identify any infections within the catchment population would need to include a greater number of sampling locations, with an emphasis on upstream branches, and an increased sampling frequency and volume, to increase detection probability.

The highest probabilities of detection are concentrated in upstream branches that do not have ‘parent’ branches, and the lowest probabilities of detection are concentrated in downstream branches with large catchment populations, where larger volumes of wastewater flow and environmental flow converge (Fig 4).

some proportion of infected individuals are known to become asymptomatic, chronic carriers, capable of shedding large numbers of bacteria into the environment, but unlikely to seek medical care or testing
320.Rationale: The passage supports increasing sampling frequency and volume when needed, but it specifically recommends doing so in conjunction with an emphasis on upstream branches or by increasing the number of sampling locations—not uniformly "at all locations." Blanket increases at every site ignore the clear heterogeneity in detection probability (upstream sites often have higher detection, downstream convergence points often have lower detection because of dilution) and would be inefficient. The quoted text therefore supports targeted increases and upstream prioritization rather than an across-the-board increase at all locations.

320.Option: Conduct sampling at downstream convergence points during early morning hours to capture the cumulative shedding from the entire catchment area before dilution increases
Evidence: The highest probabilities of detection are concentrated in upstream branches that do not have ‘parent’ branches, and the lowest probabilities of detection are concentrated in downstream branches with large catchment populations, where larger volumes of wastewater flow and environmental flow converge (Fig 4).

a negative sampling result at downstream branches such as 573 and 815 does not indicate that there are no infected persons in the wastewater network, and infected persons are likely to be missed by environmental surveillance if these sites are relied upon. In this case, sampling further upstream is more likely to yield positive sampling results.

the mean probability of detection across all sections of all branches at all hours was 0.56, with the lowest mean probability occurring at 05:00 (0.48) and the highest mean probability occurring at 12:00 (0.60).
320.Rationale: The passage indicates downstream convergence points often have lower detection probability due to dilution, and explicitly warns against relying on such downstream sites to represent the whole catchment. The time-of-day data show the lowest average detection was at 05:00 (early morning) and the highest at midday, so sampling in the early morning at downstream points is not supported as a general strategy to maximize detection.

320.Option: Use a uniform sampling strategy across all branches and times to ensure equal representation of the population and avoid bias from location-specific detection probabilities
Evidence: This model takes into account environmental flow, which enters open wastewater systems from natural background inflow, infiltration, rainfall, and stormwater runoff and creates an important dilution factor, affecting the probability of detection at any given time and at any point in the wastewater system.

The output from this model may be used to aid in the design of an environmental sampling plan, inform decisions about sampling locations and times, and aid in the interpretation of results from an environmental surveillance program.

The varying influx of pathogens and wastewater flow into the wastewater network results in the wave patterns apparent in Branches 850, 912, and 937, which visualizes portions of wastewater with higher and lower pathogen concentrations moving downstream along each branch over the course of 24-hours.
320.Rationale: The passage emphasizes spatial and temporal heterogeneity in pathogen concentrations and detection probabilities (diurnal variation, upstream vs downstream differences, wave patterns along branches), and recommends using model output to choose locations and times. A uniform sampling strategy would ignore these well-documented differences and could be inefficient or biased; the text argues for targeted timing and location selection rather than equal sampling everywhere.

320.Option: Prioritize sampling at upstream branches during midday hours to maximize detection of asymptomatic shedding and reduce delays in identifying transmission
Evidence: The highest probabilities of detection are concentrated in upstream branches that do not have ‘parent’ branches, and the lowest probabilities of detection are concentrated in downstream branches with large catchment populations, where larger volumes of wastewater flow and environmental flow converge (Fig 4).
Branch 850: Section 0 at Hour 12; Branch 912: Section 0 at Hour 11
Branch 850, Section 0 and Branch 912, Section 0 have a peak probability of detection at 12:00 and 11:00, respectively, but the detection probability remains above 50% for the remainder of the day.

some proportion of infected individuals are known to become asymptomatic, chronic carriers, capable of shedding large numbers of bacteria into the environment, but unlikely to seek medical care or testing
320.Rationale: The passage shows upstream branches often have higher detection probabilities and gives concrete examples where peak detection occurred around midday (11:00–12:00). It also notes asymptomatic or chronic shedders can be missed by clinical surveillance, so prioritizing upstream sampling at times of day with higher modeled detection maximizes the chance of detecting ongoing transmission despite delays in clinical reporting or asymptomatic shedding. This approach both accounts for under-ascertainment and preserves validity by sampling where the model indicates sensitivity is highest.

320.Option: Schedule sampling at the end of the day to align with peak wastewater flow and maximize the chance of detecting asymptomatic shedding
Evidence: the mean probability of detection across all sections of all branches at all hours was 0.56, with the lowest mean probability occurring at 05:00 (0.48) and the highest mean probability occurring at 12:00 (0.60).

Branch 937, Section 0 appears to have peak detection probability in the middle of the night (between 23:00 and 02:00). However, detection probabilities greater than 0.5 are also indicated in the late afternoon and early evening (after 16:00) (Fig 5B).
As environmental flow (and flow velocity) increases, the temporal dynamics of detection probabilities over the 24-hour hour cycle change.
320.Rationale: The passage shows peak detection times vary by branch and scenario (many peaks at midday, some at night), and that flow dynamics alter the timing of peaks. Therefore, scheduling sampling uniformly at the 'end of the day' is not a universally optimal strategy. End-of-day sampling might work for some branches or under some flow conditions, but the model supports choosing sampling times based on branch-specific temporal patterns rather than a blanket end-of-day rule.

321.Option: Higher expression of CD10 on neutrophils post-treatment is associated with reduced risk of relapse in VL/HIV patients despite clinical cure
Evidence: CD10 expression on neutrophils was also significantly higher in VL/HIV patients who did not relapse over time (Fig 3B).

We therefore measured neutrophil counts in VL/HIV patients who relapsed during follow-up and compared them with those who did not; our results show a significantly higher number of neutrophils in the latter group (Fig 1B).
321.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that CD10 expression was significantly higher in VL/HIV patients who did not relapse, indicating CD10 measured post-treatment discriminates between relapsers and non-relapsers. This directly supports the option that higher post-treatment CD10 is associated with reduced risk of relapse. The passage also notes higher neutrophil counts in non-relapsers, but among the immunological markers listed in the options, CD10 is the one specifically reported as significantly different between relapse groups.

321.Option: Higher production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by neutrophils post-treatment is associated with reduced risk of relapse in VL/HIV patients despite clinical cure
Evidence: The ability of neutrophils to produce ROS was similar (p = 0.1777) in VL/HIV patients who relapse (206464 [119571]) and those who didn’t relapse during follow-up (227781 [155669–259241]).
321.Rationale: The passage reports no significant difference in ROS production between VL/HIV patients who relapsed and those who did not (p = 0.1777). Therefore higher ROS post-treatment does not distinguish relapsers from non-relapsers in this study and cannot be considered associated with reduced relapse risk.

321.Option: Higher phagocytic capacity of neutrophils post-treatment is associated with reduced risk of relapse in VL/HIV patients despite clinical cure
Evidence: In the latter group, the ability of neutrophils to phagocytose bioparticle was similarly impaired in patients who relapse and those who didn’t during follow-up (Fig 5B, Table 7).
321.Rationale: The passage states that phagocytic capacity remained impaired and was similarly impaired in relapsers and non-relapsers; thus phagocytosis measured post-treatment does not distinguish between these groups and is not associated with reduced relapse risk in this cohort.

321.Option: Higher expression of CD62L on neutrophils post-treatment is associated with reduced risk of relapse in VL/HIV patients despite clinical cure
